cases.title: Severe Epigastric Pain Radiating to Back

guidelineReferences.name: ACG Clinical Guideline: Management of Acute Pancreatitis 2024
guidelineReferences.citation: Tenner S, et al. Am J Gastroenterol. 2024;119:419-437
keyPoints: DIAGNOSIS: 2 of 3 criteria - (1) characteristic abdominal pain, (2) lipase/amylase >3x ULN, (3) imaging findings
Routine early CT NOT recommended - reserve for unclear diagnosis or failure to improve at 48-72 hours
FLUID: Moderately aggressive hydration with Lactated Ringer's preferred (Conditional); 1.5 mL/kg/hr baseline, bolus 10 mL/kg if hypovolemic
Monitor BUN/HCT at 6-8 hours - goal is to prevent rise (indicates adequate perfusion)
ETIOLOGY: Ultrasound for ALL patients (gallstones most common 40-70%); TG >1000 mg/dL = hypertriglyceridemic pancreatitis
NUTRITION: Early oral feeding within 24-48 hours as tolerated; start with low-fat solid diet (NOT stepwise clear liquids)
For severe AP: Enteral nutrition (NG preferred over NJ) prevents infectious complications; avoid TPN unless enteral impossible
ERCP: Within 24 hours ONLY for cholangitis; medical therapy preferred over early ERCP for biliary pancreatitis without cholangitis
ANTIBIOTICS: NOT recommended prophylactically for sterile necrosis (Conditional against); use targeted antibiotics for infected necrosis
SURGERY: Same-admission cholecystectomy for mild gallstone pancreatitis; delay intervention ≥4 weeks for stable patients with necrosis
Minimally invasive approaches (endoscopic, radiologic, laparoscopic) preferred over open necrosectomy
SEVERITY: SIRS at admission predicts organ failure; elevated BUN (>20), HCT (>44), obesity, comorbidities are risk factors

male-45-gallstone.name: Michael Rodriguez
male-45-gallstone.variantDescription: Acute gallstone pancreatitis with moderate severity

missedConsequences.Acute Pancreatitis: Untreated severe pancreatitis leads to necrosis, multiorgan failure, and death (mortality up to 30% in severe cases).
missedConsequences.Gallstone Pancreatitis: Missed gallstone etiology delays cholecystectomy, risks recurrence and cholangitis.
missedConsequences.Perforated Peptic Ulcer: Surgical emergency if missed. Similar presentation with acute abdomen.
missedConsequences.Mesenteric Ischemia: Catastrophic if missed. Pain out of proportion to exam.

commonPitfalls: Not obtaining lipase (more specific than amylase for pancreatitis)
Giving excessive early feeding - NPO initially with gradual advancement
Not identifying etiology (gallstones, alcohol, medications, hypertriglyceridemia)
Underestimating severity - use BISAP or Ranson criteria
Missing necrotizing pancreatitis - CT not needed early unless diagnostic uncertainty or severe
Delaying cholecystectomy in gallstone pancreatitis

mcqs.question: The patient has epigastric pain radiating to the back with nausea and vomiting. Lipase is 1,842 U/L (normal <60). What is the MOST important next step in identifying etiology?

options.text: CT abdomen to look for pancreatic necrosis
options.explanation: CT is not routinely needed early. Reserve for diagnostic uncertainty or severe/non-improving cases after 48-72 hours.
RUQ ultrasound is essential to evaluate for gallstones - the most common cause of acute pancreatitis. Identifies if cholecystectomy is needed.
MRCP is useful for choledocholithiasis but RUQ US is first-line and more practical in acute setting.
ERCP is reserved for cholangitis or confirmed CBD stone with obstruction, not as initial diagnostic test.

mcqs.question: Ultrasound shows gallstones and a dilated common bile duct (9mm). The patient's bilirubin is 2.8 and ALT is 285. What is the MOST appropriate management?

options.text: Supportive care only - pancreatitis will resolve spontaneously
options.explanation: Gallstone pancreatitis requires cholecystectomy to prevent recurrence. Evidence of CBD involvement may need ERCP.

options.text: Urgent ERCP if cholangitis, otherwise cholecystectomy during same admission
options.explanation: Dilated CBD and elevated LFTs suggest choledocholithiasis. If cholangitis (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain), urgent ERCP. Otherwise, cholecystectomy before discharge.

options.text: Discharge with outpatient cholecystectomy in 6 weeks
options.explanation: Delaying cholecystectomy increases recurrence risk. Same-admission cholecystectomy is recommended for mild gallstone pancreatitis.

options.text: Empiric antibiotics for presumed infected necrosis
options.explanation: No evidence of necrosis or infection. Antibiotics not indicated for acute pancreatitis without documented infection.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Pancreatitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Severe epigastric pain radiating to back after fatty meal, with nausea/vomiting. Classic presentation.

allDiagnoses.name: Biliary Colic/Acute Cholecystitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: RUQ/epigastric pain after fatty meal. Need to differentiate from pancreatitis.

allDiagnoses.name: Perforated Peptic Ulcer
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can present with acute epigastric pain. Would have peritonitis and free air on imaging.

allDiagnoses.name: Mesenteric Ischemia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Severe abdominal pain, especially in elderly with vascular disease. Pain out of proportion to exam.

allDiagnoses.name: Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm
allDiagnoses.rationale: Back pain component. Pulsatile mass. Can be catastrophic.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Myocardial Infarction
allDiagnoses.rationale: Inferior MI can present as epigastric pain. Always consider in appropriate age group.

allDiagnoses.name: Small Bowel Obstruction
allDiagnoses.rationale: Abdominal pain with vomiting. Different pattern but important to consider.

allDiagnoses.name: Peptic Ulcer Disease
allDiagnoses.rationale: Epigastric pain but usually not radiating to back, and not this severe acutely.

allDiagnoses.name: Gastroenteritis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Nausea/vomiting but pain not typically this severe or localized.

allDiagnoses.name: GERD
allDiagnoses.rationale: Burning epigastric pain but not this severe.

male-45-gallstone.oldcartsNarrative: "This pain started about 8 hours ago. It came on gradually but got really bad over a few hours. It's right in the middle of my upper stomach. It's constant and severe - I'd say 9 out of 10. Nothing makes it better. I've tried antacids, lying in different positions, nothing helps. I've been vomiting - probably 4-5 times. The vomit is just stomach contents, no blood. I've never had anything like this before."

historyQuestions.text: When did this start?
historyQuestions.answer: About 8 hours ago. It came on gradually but got really severe over a few hours. I've never had pain like this before.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Acute onset distinguishes from chronic pancreatitis. Timing helps narrow etiology.

historyQuestions.text: Describe the pain - where is it and where does it go?
historyQuestions.answer: It's right here in my upper middle stomach, and it bores straight through to my back. It's constant and severe, like something is tearing.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Epigastric pain radiating to back is classic for pancreatitis (retroperitoneal location)

historyQuestions.text: What were you doing when the pain started?
historyQuestions.answer: It started maybe an hour after I ate a big fatty meal - fried chicken and mac and cheese.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Postprandial onset, especially after fatty meal, suggests biliary etiology triggering pancreatitis

historyQuestions.text: How bad is the pain on a scale of 1 to 10?
historyQuestions.answer: It's a 9 out of 10. This is the worst pain I've ever had in my life.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Severe pain is characteristic of pancreatitis

historyQuestions.text: Does anything make the pain better or worse?
historyQuestions.answer: Nothing really helps. Maybe leaning forward a little bit takes the edge off, but lying on my back makes it worse.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Pain worse supine, better leaning forward - classic for pancreatitis (reduces stretch on retroperitoneum)

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the vomiting
historyQuestions.answer: I've thrown up 4 or 5 times. Just food at first, then mostly liquid and bile. No blood. The vomiting doesn't make the pain better at all.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Nausea and vomiting very common with pancreatitis. Unlike obstruction, vomiting doesn't relieve pain.

historyQuestions.text: How much alcohol do you drink?
historyQuestions.answer: Not much at all. Maybe one beer once a month or so. I'm not a drinker.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Alcohol is second most common cause (after gallstones). Minimal use here points to biliary etiology.

historyQuestions.text: Any history of gallstones or gallbladder problems?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes! They found gallstones on an ultrasound about 2 years ago when I had some mild stomach discomfort. The surgeon said I didn't need surgery since my symptoms went away.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Known gallstones strongly suggests gallstone pancreatitis - most common etiology

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever had pain like this before?
historyQuestions.answer: I've had some milder stomach pains after eating fatty foods, maybe once or twice, but nothing anywhere near this bad.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior biliary colic episodes support gallstone etiology

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Just lisinopril for blood pressure. That's it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Some medications can cause pancreatitis (valproate, azathioprine, etc.). Lisinopril not a common cause.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: Just high blood pressure and the gallstones they found. No diabetes or anything else.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medical history helps assess etiology and severity risk factors

historyQuestions.text: Any fevers or chills?
historyQuestions.answer: I've felt a little warm but haven't taken my temperature. No real shaking chills.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Low-grade fever common in pancreatitis. High fever with rigors suggests cholangitis or infected necrosis.

historyQuestions.text: Have you noticed any yellowing of your skin or eyes?
historyQuestions.answer: My wife said my eyes looked a little yellow this morning.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Jaundice suggests common bile duct stone - cholangitis may be developing

historyQuestions.text: Any allergies to medications?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important before pain management and potential antibiotics

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries. They talked about taking out my gallbladder 2 years ago but I never got around to it since the pain went away.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Missed opportunity for cholecystectomy; no prior abdominal surgery

historyQuestions.text: Any family history of gallbladder or pancreas problems?
historyQuestions.answer: My mother had her gallbladder out. No pancreas problems that I know of in the family.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Gallstones have familial tendency; hereditary pancreatitis rare but important

historyQuestions.text: Do you smoke?
historyQuestions.answer: I quit about 10 years ago. Smoked a pack a day for about 15 years before that.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Smoking is risk factor for pancreatitis and pancreatic cancer

historyQuestions.text: What do you do for work?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm a construction supervisor. Physical job but mostly directing crews now.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Occupation for social history

historyQuestions.text: When did you last eat or drink?
historyQuestions.answer: That fatty meal was about 8-9 hours ago. Haven't been able to eat anything since - even water makes me nauseous.
historyQuestions.reasoning: NPO status important for management; confirms postprandial onset

historyQuestions.text: How is this affecting what you can do?
historyQuestions.answer: I can't do anything. The pain is so bad I can barely move. I just want to curl up in a ball.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Severity assessment; patients prefer to stay still and lean forward

historyQuestions.text: What are you most worried about?
historyQuestions.answer: I just want this pain to stop. Is something seriously wrong? My wife is worried I need surgery.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Opens discussion about diagnosis and management plan including likely cholecystectomy

physicalExam.finding: BP 138/88, HR 108, RR 22, Temp 100.8°F, SpO2 96% on RA
physicalExam.reasoning: Tachycardia and low-grade fever common. Assess for hypovolemia (pancreatitis causes third-spacing).

physicalExam.finding: Uncomfortable male lying still, avoiding movement. Knees drawn up. Appears distressed.
physicalExam.reasoning: Patients with pancreatitis prefer to stay still. Leaning forward or fetal position may reduce pain.

physicalExam.finding: Abdomen slightly distended. No visible bruising around umbilicus or flanks.
physicalExam.reasoning: Cullen sign (periumbilical ecchymosis) and Grey-Turner sign (flank ecchymosis) = hemorrhagic/necrotizing pancreatitis. Absent here.

physicalExam.finding: Marked epigastric tenderness with voluntary guarding. No rebound tenderness. Some RUQ tenderness as well. Bowel sounds hypoactive.
physicalExam.reasoning: Epigastric tenderness classic. Guarding without rebound suggests retroperitoneal inflammation, not peritonitis.

physicalExam.finding: Equivocal - some discomfort with deep RUQ palpation but patient guards everywhere.
physicalExam.reasoning: Murphy's sign is inspiratory arrest with RUQ palpation. May coexist with pancreatitis.

physicalExam.finding: Tachycardic, regular. Normal S1/S2. No murmurs.
physicalExam.reasoning: Tachycardia from pain and hypovolemia

physicalExam.finding: Decreased breath sounds at left base. No crackles or wheezes elsewhere.
physicalExam.reasoning: Left pleural effusion common in pancreatitis. Elevated lipase in pleural fluid is diagnostic.

labs.name: Lipase
labs.reasoning: Lipase >3x ULN is diagnostic criterion for acute pancreatitis. More specific than amylase.

labs.name: Amylase
labs.reasoning: Elevated but less specific than lipase. Can be elevated in other conditions.

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Leukocytosis common. Hemoconcentration (high Hct) suggests third-spacing/dehydration.

labs.name: BMP
labs.result: Na 137, K 3.6, Cl 101, CO2 22, BUN 28, Cr 1.3, Glucose 186
labs.reasoning: Assess for AKI (hypovolemia), hyperglycemia (pancreatic dysfunction). BUN>25 is BISAP criterion.

labs.name: Liver Function Tests
labs.result: AST 142, ALT 285, Alk Phos 168, T.Bili 2.8, D.Bili 1.9
labs.reasoning: ALT >150 has 95% PPV for gallstone pancreatitis. Elevated bilirubin suggests CBD stone.

labs.name: Calcium
labs.reasoning: Hypocalcemia is poor prognostic sign (Ranson criterion). Fat saponification sequesters calcium.

labs.name: Triglycerides
labs.reasoning: Hypertriglyceridemia (>1000) can cause pancreatitis. Normal here supports biliary etiology.

labs.name: Lactate
labs.reasoning: Mild elevation from hypovolemia. Markedly elevated would suggest severe disease/necrosis.

imaging.name: Right Upper Quadrant Ultrasound
imaging.result: Multiple gallstones present. Common bile duct dilated to 9mm (normal <6mm). Gallbladder wall not thickened. No pericholecystic fluid.
imaging.reasoning: Confirms gallstone etiology. Dilated CBD suggests choledocholithiasis. Essential for determining need for cholecystectomy.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast
imaging.result: Not routinely indicated in first 48-72 hours. Reserve for diagnostic uncertainty, severe pancreatitis, or clinical deterioration.
imaging.reasoning: CT is best for detecting necrosis but early CT underestimates necrosis. Order if not improving or severe.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: Small left pleural effusion. No pneumoperitoneum. Heart size normal.
imaging.reasoning: Pleural effusion common (especially left). Rules out free air (perforation).

treatments.name: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation
treatments.result: Lactated Ringer's 250-500 mL/hour initially, titrate to urine output >0.5 mL/kg/hr
treatments.reasoning: Cornerstone of treatment. Third-spacing causes massive fluid losses. Goal-directed to urine output and vitals.

treatments.name: NPO status initially
treatments.result: NPO until pain improving, then advance diet as tolerated
treatments.reasoning: Initial pancreatic rest. Advance to low-fat solid diet once pain improving (usually 24-48 hours). No need for clear liquid progression.

treatments.name: Pain control
treatments.result: IV hydromorphone or morphine for pain. PCA if needed.
treatments.reasoning: Adequate analgesia is important. Opioids are appropriate. Old concerns about sphincter of Oddi spasm not clinically significant.

treatments.name: Antiemetics
treatments.result: Ondansetron 4mg IV q6h PRN nausea
treatments.reasoning: Control nausea to improve comfort and allow oral intake when ready

treatments.name: Avoid prophylactic antibiotics
treatments.result: Antibiotics NOT indicated for acute pancreatitis without documented infection
treatments.reasoning: Prophylactic antibiotics do not prevent infected necrosis and increase resistant organisms

treatments.name: ERCP if indicated
treatments.result: Consider urgent ERCP if cholangitis present. Otherwise, cholecystectomy can clear CBD.
treatments.reasoning: Urgent ERCP for cholangitis (Charcot's triad). Otherwise CBD stones often pass or can be addressed surgically.

consults.name: Surgery consult
consults.result: Consult surgery for cholecystectomy during same admission
consults.reasoning: Same-admission cholecystectomy for mild gallstone pancreatitis reduces recurrence. Don't delay.

consults.name: GI consult
consults.result: GI consult if ERCP needed for cholangitis or persistent CBD stone
consults.reasoning: ERCP if cholangitis or confirmed obstructing CBD stone not clearing

disposition.name: Admit to medicine/surgery
disposition.result: Admit for IV fluids, pain control, and monitoring. Surgery consult for cholecystectomy.
disposition.reasoning: All acute pancreatitis requires admission for fluid resuscitation and monitoring.

disposition.name: ICU admission
disposition.result: Consider ICU for severe pancreatitis (BISAP ≥3, organ failure, or high risk)
disposition.reasoning: This case is moderate severity. ICU if deteriorating or develops organ failure.

actualDiagnosis.name: Acute Gallstone Pancreatitis - Moderate Severity
keyFindings: Epigastric pain radiating to back, worse supine, better leaning forward
Onset after fatty meal in patient with known gallstones
Nausea and vomiting not relieving pain
ALT >150 (285) - highly predictive of gallstone etiology
RUQ US confirms gallstones with dilated CBD (9mm)
Leukocytosis and hemoconcentration indicating inflammation and third-spacing
teachingPoints: Diagnosis requires 2 of 3: typical pain, lipase >3x ULN, imaging findings
Two most common causes: Gallstones (40%) and Alcohol (40%). Remember 'I GET SMASHED' mnemonic for others.
ALT >150 U/L has 95% PPV for gallstone pancreatitis
BISAP score (0-5): BUN>25, Impaired mental status, SIRS, Age>60, Pleural effusion. ≥3 = severe.
Aggressive early IV fluid resuscitation (LR preferred) is cornerstone of treatment - goal UOP >0.5 mL/kg/hr
CT not needed early - reserve for diagnostic uncertainty or severe/non-improving cases at 48-72 hours
Prophylactic antibiotics are NOT indicated (even for severe pancreatitis)
Advance diet when pain improving - can go directly to low-fat solid diet
Same-admission cholecystectomy for mild gallstone pancreatitis reduces recurrence
Urgent ERCP only for cholangitis (fever + jaundice + RUQ pain) or clear CBD obstruction

guidelineReferences.name: ACG Guidelines on Acute Pancreatitis 2024
guidelineReferences.citation: Tenner S et al. Am J Gastroenterol. 2024;119(3):419-437
keyPoints: Diagnosis: 2 of 3 criteria (typical pain, lipase >3x ULN, imaging)
Goal-directed moderate IV fluid resuscitation (avoid aggressive overhydration)
Early oral feeding when pain improving, nausea resolved
Prophylactic antibiotics NOT recommended, even for severe pancreatitis
Same-admission cholecystectomy for mild gallstone pancreatitis

guidelineReferences.name: Revised Atlanta Classification for Acute Pancreatitis 2012
guidelineReferences.citation: Banks PA et al. Gut. 2013;62(1):102-111
keyPoints: Severity: Mild (no organ failure), Moderately severe (transient <48h), Severe (persistent organ failure)
CT at 48-72 hours if no improvement or diagnostic uncertainty
Contrast-enhanced CT for detecting necrosis and collections
Peripancreatic fluid collections vs pseudocyst vs walled-off necrosis
Modified Marshall score for organ failure assessment

bisap.name: BISAP Score
bisap.description: Predicts mortality in acute pancreatitis within 24 hours of presentation

thisPatient.nextStep: Admit to floor, aggressive fluid resuscitation, close monitoring
thisPatient.clinicalAction: BISAP 0-2 = low mortality. ≥3 = consider ICU admission.

ranson.name: Ranson Criteria
ranson.description: Classic prognostic score - 5 criteria at admission, 6 at 48 hours
ranson.note: BISAP is simpler and available at admission. Ranson requires 48-hour reassessment.
ranson.source: Ranson JH et al. Surg Gynecol Obstet. 1974;139(1):69-81

patientBackground.recentEvents: Patient was in usual state of health until symptoms began. No major recent life changes.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: No significant recent illnesses other than this presentation.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Dealing with typical life stressors. This illness is disrupting normal activities.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Has family and friends available for support. Not socially isolated.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Generally trusts medical advice. Wants to understand the reasoning behind recommendations.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Uses healthcare appropriately. No excessive ER utilization.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient was a heavy drinker?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same presentation but patient drinks 6+ beers daily and had binge last night

chiefComplaint.original: Severe stomach pain that goes through to my back
chiefComplaint.modified: Severe stomach pain that goes through to my back. I had a lot to drink last night.

feedback.correct: Good! Alcoholic pancreatitis is the other major cause. Key differences: normal ALT, no gallstones, and must assess for withdrawal risk.
feedback.partial: Consider how etiology changes management. What additional concern exists with alcoholic pancreatitis?
feedback.incorrect: Heavy alcohol use makes alcoholic pancreatitis the likely etiology. ALT is usually normal (unlike gallstone). Must also monitor for alcohol withdrawal during hospitalization.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Gallstone = ALT>150, gallstones on US, same-admission cholecystectomy. Alcohol = normal ALT, no stones, must monitor for withdrawal (CIWA protocol), no surgery needed, counseling for abstinence.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient developed fever on day 5?
whatIfScenarios.description: Patient initially improved but develops fever 103°F and increased pain on hospital day 5

chiefComplaint.original: Severe stomach pain that goes through to my back
chiefComplaint.modified: I was getting better but now I have high fever and the pain is worse again

feedback.correct: Excellent! Late deterioration with fever suggests infected necrosis - a serious complication. Need CT and possible IR drainage/surgery.
feedback.partial: Good concern for complication. What specific complication develops at this timeframe?
feedback.incorrect: Fever developing at day 5+ suggests INFECTED NECROSIS - mortality jumps significantly. Requires CT to assess necrosis, may need FNA to confirm infection, and likely drainage.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Early fever (days 1-3) = SIRS from inflammation. Late fever (days 5+) = infected necrosis until proven otherwise. Prophylactic antibiotics don't prevent this - but once infected, need antibiotics and drainage.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient had Charcot's triad?
whatIfScenarios.description: Fever, jaundice, and RUQ pain along with the pancreatitis

chiefComplaint.original: Severe stomach pain that goes through to my back
chiefComplaint.modified: Severe pain, I'm turning yellow, and I have chills and fever

feedback.correct: Charcot's triad = cholangitis = URGENT ERCP needed. This is a biliary emergency requiring emergent decompression.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of Charcot's triad. What's the urgent intervention?
feedback.incorrect: Fever + jaundice + RUQ pain = CHOLANGITIS. This is a biliary emergency requiring URGENT ERCP for biliary decompression within 24-48 hours. Antibiotics alone are not enough.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Simple gallstone pancreatitis = elective ERCP if CBD stone, same-admission cholecystectomy. Cholangitis = URGENT ERCP within 24-48h for decompression - this is life-threatening.

guidelineReferences.name: ACG Upper GI Bleeding Guidelines 2021
guidelineReferences.citation: Laine L, et al. Am J Gastroenterol. 2021;116:899-917
keyPoints: Risk stratify with Glasgow-Blatchford Score (GBS)
GBS 0-1 may be candidates for outpatient management
IV PPI bolus then infusion for high-risk stigmata
EGD within 24 hours for most patients; 12 hours if hemodynamically unstable
Transfusion threshold: Hgb <7 g/dL (8 g/dL if active CV disease)
Restart anticoagulation within 7 days for most patients

male-58-pud.name: Thomas Smith
male-58-pud.variantDescription: Upper GI bleed from peptic ulcer disease

missedConsequences.Upper GI Bleeding: Uncontrolled hemorrhage leads to hypovolemic shock and death. Mortality 5-10% for hospitalized UGIB.
missedConsequences.Variceal Bleeding: Different management than non-variceal. Missed varices lead to massive hemorrhage if not properly treated.
missedConsequences.Peptic Ulcer Disease: Ongoing NSAID use or untreated H. pylori leads to recurrent bleeding.

commonPitfalls: Underestimating blood loss - orthostatic vitals and hematocrit can lag behind actual loss
Delaying resuscitation for diagnostic workup
Not calculating Glasgow-Blatchford score to risk-stratify
Missing signs of variceal bleeding (stigmata of cirrhosis) which requires different treatment
Overtransfusing - restrictive strategy (Hgb <7) is preferred in most UGIB
Not starting PPI before endoscopy
Forgetting to ask about anticoagulant/antiplatelet use

mcqs.question: The patient has hematemesis and melena with HR 112 and BP 98/62. What is the MOST important initial step?

options.text: Urgent upper endoscopy to identify bleeding source
options.explanation: Endoscopy is important but not before stabilization. Resuscitation comes first.

options.text: IV access with large-bore IVs, fluid resuscitation, type and screen
options.explanation: ABCs first! Establish IV access (two large-bore peripheral IVs), start crystalloid resuscitation, type and screen/crossmatch for potential transfusion.
CT angiography is for lower GI bleeds or when endoscopy fails. EGD is diagnostic AND therapeutic for UGIB.

options.text: Check coagulation studies before any intervention
options.explanation: While helpful, don't delay resuscitation for lab results. Can draw labs while establishing access.

mcqs.question: The patient's hemoglobin is 7.8 g/dL (baseline unknown). He has no cardiac history. What is the transfusion target?

options.explanation: Liberal transfusion strategy (Hgb >10) is associated with worse outcomes in UGIB.
Restrictive transfusion (target Hgb 7-8) is associated with better outcomes in UGIB. Exception: active coronary ischemia may need higher target.

options.text: No transfusion needed if patient is asymptomatic
options.explanation: With hemodynamic instability (tachycardia, hypotension), transfusion is indicated even if patient reports no symptoms.

options.text: Transfuse only if hemoglobin drops below 5 g/dL
options.explanation: Hgb <5 would be extreme and dangerous. Restrictive doesn't mean that restrictive.

allDiagnoses.name: Upper GI Bleeding (Peptic Ulcer Disease)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Hematemesis, melena, NSAID use, and epigastric pain history. PUD is most common cause of non-variceal UGIB.

allDiagnoses.name: Gastritis/Erosive Disease
allDiagnoses.rationale: NSAID use can cause gastric erosions. Less likely to cause massive bleeding than ulcer.

allDiagnoses.name: Variceal Bleeding
allDiagnoses.rationale: Must assess for liver disease stigmata. Different management with octreotide, antibiotics, and lower BP targets.

allDiagnoses.name: Mallory-Weiss Tear
allDiagnoses.rationale: Retching before hematemesis suggests MW tear. Usually self-limited but can be severe.

allDiagnoses.name: Esophageal/Gastric Malignancy
allDiagnoses.rationale: Must be excluded on endoscopy, especially with weight loss or dysphagia.

allDiagnoses.name: Aortoenteric Fistula
allDiagnoses.rationale: Rare but catastrophic. Consider in patients with prior aortic surgery. 'Herald bleed' precedes massive hemorrhage.

allDiagnoses.name: Lower GI Bleeding
allDiagnoses.rationale: Hematemesis and melena indicate upper source. Lower GIB typically causes hematochezia.

allDiagnoses.name: Epistaxis (swallowed)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause hematemesis but should see nasal bleeding. No history here.

male-58-pud.oldcartsNarrative: "This morning I woke up feeling nauseous and then vomited what looked like coffee grounds and some bright red blood - maybe half a cup total. It scared me to death. I'm feeling weak and lightheaded, especially when I stand up. The vomiting came on suddenly - I wasn't sick before this. I've had some burning in my stomach on and off for a few weeks that I thought was heartburn. The blood was dark brown like coffee grounds mixed with some brighter red. It happened twice this morning, about an hour apart."

historyQuestions.text: When did this start?
historyQuestions.answer: This morning - I woke up feeling nauseous and then vomited the blood. It happened twice, about an hour apart. Before that, I've had some stomach burning for a few weeks but nothing like this.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Acute onset this morning with prodromal symptoms helps establish timeline and acuity

historyQuestions.text: Describe what you vomited
historyQuestions.answer: It looked like coffee grounds mixed with some bright red blood. Maybe half a cup total. Happened twice this morning.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Coffee-ground emesis = partially digested blood (upper GI). Fresh blood indicates active or very recent bleeding.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your bowel movements
historyQuestions.answer: They've been black and tarry, almost like tar, for 2-3 days. Really sticky and foul-smelling.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Melena = digested blood, black/tarry stool. Indicates upper GI source (or very proximal lower GI).

historyQuestions.text: Have you felt dizzy or lightheaded?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, especially when I stand up. I almost passed out in the bathroom this morning.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Orthostatic symptoms indicate significant volume loss (typically >15-20% blood volume)

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take, especially pain medications?
historyQuestions.answer: I've been taking a lot of ibuprofen for my back - probably 4-6 tablets a day for the past few weeks. I also take aspirin and lisinopril.
historyQuestions.reasoning: NSAIDs + aspirin = major risk factors for peptic ulcer and GI bleeding. Dual antiplatelet is very high risk.

historyQuestions.text: Any stomach pain before this?
historyQuestions.answer: Yeah, burning pain right here in my upper stomach on and off for a few weeks. I thought it was just heartburn.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prodromal epigastric pain suggests pre-existing ulcer that has now bled

historyQuestions.text: Was the stomach pain related to eating? Better or worse with meals?
historyQuestions.answer: It would get worse after eating, especially with spicy food or coffee. Sometimes eating would help briefly then it would come back worse.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Meal timing helps characterize ulcer type - gastric ulcers worsen with food, duodenal may transiently improve then worsen 2-3 hours later.

historyQuestions.text: How much alcohol do you drink?
historyQuestions.answer: Not much. Maybe 2-3 beers on the weekend. I'm not a heavy drinker.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Assessing for liver disease/varices. Minimal alcohol use makes varices less likely.

historyQuestions.text: Any history of liver problems, hepatitis, or cirrhosis?
historyQuestions.answer: No, never had any liver problems. No hepatitis.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No known liver disease makes variceal bleeding less likely but should still examine for stigmata

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever had bleeding from your stomach before?
historyQuestions.answer: No, never. This is the first time anything like this has happened.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior GI bleed would increase risk and suggest recurrent source

historyQuestions.text: Are you on any blood thinners?
historyQuestions.answer: Just the aspirin. No other blood thinners.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Anticoagulants affect bleeding risk and may need reversal

historyQuestions.text: Were you vomiting or retching before you saw blood?
historyQuestions.answer: I felt nauseous but I wasn't really vomiting repeatedly before this. It kind of came out of nowhere.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Repeated retching before hematemesis suggests Mallory-Weiss tear. Absent here makes PUD more likely.

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important before PPI therapy, potential blood products, and endoscopy medications

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure and high cholesterol. Some back problems that's why I've been taking the ibuprofen. No heart disease.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Assessing overall health for risk stratification; cardiac history affects transfusion threshold

historyQuestions.text: Any prior surgeries, especially abdominal?
historyQuestions.answer: I had my appendix out as a kid. No other surgeries. Never had anything with my aorta.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior aortic surgery is critical - aortoenteric fistula is rare but catastrophic cause of GI bleed

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever been tested or treated for H. pylori?
historyQuestions.answer: I don't think so. Never heard of that.
historyQuestions.reasoning: H. pylori is major cause of PUD; will need testing and treatment if positive

historyQuestions.text: Any unexplained weight loss?
historyQuestions.answer: No, my weight has been stable. Maybe lost a few pounds this week from not eating well because of the stomach pain.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Significant weight loss would raise concern for malignancy

historyQuestions.text: Any trouble swallowing?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I can swallow fine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Dysphagia would increase suspicion for esophageal pathology

historyQuestions.text: Do you smoke?
historyQuestions.answer: I quit about 10 years ago. Smoked for 20 years before that.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Smoking history is risk factor for PUD and malignancy

historyQuestions.text: When did you last eat?
historyQuestions.answer: I had some toast yesterday evening but haven't been able to eat since. Haven't felt like it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: NPO status for potential endoscopy

physicalExam.finding: BP 98/62, HR 112, RR 18, Temp 98.4°F, SpO2 97%. Orthostatic: BP drops to 82/54 with HR 128 when standing.
physicalExam.reasoning: Tachycardia and hypotension indicate significant blood loss. Positive orthostatics confirm hypovolemia.

physicalExam.finding: Pale, diaphoretic, anxious-appearing male. Alert but appears unwell.
physicalExam.reasoning: Pallor and diaphoresis indicate significant hemorrhage and sympathetic response

physicalExam.finding: Pale skin and mucous membranes. No jaundice. No spider angiomata. No palmar erythema. Capillary refill 3 seconds.
physicalExam.reasoning: Pallor from anemia. Absence of stigmata of liver disease (spider angiomata, palmar erythema, jaundice) argues against varices.

physicalExam.finding: Soft, mild epigastric tenderness. No rebound or guarding. No hepatosplenomegaly. No ascites. Normal bowel sounds.
physicalExam.reasoning: Epigastric tenderness suggests PUD. No liver stigmata (hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, ascites) argues against portal hypertension.

physicalExam.finding: Black, tarry stool on exam. Guaiac strongly positive. No masses. Normal tone.
physicalExam.reasoning: Confirms melena. Rectal exam is essential in all GI bleeding to assess stool character.

physicalExam.finding: Tachycardic, regular rhythm. Normal S1/S2. No murmurs. JVP flat.
physicalExam.reasoning: Tachycardia compensatory for blood loss. Flat JVP indicates volume depletion.

physicalExam.finding: Alert and oriented. No asterixis. No focal deficits.
physicalExam.reasoning: Absence of asterixis and encephalopathy argues against liver failure

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Hemoglobin indicates severity. Note: Hgb may not reflect acute loss immediately (takes 24-72h to equilibrate).

labs.name: Type and Screen/Crossmatch
labs.reasoning: Essential for potential transfusion. Crossmatch 2-4 units in significant UGIB.

labs.name: BMP
labs.result: Na 140, K 3.8, Cl 102, CO2 24, BUN 42, Cr 1.1, Glucose 118
labs.reasoning: Elevated BUN with normal Cr = 'upper GI bleed pattern' (digested blood protein absorbed). BUN:Cr >20 suggests UGIB.

labs.name: PT/INR, PTT
labs.reasoning: Assess coagulopathy. Important if on anticoagulants or liver disease suspected.

labs.name: Liver Function Tests
labs.result: AST 32, ALT 28, Alk Phos 78, T.Bili 0.8, Albumin 3.8
labs.reasoning: Normal LFTs and albumin argue against chronic liver disease/varices

labs.name: Lactate
labs.reasoning: Mild elevation from hypoperfusion. Markedly elevated would indicate shock.

imaging.name: Imaging
imaging.result: No imaging routinely needed for UGIB. EGD is diagnostic AND therapeutic.
imaging.reasoning: Unlike lower GI bleed, UGIB doesn't need CT. Endoscopy is the test of choice.

treatments.name: Large-bore IV access
treatments.result: Two 18-gauge or larger peripheral IVs placed
treatments.reasoning: Large-bore access for rapid volume/blood administration. Central line if peripheral access difficult.

treatments.name: IV fluid resuscitation
treatments.result: Lactated Ringer's bolus 1-2L, titrate to vital signs
treatments.reasoning: Restore intravascular volume. Crystalloid first, then blood products as needed.

treatments.name: Blood transfusion
treatments.result: Transfuse pRBCs with goal Hgb >7 g/dL (restrictive strategy)
treatments.reasoning: Restrictive transfusion (Hgb 7-8) improves outcomes vs liberal (Hgb >9). Exception: active ACS.

treatments.name: IV PPI
treatments.result: Pantoprazole 80mg IV bolus, then 8mg/hour infusion (or 40mg IV BID)
treatments.reasoning: High-dose IV PPI before endoscopy reduces rebleeding and need for endoscopic therapy. Continue after high-risk lesion found.

treatments.name: Hold anticoagulants/antiplatelets
treatments.result: Hold aspirin and NSAIDs. Restart aspirin after hemostasis if cardiac indication.
treatments.reasoning: Stop NSAIDs permanently. Aspirin for cardiac protection can restart after endoscopic hemostasis (usually within 3-5 days).

treatments.name: NPO for endoscopy
treatments.result: NPO in preparation for upper endoscopy
treatments.reasoning: Patient must be NPO for safe endoscopy. Can consider erythromycin to promote gastric emptying.

treatments.name: Upper endoscopy (EGD)
treatments.result: EGD within 24 hours of presentation (within 12 hours if high-risk features)
treatments.reasoning: Diagnostic AND therapeutic. Can identify source and provide hemostasis (clips, epinephrine, cautery).

consults.name: GI consult
consults.result: Urgent GI consult for endoscopy
consults.reasoning: GI performs EGD for diagnosis and therapeutic intervention

consults.name: Surgery consult
consults.result: Consider if massive bleeding, hemodynamic instability despite resuscitation, or failed endoscopic therapy
consults.reasoning: Surgery for refractory bleeding or perforation. Not needed initially in most cases.

disposition.name: ICU admission
disposition.result: Admit to ICU given hemodynamic instability
disposition.reasoning: Hypotension and tachycardia warrant ICU monitoring. High Glasgow-Blatchford score.

disposition.name: Floor admission
disposition.result: Stable, low-risk patients may go to monitored floor bed
disposition.reasoning: This patient is not low-risk. Floor for Glasgow-Blatchford 0-1 only.

actualDiagnosis.name: Upper GI Bleeding - Peptic Ulcer Disease (NSAID-induced)
keyFindings: Elevated BUN:Cr ratio (42:1.1 = 38) - classic for UGIB
No stigmata of liver disease (varices unlikely)
teachingPoints: Causes of UGIB: Peptic ulcer (most common ~50%), gastritis/erosions, varices, Mallory-Weiss, malignancy, Dieulafoy, AVM
Glasgow-Blatchford Score (0-23): Predicts need for intervention. Score 0-1 may be safe for outpatient management.
BUN:Cr ratio >20:1 suggests upper GI source (blood protein digested and absorbed)
Resuscitate BEFORE endoscopy - ABCs first, establish IV access, crystalloid, then blood if needed
Restrictive transfusion (Hgb >7) superior to liberal (Hgb >9) in non-cardiac UGIB
IV PPI should be started BEFORE endoscopy - reduces rebleeding and interventions needed
EGD within 24 hours for most UGIB; within 12 hours if high-risk (hemodynamic instability, bloody NG aspirate)
High-risk stigmata on EGD: active spurting, visible vessel, adherent clot
For PUD: test and treat H. pylori, stop NSAIDs, PPI for 8 weeks minimum
Aspirin for secondary cardiac prevention can restart within 3-5 days after hemostasis (benefits outweigh bleeding risk)

guidelineReferences.name: ACG Clinical Guideline: Upper Gastrointestinal and Ulcer Bleeding 2021
guidelineReferences.citation: Laine L et al. Am J Gastroenterol. 2021;116(5):899-917
keyPoints: IV PPI (e.g., pantoprazole 80mg bolus then 8mg/hr) before endoscopy
Restrictive transfusion strategy (Hgb threshold 7 g/dL) recommended
EGD within 24 hours; consider within 12 hours if high-risk features
Glasgow-Blatchford Score ≤1 may allow outpatient management
Test all patients for H. pylori and eradicate if positive

guidelineReferences.name: International Consensus on Non-Variceal Upper GI Bleeding 2019
guidelineReferences.citation: Barkun AN et al. Ann Intern Med. 2019;171(11):805-822
keyPoints: Risk stratification with validated tools (Glasgow-Blatchford, Rockall)
Prokinetic agents (erythromycin 250mg IV) may improve visualization
Endoscopic therapy for high-risk stigmata: active bleeding, visible vessel, adherent clot
Dual endoscopic therapy (e.g., injection + clips) recommended for high-risk lesions
Oral PPI BID x 14 days, then daily for maintenance after endoscopic therapy

glasgowBlatchford.name: Glasgow-Blatchford Score (GBS)
glasgowBlatchford.description: Predicts need for intervention in upper GI bleeding - determines who needs admission vs outpatient management

thisPatient.interpretation: HIGH RISK - requires admission and intervention
thisPatient.clinicalAction: GBS ≥1 typically requires admission. This patient has significant anemia, tachycardia, hypotension, and melena.

low.action: May be safe for outpatient management with close follow-up

high.action: Admission recommended; higher scores = higher need for intervention

aims65.name: AIMS65 Score
aims65.description: Predicts mortality in upper GI bleeding

thisPatient.clinicalAction: Lower mortality risk but still requires admission given active bleeding and hemodynamic changes.

aims65.source: Saltzman JR et al. Gastrointest Endosc. 2011;74(6):1215-1224

rockall.name: Rockall Score (Post-endoscopy)
rockall.description: Predicts rebleeding and mortality after endoscopy
rockall.note: Full Rockall requires endoscopy findings. Pre-endoscopy Rockall uses age, shock, and comorbidities only.

patientBackground.recentEvents: Patient was in usual state of health until symptoms began. No major recent life changes.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: No significant recent illnesses other than this presentation.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Dealing with typical life stressors. This illness is disrupting normal activities.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Has family and friends available for support. Not socially isolated.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Generally trusts medical advice. Wants to understand the reasoning behind recommendations.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Uses healthcare appropriately. No excessive ER utilization.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient had cirrhosis?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same presentation but in a patient with known alcoholic cirrhosis

chiefComplaint.original: I've been throwing up blood since this morning
chiefComplaint.modified: I've been throwing up large amounts of blood. I have cirrhosis from drinking.

historyAnswers.chief_complaint: I vomited a large amount of bright red blood. I have liver disease.
historyAnswers.what_happened: I was just sitting watching TV and suddenly felt nauseous, then vomited a lot of blood - bright red, like cups of it.
historyAnswers.blood_vomit: Yes, it was bright red blood - not coffee ground, actual red blood. I filled a whole bowl.
historyAnswers.hematemesis: I've vomited blood three times now. Each time was a lot - maybe a cup or more.
historyAnswers.melena: Yes, my stools have been black and tarry for the past day. They smell really bad.
historyAnswers.liver_disease: Yes, I have cirrhosis. I was diagnosed about 3 years ago.
historyAnswers.cirrhosis: Yes, from hepatitis C. I also used to drink heavily.
historyAnswers.alcohol: I used to drink a lot - a six-pack or more daily for about 20 years. I stopped 3 years ago when I got sick.
historyAnswers.hepatitis: Yes, hepatitis C. I found out about it when I got the cirrhosis diagnosis.
historyAnswers.varices: My doctor told me I have varices in my esophagus. I had them banded once about a year ago.
historyAnswers.prior_bleeding: Yes, I had bleeding from varices about a year ago. They did a procedure to band them.
historyAnswers.ascites: Yes, I have fluid in my belly. I have to get it drained every few weeks.
historyAnswers.encephalopathy: Sometimes I get confused. My family says I get forgetful when I'm sicker.
historyAnswers.medications: I take propranolol for my varices, spironolactone and furosemide for the fluid, and lactulose.
historyAnswers.nsaids: No, my doctor told me never to take ibuprofen or aspirin because of my liver.
historyAnswers.dizzy: Yes, I feel dizzy when I stand up. Everything goes gray.
historyAnswers.lightheaded: Very lightheaded. I almost passed out in the bathroom.
historyAnswers.weakness: I feel very weak, like I could pass out.
historyAnswers.sweating: Yes, I'm sweating and feel clammy.

examFindings.general: Ill-appearing male, pale, diaphoretic, in moderate distress
examFindings.skin: Jaundiced. Spider angiomata on chest and back. Palmar erythema bilateral. Caput medusae present.
examFindings.abdomen: Distended with ascites. Positive fluid wave. Splenomegaly palpable. No peritoneal signs.
examFindings.neuro: Oriented but slow to respond. No asterixis currently. GCS 14.

feedback.correct: Variceal bleeding is a distinct entity. Add octreotide, prophylactic antibiotics (ceftriaxone), and consider emergent TIPS if refractory. Higher mortality than non-variceal UGIB.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of different etiology. What medications are specific to variceal bleeding?
feedback.incorrect: Variceal UGIB = different management: OCTREOTIDE (reduces portal pressure), prophylactic ANTIBIOTICS (ceftriaxone - reduces mortality), emergent EGD with banding, consider TIPS if refractory. Much higher mortality.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: CRITICAL DIFFERENCES from PUD bleed: Variceal needs octreotide/terlipressin, prophylactic antibiotics (ceftriaxone), avoid aggressive FFP. EGD within 12 hours. Band ligation is treatment. NO PPIs until source confirmed (PPIs don't help varices). Risk stratification: Child-Pugh class determines mortality.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient was on warfarin?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same bleeding but patient on warfarin for AF with INR 4.2

chiefComplaint.original: I've been throwing up blood since this morning
chiefComplaint.modified: I've been throwing up blood. I take warfarin for my afib.

feedback.correct: Supratherapeutic INR with major bleeding = reverse with Vitamin K + PCC (or FFP). Hold warfarin. Cardiology input on when to restart anticoagulation.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of coagulopathy. What reversal agents are used?
feedback.incorrect: Major bleeding on warfarin = REVERSE: Vitamin K (10mg IV) + PCC (4-factor preferred) or FFP. PCC works faster than FFP. Decision to restart anticoagulation involves balancing stroke vs rebleeding risk.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Non-anticoagulated UGIB = standard management. Anticoagulated UGIB = may need reversal (Vitamin K, PCC/FFP for warfarin; idarucizumab for dabigatran; andexanet for Xa inhibitors). Complex decision on restarting.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there was ongoing massive hemorrhage?
whatIfScenarios.description: Continuous hematemesis, BP 70/40, HR 140, not responding to transfusion

chiefComplaint.original: I've been throwing up blood since this morning
chiefComplaint.modified: [Patient vomiting large amounts of fresh blood, altered mental status, BP 70/40]

historyAnswers.what_happened: I vomited a huge amount of blood - it just kept coming. I passed out briefly.
historyAnswers.syncope: Yes, I passed out on my way to the bathroom. My wife found me on the floor.
historyAnswers.amount: It was like a fountain. My wife said there was blood all over the bathroom floor.
historyAnswers.ongoing: It's still happening. I vomited more blood in the ambulance.
historyAnswers.stool: I had bloody diarrhea - bright red blood mixed with stool.
historyAnswers.hematochezia: Yes, bright red blood from my rectum. Not just when I wipe - it's running out.
historyAnswers.dizzy: Very dizzy. The room is spinning.
historyAnswers.chest_pain: I feel pressure in my chest.
historyAnswers.breathing: Short of breath, like I can't catch my breath.
historyAnswers.confusion: I feel confused. I'm having trouble thinking clearly.
historyAnswers.aaa_history: I had a surgery on my aorta a few years ago for an aneurysm.

examFindings.general: Critically ill, altered mental status, actively vomiting blood
examFindings.skin: Cold, clammy, mottled extremities. Delayed capillary refill >4 seconds.

keyDiscriminators: Hematochezia from upper source (indicates rapid transit)

feedback.correct: Massive UGIB not responding to resuscitation = emergent EGD (even if not fully resuscitated), activate massive transfusion protocol, consider IR angiography or surgery if endoscopy fails.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of emergency. What's the role of IR and surgery?
feedback.incorrect: Refractory massive UGIB = emergent EGD (don't wait for perfect resuscitation), massive transfusion protocol (1:1:1 RBC:FFP:platelets), if endoscopy fails → IR angioembolization or surgery. This is life-threatening.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: MASSIVE HEMORRHAGE PROTOCOL: Activate MTP (1:1:1 ratio). Permissive hypotension (target SBP 80-90) until bleeding controlled. Early intubation for airway protection. EMERGENT EGD. Consider IR angioembolization or surgery if fails. TXA within 3h may reduce mortality. If hx of AAA repair: MUST rule out aortoenteric fistula.

female-68-sbo.name: Patrick Davis
female-68-sbo.chiefComplaint: My stomach hurts and I keep vomiting

missedConsequences.Small Bowel Obstruction: Complete obstruction leads to bowel necrosis, perforation, sepsis, and death without intervention.
missedConsequences.Strangulated Obstruction: Compromised blood supply = surgical emergency. Mortality increases dramatically with delay.
missedConsequences.Large Bowel Obstruction: Different management - may require emergent decompression for cecal dilatation.

commonPitfalls: Missing signs of strangulation (fever, tachycardia, focal tenderness, peritonitis)
Delaying surgical consultation
Giving oral contrast when obstruction is complete
Not placing NG tube for decompression
Missing incarcerated hernia on exam

mcqs.question: CT shows dilated small bowel with transition point and no contrast passing distally. What is the MOST important immediate step?

options.explanation: Surgery may be needed, but initial management is resuscitation and decompression. Surgery indicated if signs of strangulation or complete obstruction not resolving.

options.text: NPO, NG tube decompression, IV fluids, surgical consult
options.explanation: Initial management: NPO, NG tube (decompresses stomach, reduces vomiting/aspiration risk), aggressive IV fluids (significant third-spacing), and surgical consult for monitoring and potential intervention.
This is SMALL bowel obstruction. Colonoscopy is for large bowel. Also contraindicated in obstruction.
Antibiotics only if perforation, strangulation, or peritonitis suspected. Not routine for uncomplicated SBO.

mcqs.question: Which finding would indicate STRANGULATED bowel requiring emergent surgery?

options.explanation: High-pitched (tinkling) bowel sounds are classic for obstruction but don't indicate strangulation.
Air-fluid levels indicate obstruction but not necessarily strangulation.

options.text: Fever, peritoneal signs, and elevated lactate
options.explanation: Strangulation signs: Fever, tachycardia, peritoneal signs (rebound, guarding), elevated lactate/WBC, and focal constant tenderness. SURGICAL EMERGENCY.
Distension is expected in obstruction but doesn't indicate strangulation.

allDiagnoses.name: Small Bowel Obstruction
allDiagnoses.rationale: Colicky abdominal pain, vomiting, distension, obstipation, prior surgery (adhesion risk), CT findings.

allDiagnoses.name: Adhesive Small Bowel Obstruction
allDiagnoses.rationale: Most common cause of SBO in developed countries. History of prior abdominal surgery.

allDiagnoses.name: Strangulated Obstruction
allDiagnoses.rationale: Ischemic bowel - surgical emergency. Watch for fever, peritonitis, elevated lactate.

allDiagnoses.name: Incarcerated Hernia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Hernia containing bowel can cause obstruction. Examine ALL hernia sites carefully.

allDiagnoses.name: Large Bowel Obstruction
allDiagnoses.rationale: Different management than SBO. CT helps differentiate. Often malignant.

allDiagnoses.name: Closed Loop Obstruction
allDiagnoses.rationale: Segment obstructed at two points - high risk of strangulation.

allDiagnoses.name: Ileus
allDiagnoses.rationale: Functional obstruction without mechanical cause. Different on imaging (diffuse dilation, no transition point).

allDiagnoses.name: Gastroparesis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Delayed gastric emptying, but different presentation and imaging findings.

female-68-sbo.oldcartsNarrative: "My stomach has been hurting for 2 days. It started as crampy pain that comes and goes in waves, but now it's pretty constant with these waves of worse cramping. I've been vomiting a lot - at first just what I ate, now it's this yellow-green bitter stuff. The pain is all over my stomach, maybe worse in the middle around my belly button. It's probably a 7 out of 10 right now. Nothing makes it better - I've tried antacids and lying in different positions. I've been trying to drink water but it just comes back up. I feel weak and a little dizzy."

historyQuestions.text: When did this start?
historyQuestions.answer: It started 2 days ago. First it was just crampy stomach pain that came and went, but over the past day it's gotten worse and more constant. The vomiting started yesterday.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Timeline helps determine severity and urgency - longer duration increases strangulation risk

historyQuestions.text: Describe the abdominal pain
historyQuestions.answer: It started as crampy, coming and going in waves. Now it's more constant but still has these waves of worse cramping. It's all over my belly, maybe worse around my belly button.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Colicky (crampy, intermittent) pain is classic for obstruction - peristalsis against obstruction

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the vomiting
historyQuestions.answer: I've vomited probably 8-10 times. At first it was food, then just this yellow-green bitter stuff. I can't keep anything down.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Bilious vomiting (green) indicates obstruction distal to the ampulla. Feculent vomiting would suggest more distal obstruction.

historyQuestions.text: When was your last bowel movement? Have you passed gas?
historyQuestions.answer: No bowel movement for 3 days, and I haven't passed gas either.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Obstipation (no stool or flatus) is cardinal sign of complete obstruction

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any abdominal surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I had my gallbladder removed laparoscopically 10 years ago and a hysterectomy through a big incision 15 years ago.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior abdominal surgery = risk for adhesions (scar tissue), the #1 cause of SBO

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Just metformin for my diabetes and lisinopril for blood pressure. Nothing for pain usually.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medication review - opioids can cause ileus; diabetes affects wound healing

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever had a hernia or noticed any bulges in your groin or belly?
historyQuestions.answer: No hernias that I know of. I haven't noticed any lumps.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Incarcerated hernia can cause SBO. Must examine hernia sites regardless of history.

historyQuestions.text: Any fevers or chills?
historyQuestions.answer: I've felt a little warm but no real shaking chills.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Fever suggests possible strangulation/ischemia or perforation - concerning sign

historyQuestions.text: Have you been able to drink or eat anything?
historyQuestions.answer: I've tried sipping water but it just comes right back up. I'm really thirsty and feel weak.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Significant fluid losses from vomiting and third-spacing - will need aggressive IV fluids

historyQuestions.text: Any allergies to medications or anesthesia?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies. I've had anesthesia before for my surgeries without any problems.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for potential surgical intervention and anesthesia planning

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: I have diabetes and high blood pressure - both controlled with pills. Had my gallbladder out and hysterectomy years ago. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Diabetes may affect surgical risk and wound healing; comorbidities for risk stratification

historyQuestions.text: Any family history of bowel problems or cancer?
historyQuestions.answer: My mother had colon cancer in her 70s. I've had colonoscopies every 5 years - my last one was 2 years ago and was normal.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Family history of colon cancer raises concern for malignant obstruction; recent normal colonoscopy reassuring

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever had a bowel obstruction before?
historyQuestions.answer: No, this has never happened to me before.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Recurrent SBO is common with adhesive disease

historyQuestions.text: Any recent weight loss?
historyQuestions.answer: No, my weight has been stable. I've actually been trying to lose a few pounds but haven't been successful.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Unexplained weight loss would increase concern for malignancy

historyQuestions.text: When did you last eat or drink?
historyQuestions.answer: I had some soup 2 days ago before this started. Since then just small sips of water that I vomited back up.
historyQuestions.reasoning: NPO status for potential surgery; confirms prolonged inability to tolerate PO

historyQuestions.text: Have you been urinating?
historyQuestions.answer: Barely. Just a small amount of dark urine this morning. I'm definitely not drinking enough.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Decreased urine output confirms significant dehydration and third-spacing

historyQuestions.text: Who is at home with you?
historyQuestions.answer: My husband is with me. He drove me in. We live together in a one-story house.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Support system and discharge planning considerations

physicalExam.reasoning: Tachycardia from dehydration/pain. Low-grade fever slightly concerning. Hypotension would be very concerning.

physicalExam.finding: Uncomfortable, lying still, knees drawn up. Dry mucous membranes. Appears dehydrated.
physicalExam.reasoning: Patients with obstruction often lie still. Dehydration evident.

physicalExam.finding: Abdomen visibly distended. Old well-healed surgical scars: RUQ (cholecystectomy ports) and low transverse (hysterectomy). No visible hernia bulges. No ecchymosis.
physicalExam.reasoning: Distension from dilated bowel. Scars confirm prior surgery. No visible hernias but still examine.

physicalExam.finding: High-pitched, tinkling bowel sounds with occasional rushes. Hyperactive in bursts.
physicalExam.reasoning: High-pitched, tinkling sounds with rushes are classic for mechanical obstruction (unlike ileus which has absent/hypoactive sounds)

physicalExam.finding: Diffusely tender, mildly distended. Tympanitic to percussion. No focal tenderness, no rebound, no guarding. No masses.
physicalExam.reasoning: Diffuse tenderness without peritoneal signs suggests obstruction without strangulation (yet). Focal tenderness/peritonitis = worry for ischemia.

physicalExam.finding: No inguinal hernias bilaterally. No femoral hernias. No umbilical hernia. No incisional hernia at surgical scars.
physicalExam.reasoning: MUST examine ALL hernia sites (inguinal, femoral, umbilical, incisional) in any patient with obstruction

physicalExam.finding: Empty rectal vault. No masses. No blood. Normal tone.
physicalExam.reasoning: Empty vault supports obstruction. Blood would suggest ischemia or other pathology.

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Mild leukocytosis. Markedly elevated WBC would suggest strangulation. Hemoconcentration from dehydration.

labs.name: Basic Metabolic Panel
labs.result: Na 138, K 3.2, Cl 94, CO2 30, BUN 32, Cr 1.3, Glucose 108
labs.reasoning: Hypokalemia and hypochloremia from vomiting (losing gastric acid). Elevated BUN from dehydration. Metabolic alkalosis (high CO2).

labs.name: Lactate
labs.reasoning: Normal lactate argues against bowel ischemia. Elevated lactate would be very concerning for strangulation.

labs.name: Lipase
labs.reasoning: Rules out pancreatitis as cause of vomiting and pain

imaging.name: Abdominal X-ray (KUB)
imaging.result: Multiple dilated loops of small bowel with air-fluid levels. No free air. Minimal gas in colon/rectum.
imaging.reasoning: Shows dilated small bowel (>3cm), air-fluid levels (classic stepladder pattern). Absence of colonic gas supports complete obstruction.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis with IV contrast
imaging.result: Dilated small bowel loops (up to 4.5cm) with decompressed distal small bowel. Transition point identified in RLQ with small bowel-to-small bowel adhesion. No free fluid, no pneumatosis, no portal venous gas. No hernia.
imaging.reasoning: CT is gold standard: identifies transition point, cause (adhesion vs hernia vs mass), and signs of strangulation (pneumatosis, portal gas, lack of wall enhancement).

treatments.name: NPO (nothing by mouth)
treatments.result: Strict NPO - no food or liquids by mouth
treatments.reasoning: Cannot eat/drink with obstruction. Would worsen distension and vomiting.

treatments.name: Nasogastric tube
treatments.result: NG tube placed to low intermittent suction. Drained 800cc bilious fluid.
treatments.reasoning: Decompresses stomach, reduces vomiting and aspiration risk, may decompress proximal small bowel.

treatments.name: IV fluid resuscitation
treatments.result: Lactated Ringer's 2L bolus, then 150 mL/hr. Replace NG losses.
treatments.reasoning: Significant third-spacing and vomiting losses. Need aggressive replacement. Replace NG output mL for mL.

treatments.name: Electrolyte repletion
treatments.result: Potassium 40 mEq IV
treatments.reasoning: Hypokalemia common from vomiting (losing H+ and K+). Must replete.

treatments.name: Foley catheter
treatments.result: Foley placed to monitor urine output
treatments.reasoning: Monitor resuscitation adequacy with urine output

treatments.name: Serial abdominal exams
treatments.result: Frequent reassessment for signs of strangulation
treatments.reasoning: Watch for worsening: fever, increasing tenderness, peritonitis, tachycardia

consults.name: Surgical consult
consults.result: Surgery consulted. Plan for conservative management trial (24-48 hours) with close monitoring.
consults.reasoning: Surgery must be involved early. Many adhesive SBOs resolve with conservative care, but surgery needed if no improvement, strangulation, or complete obstruction.

disposition.name: Admit to surgical service
disposition.result: Admit for bowel rest, decompression, IV fluids, and serial exams
disposition.reasoning: All SBO requires admission for monitoring and treatment.

actualDiagnosis.name: Adhesive Small Bowel Obstruction - Partial, Non-Strangulated
keyFindings: Cardinal symptoms: Colicky abdominal pain, bilious vomiting, distension, obstipation
Prior abdominal surgery (hysterectomy, cholecystectomy) = adhesion risk
Abdominal X-ray: Dilated small bowel with air-fluid levels
CT: Transition point in RLQ, adhesive band identified, no strangulation signs
No peritoneal signs (guarding, rebound) - argues against strangulation
teachingPoints: SBO cardinal symptoms: Crampy abdominal pain, vomiting (bilious if distal to ampulla), distension, obstipation (no stool/flatus)
Most common causes of SBO: Adhesions (65%), hernias (15%), malignancy (15%)
Large bowel obstruction differs: more distension, later vomiting, consider malignancy and volvulus
Signs of strangulation (surgical emergency): Fever, tachycardia, peritoneal signs, elevated lactate, focal tenderness, CT showing pneumatosis/portal gas/lack of wall enhancement
Initial management: NPO, NG tube decompression, aggressive IV fluids, electrolyte replacement, surgical consult
Electrolyte abnormalities from vomiting: Hypokalemia, hypochloremia, metabolic alkalosis (losing H+ and Cl- in gastric contents)
Most adhesive SBO resolves with conservative management (up to 72% in some studies)
Indications for surgery: Complete obstruction, strangulation, peritonitis, no improvement in 24-48 hours, closed-loop obstruction
CT findings concerning for strangulation: Mesenteric haziness, ascites, pneumatosis intestinalis (air in bowel wall), portal venous gas

guidelineReferences.name: WSES Guidelines for Management of Adhesive Small Bowel Obstruction 2017
guidelineReferences.citation: Ten Broek RPG et al. World J Emerg Surg. 2017;12:24
keyPoints: Water-soluble contrast (Gastrografin) at 24-48h predicts need for surgery
Signs of strangulation mandate immediate surgery
Conservative management successful in 70-90% of partial SBO
CT with IV contrast is gold standard for diagnosis
Surgery if no resolution after 72 hours of conservative management

guidelineReferences.name: EAST Practice Guidelines for Small Bowel Obstruction 2012
guidelineReferences.citation: Maung AA et al. J Trauma Acute Care Surg. 2012;73(5 Suppl 4):S362-369
keyPoints: CT superior to plain films for diagnosis and etiology
NG decompression + IV fluids + bowel rest for initial management
Laparoscopic adhesiolysis appropriate for experienced surgeons
Correct electrolyte abnormalities from vomiting
Identify transition point on CT to guide surgical planning

patientBackground.recentEvents: Patient was in usual state of health until symptoms began. No major recent life changes.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: No significant recent illnesses other than this presentation.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Dealing with typical life stressors. This illness is disrupting normal activities.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Has family and friends available for support. Not socially isolated.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Generally trusts medical advice. Wants to understand the reasoning behind recommendations.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Uses healthcare appropriately. No excessive ER utilization.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if lactate was elevated with peritoneal signs?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same obstruction but with fever 102°F, focal tenderness, guarding, and lactate 4.8

chiefComplaint.original: My stomach is cramping and I can't stop throwing up
chiefComplaint.modified: My stomach pain is constant now and much worse. I feel terrible.

historyAnswers.pain_pattern: At first the pain came in waves, but now it's constant and severe. It never lets up.
historyAnswers.pain_character: It changed. It used to cramp and then ease off. Now it's constant, horrible pain.
historyAnswers.pain_severity: 10 out of 10. This is the worst pain I've ever felt.
historyAnswers.pain_location: It started all over, but now it's really focused on my lower right side.
historyAnswers.fever: Yes, I developed a fever a few hours ago. I feel hot and shaky.
historyAnswers.sweating: I'm sweating but feel cold at the same time.
historyAnswers.vomit: I'm still vomiting, but it's starting to look brownish and smell really foul.
historyAnswers.vomit_appearance: It's brown and smells like stool. It's horrible.
historyAnswers.gas: No gas at all. Nothing is coming out.
historyAnswers.bloody_stool: When I tried to have a bowel movement, there was some blood on the toilet paper.
historyAnswers.getting_worse: Much worse. The pain is unbearable and I feel like I'm going to die.
historyAnswers.how_long_constant: The pain became constant about 4-5 hours ago.

examFindings.abdomen: Distended, tense. Focal tenderness RLQ with guarding. Rebound tenderness present. Absent bowel sounds.
examFindings.peritoneal_signs: Positive rebound, positive percussion tenderness, involuntary guarding

feedback.correct: STRANGULATION = SURGICAL EMERGENCY. Elevated lactate + peritoneal signs + fever = bowel is dying. Emergent surgery, not conservative management trial.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of strangulation signs. What's the management change?
feedback.incorrect: Signs of strangulation: peritoneal signs, fever, elevated lactate, constant pain (not colicky). This is SURGICAL EMERGENCY - bowel is ischemic/necrotic. No time for conservative trial.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: SIMPLE vs STRANGULATED SBO: Simple = colicky pain, no peritoneal signs, normal lactate → trial of conservative management. Strangulated = CONSTANT pain, peritoneal signs, fever, elevated lactate → EMERGENT SURGERY. CT findings: bowel wall thickening, mesenteric haziness, reduced enhancement, pneumatosis. NO NG tube decompression trial if strangulation suspected.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there was a tender groin bulge?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same symptoms but with incarcerated inguinal hernia on exam

chiefComplaint.original: My stomach is cramping and I can't stop throwing up
chiefComplaint.modified: My stomach is cramping, I'm throwing up, and this lump in my groin is painful and won't go back in

historyAnswers.hernia: Actually, yes - I've had a bulge in my groin for years. I never got it fixed.
historyAnswers.hernia_history: My doctor told me I had an inguinal hernia about 3 years ago, but I kept putting off the surgery.
historyAnswers.bulge: The bulge is much bigger now and it's really painful. It won't go back in like it used to.
historyAnswers.reducible: Usually I can push it back in, but now I can't. It's stuck out and very tender.
historyAnswers.groin_pain: Yes, my right groin is extremely painful where the bulge is.
historyAnswers.prior_surgery: No abdominal surgeries. Just the hernia they found.

examFindings.inguinal: Right inguinal bulge, firm, tender, non-reducible. Skin over bulge slightly erythematous.
examFindings.abdomen: Distended, tympanitic. Diffuse tenderness. High-pitched bowel sounds with rushes.

feedback.correct: Incarcerated hernia = attempt gentle reduction if no strangulation signs. If irreducible, tender, or skin changes = emergent surgery. This is the cause of the obstruction.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of hernia as obstruction cause. What's the initial management?
feedback.incorrect: Incarcerated (stuck) hernia causing SBO. Attempt gentle reduction if recently incarcerated with no strangulation signs. If irreducible, tender, erythematous skin, or systemic toxicity = emergent surgery. This is cause, not adhesions.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: INCARCERATED vs STRANGULATED HERNIA: Incarcerated = stuck, can't reduce. Strangulated = blood supply compromised, ischemic. Adhesive SBO = no external cause found. Incarcerated hernia = cause is identified, must address hernia (reduction or surgery). Signs of strangulation: fever, skin changes (erythema), extreme tenderness, systemic toxicity.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the obstruction was in the large bowel?
whatIfScenarios.description: Elderly patient with massive distension, late vomiting, and apple-core lesion on CT

chiefComplaint.original: My stomach is cramping and I can't stop throwing up
chiefComplaint.modified: My belly has been getting bigger for a week. I haven't had a bowel movement in 5 days. Now I'm vomiting.

feedback.correct: LBO in elderly = colon cancer until proven otherwise. More distension, later vomiting than SBO. Risk of cecal perforation if ileocecal valve competent. May need decompressing colostomy then staged resection.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of LBO pattern. What's the most common cause in elderly?
feedback.incorrect: Large bowel obstruction differs from small: more distension, later vomiting, longer duration. In elderly, COLON CANCER is most common cause. Risk of cecal perforation with closed-loop. May need diverting colostomy before definitive resection.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: SBO = earlier vomiting, less distension, adhesions most common. LBO = more distension, late vomiting, cancer/volvulus common causes. Treatment differs significantly.

guidelineReferences.citation: Katz PO, et al. Am J Gastroenterol. 2022;117:27-56
keyPoints: PPI for 8 weeks trial
EGD for alarm symptoms
Lifestyle modifications

PPI for NSAID-induced ulcers
