treatments.name: IV PPI (pantoprazole)
treatments.result: Started 40mg IV BID
treatments.reasoning: Helps if concomitant PUD, gastritis, or post-banding ulcers.

treatments.name: Platelet transfusion
treatments.result: Not given - platelets 68k, threshold typically <50k with active bleeding.
treatments.reasoning: Consider if platelets <50,000 with active bleeding.

treatments.name: Cryoprecipitate
treatments.result: Not given - fibrinogen 145.
treatments.reasoning: Give if fibrinogen <100-120 mg/dL with active bleeding.

treatments.name: Terlipressin (if available)
treatments.result: Not available in US. Alternative to octreotide.
treatments.reasoning: Vasopressin analogue used in Europe. May have survival benefit over octreotide.

treatments.name: Non-selective beta-blocker (nadolol/propranolol)
treatments.result: Hold in acute bleeding. Start after stabilization for secondary prophylaxis.
treatments.reasoning: DO NOT start during acute bleed. Start for secondary prophylaxis once stable.

treatments.name: IV fluid resuscitation
treatments.result: Use cautiously - avoid over-resuscitation.
treatments.reasoning: Be conservative - excessive fluids increase portal pressure and rebleeding risk.

treatments.name: Vitamin K
treatments.result: 10mg IV given
treatments.reasoning: Limited benefit in cirrhosis (problem is synthetic dysfunction, not vitamin K deficiency), but often given.

consults.name: GI consult - emergent
consults.result: Emergent GI consulted for EGD.
consults.reasoning: Emergent EGD within 12 hours is standard of care.

consults.name: ICU admission
consults.result: Admitted to MICU for hemodynamic monitoring and airway management.
consults.reasoning: Variceal bleeding requires ICU-level care.

consults.name: Hepatology consult
consults.result: Consulted for cirrhosis management and transplant evaluation.
consults.reasoning: Optimize cirrhosis management; evaluate transplant candidacy.

consults.name: IR consult for TIPS
consults.result: Aware and on standby if EGD fails.
consults.reasoning: TIPS is rescue therapy if endoscopic treatment fails.

disposition.name: Admit to ICU
disposition.result: Appropriate - hemodynamically unstable variceal bleed.
disposition.reasoning: Variceal bleeding with hemodynamic instability requires ICU.

disposition.name: Admit to floor
disposition.result: Not appropriate for acute variceal bleed.
disposition.reasoning: Active variceal bleeding requires ICU-level monitoring.

disposition.name: Discharge
disposition.result: Absolutely not appropriate.
disposition.reasoning: Variceal bleeding has 20-30% 6-week mortality - never discharge acutely.

actualDiagnosis.name: Ruptured Esophageal Varices - Decompensated Alcoholic Cirrhosis
keyFindings: Known esophageal varices (not on beta-blocker prophylaxis)
Stigmata of chronic liver disease (spider angiomata, ascites, encephalopathy)
teachingPoints: MORTALITY: 20-30% at 6 weeks despite treatment. This is a true emergency.
TARGET Hb ~8 g/dL: Over-transfusion increases portal pressure and rebleeding risk
PROPHYLACTIC ANTIBIOTICS: ALL cirrhotics with GI bleed need antibiotics (reduces mortality!)
THREE GOALS: (1) Hemodynamic stability, (2) Prevent complications (aspiration, encephalopathy, renal failure), (3) Stop bleeding
OCTREOTIDE: Start immediately, before EGD. Splanchnic vasoconstriction reduces portal pressure.
EGD within 12 hours: Both diagnostic AND therapeutic. Band ligation preferred over sclerotherapy.
REBLEEDING: Risk highest in first 48 hours. 50% of rebleeds occur within 10 days.
TIPS: Reserved for failure of endoscopic + pharmacologic therapy

immediate: Large-bore IV access x2 or central line
resuscitation: Transfuse PRBCs to target Hgb ~8 g/dL
Correct coagulopathy: FFP if INR >1.5, Platelets if <50,000
Monitor for encephalopathy - start lactulose if present
definitiveTherapy: EGD within 12 hours - band ligation preferred
If EGD fails → repeat EGD or TIPS
Balloon tamponade (Sengstaken-Blakemore) = bridge to definitive therapy only

evidenceBasedPearls.content: Reduces bacterial infections (RR 0.40) and mortality (RR 0.73). Use ceftriaxone for advanced cirrhosis (Child B/C).
Target Hb 8 g/dL. Higher levels increase portal pressure and rebleeding risk.
Somatostatin/octreotide + endoscopic therapy decreases early rebleeding but doesn't improve mortality.
Highest in first 48 hours. Risk factors: bilirubin, PT, albumin, encephalopathy, age >60, large varices.

guidelineReferences.name: AASLD Practice Guidance on Portal Hypertensive Bleeding 2022
guidelineReferences.citation: Garcia-Tsao G et al. Hepatology. 2022;76(3):799-842
keyPoints: Start IV octreotide and prophylactic antibiotics immediately
EGD within 12 hours with band ligation for esophageal varices
Restrictive transfusion: target Hgb 7-8 g/dL
Pre-emptive TIPS for high-risk (Child C or B with active bleeding)
TIPS for refractory bleeding or early rebleed

guidelineReferences.name: Baveno VII Consensus on Portal Hypertension 2022
guidelineReferences.citation: de Franchis R et al. J Hepatol. 2022;76(4):959-974
keyPoints: Non-selective beta-blockers (NSBB) for primary prophylaxis
Carvedilol is preferred NSBB in most patients
Avoid NSBB in refractory ascites with SBP <90
Antibiotic prophylaxis for 5-7 days after variceal bleed
Consider early TIPS within 72 hours for high-risk patients

alcoholic-56-varices.clinicalPearl: BOTTOM LINE: Variceal bleeding has 20-30% mortality at 6 weeks. Start octreotide and antibiotics IMMEDIATELY. Target Hb ~8 (not normal!) - over-transfusion increases portal pressure and rebleeding. EGD within 12 hours for band ligation. ALL cirrhotics with GI bleed need prophylactic antibiotics - this reduces mortality!

patientBackground.recentEvents: Patient was in usual state of health until symptoms began. No major recent life changes.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: No significant recent illnesses other than this presentation.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Dealing with typical life stressors. This illness is disrupting normal activities.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Has family and friends available for support. Not socially isolated.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Generally trusts medical advice. Wants to understand the reasoning behind recommendations.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Uses healthcare appropriately. No excessive ER utilization.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if endoscopy failed to control bleeding?
whatIfScenarios.description: Continued massive hemorrhage despite band ligation attempts

chiefComplaint.original: I'm vomiting blood
chiefComplaint.modified: Patient continues to vomit blood after two EGD attempts at band ligation

expectedReasoning.topDiagnosis: Refractory variceal bleeding - need rescue therapy

feedback.correct: Failed endoscopy = TIPS (transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt). Balloon tamponade (Sengstaken-Blakemore or Minnesota tube) is BRIDGE to TIPS only - high complication rate, max 24 hours.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of rescue therapy need. What's the definitive option?
feedback.incorrect: Rescue for failed endoscopy: TIPS is definitive (reduces portal pressure). Balloon tamponade (Sengstaken-Blakemore) is temporary bridge only - risk of esophageal necrosis/perforation, max 24 hours. Always involves IR or surgery.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: First-line = EGD with band ligation + octreotide. Failed endoscopy = TIPS. Balloon tamponade = temporizing only, high risk. Never leave balloon inflated >24 hours.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient became confused during resuscitation?
whatIfScenarios.description: Altered mental status developing during variceal bleed treatment

chiefComplaint.original: I'm vomiting blood
chiefComplaint.modified: Patient was answering questions but now is confused and drowsy

expectedReasoning.topDiagnosis: Hepatic encephalopathy precipitated by GI bleed

feedback.correct: GI bleed precipitates hepatic encephalopathy - blood in gut = protein load → ammonia. Start lactulose, consider rifaximin. Also: INTUBATE for airway protection before EGD. Check glucose.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of encephalopathy. What's the mechanism with GI bleed?
feedback.incorrect: GI bleed in cirrhotic → blood = protein in gut → bacterial breakdown → ammonia → hepatic encephalopathy. Treatment: lactulose (trap ammonia), rifaximin. CRITICAL: encephalopathy + vomiting blood = intubate for airway protection before EGD.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Alert cirrhotic with variceal bleed = EGD without intubation often OK. Encephalopathic cirrhotic = INTUBATE first for airway protection, then EGD. Altered mental status changes the airway plan.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if Hgb was transfused to 12 g/dL?
whatIfScenarios.description: Aggressive transfusion brought hemoglobin to 12, now rebleeding

chiefComplaint.original: I'm vomiting blood
chiefComplaint.modified: We transfused 6 units, Hgb now 12. Patient just had another large hematemesis.

feedback.correct: Over-transfusion in variceal bleeding INCREASES portal pressure and rebleeding risk! Target Hgb 7-8, not normal. Restrictive transfusion strategy has BETTER outcomes in variceal bleeding.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of the transfusion issue. What's the target Hgb?
feedback.incorrect: Variceal bleeding transfusion target: Hgb 7-8 g/dL (NOT normal!). Higher Hgb → increased blood volume → increased portal pressure → increased variceal pressure → REBLEEDING. Restrictive transfusion improves survival.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Non-variceal GI bleed = can transfuse to Hgb 7-8 (or higher if cardiac disease). Variceal bleed = MUST keep restrictive (Hgb 7-8) to avoid increasing portal pressure. Different physiology, different targets.

diabetic-78-ischemic.name: E. Patterson
diabetic-78-ischemic.chiefComplaint: Abdominal pain and bloody bowel movements
diabetic-78-ischemic.variantDescription: Ischemic colitis in elderly diabetic vasculopath

missedConsequences.Ischemic Colitis: Assumed to be infectious colitis, sent home with antibiotics. Returns 2 days later with peritonitis from transmural necrosis requiring emergent colectomy.
missedConsequences.Mesenteric Ischemia: Ischemic colitis diagnosed but mesenteric ischemia not considered. Patient actually has SMA thrombosis and develops bowel infarction.

commonPitfalls: Confusing ischemic colitis with infectious colitis - both have bloody diarrhea but different management
Missing the 'watershed' distribution - splenic flexure and rectosigmoid most vulnerable
Ordering barium enema - contraindicated
Not recognizing risk factors for ischemia (DM, vascular disease, AF, hypotension)
Performing colonoscopy with standard bowel prep - can worsen ischemia
Missing progression to transmural necrosis requiring surgery

allDiagnoses.name: Ischemic Colitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Elderly diabetic vasculopath with gradual onset pain + bloody stools. Classic presentation.

allDiagnoses.name: Infectious Colitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Bloody diarrhea can be infectious (C. diff, E. coli, Salmonella). Need to rule out.

allDiagnoses.name: Mesenteric Ischemia (Acute)
allDiagnoses.rationale: CRITICAL: More severe than ischemic colitis. Sudden severe pain, toxic appearance.

allDiagnoses.name: Inflammatory Bowel Disease
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can present similarly. Bimodal distribution but less common at this age.

allDiagnoses.name: Colorectal Cancer
allDiagnoses.rationale: Must rule out in elderly with rectal bleeding.

allDiagnoses.name: Diverticulitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Usually LLQ pain, fever, leukocytosis. Can have bleeding but typically not profuse bloody diarrhea.

allDiagnoses.name: C. difficile Colitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Ask about recent antibiotics. Watery diarrhea more common than bloody.

diabetic-78-ischemic.oldcartsNarrative: "My stomach started hurting about 3 days ago - a crampy pain mostly on the left side. It came on gradually and has gotten worse. Then yesterday I started having bloody bowel movements - there's a lot of blood mixed in with the stool. I've had maybe 6-8 bloody stools since then. The pain isn't as bad as you'd think with all that blood. I have diabetes, high blood pressure, and I've had a small stroke before. I take metformin, lisinopril, and aspirin."

historyQuestions.text: When did this start?
historyQuestions.answer: The crampy pain started about 3 days ago and came on gradually. Then yesterday morning I noticed the bloody stools - maybe 6-8 episodes since then.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Gradual onset over days distinguishes from acute mesenteric ischemia which is sudden and severe

historyQuestions.text: Tell me exactly how the pain started
historyQuestions.answer: It came on gradually over a day or so. Not sudden at all. Started as cramping, then got worse.
historyQuestions.reasoning: GRADUAL onset distinguishes ischemic colitis from mesenteric ischemia (which is SUDDEN, severe).

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your vascular history - strokes, heart disease, peripheral vascular disease?
historyQuestions.answer: I had a small stroke 2 years ago. My doctor says I have 'hardening of the arteries.' My legs hurt when I walk far.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Vasculopathy (DM, stroke, PVD) = high risk for ischemic colitis.

historyQuestions.text: How severe is the pain compared to the amount of bleeding?
historyQuestions.answer: Honestly, the pain is moderate - maybe 5 out of 10. I expected it to hurt more with all that blood.
historyQuestions.reasoning: 'Pain out of proportion' (more pain than expected) suggests mesenteric ischemia. Moderate pain with bleeding = ischemic colitis.

historyQuestions.text: Have you been sick, dehydrated, or had low blood pressure recently?
historyQuestions.answer: I was pretty sick with a stomach bug last week. Wasn't eating or drinking much for a few days.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Low-flow state (dehydration, hypotension) is a common cause of NON-OCCLUSIVE ischemic colitis.

historyQuestions.text: Any recent antibiotics?
historyQuestions.answer: No antibiotics recently.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Rules out antibiotic-associated C. diff colitis.

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: I take metformin for my diabetes, lisinopril for blood pressure, and aspirin every day since my stroke.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Aspirin use important for bleeding risk. ACE-I can affect renal perfusion. Full medication list needed before any procedures or interventions.

historyQuestions.text: Any allergies to medications?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm allergic to sulfa drugs - I get a rash. No other allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical before antibiotics, contrast, and any other medications. Sulfa allergy affects antibiotic choices.

historyQuestions.text: What other medical problems do you have besides diabetes?
historyQuestions.answer: Diabetes, high blood pressure, high cholesterol, and I had that mini-stroke 2 years ago. I also have pain in my legs when I walk - they called it claudication.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Confirms extensive vascular risk factors. Claudication confirms peripheral arterial disease - vasculopathic patient at high risk for mesenteric ischemia.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

physicalExam.finding: Elderly woman, mildly uncomfortable but NOT toxic appearing. No acute distress.
physicalExam.reasoning: NON-TOXIC appearance is hallmark of ischemic colitis. Toxic = consider mesenteric ischemia or perforation.

physicalExam.finding: Mild tenderness in LEFT lower quadrant. No rebound, no guarding. Bowel sounds present. Abdomen soft.
physicalExam.reasoning: LEFT-sided tenderness (watershed areas: splenic flexure, sigmoid). NO peritoneal signs = mucosal ischemia, not transmural.

physicalExam.finding: Bright red blood mixed with stool. No masses.
physicalExam.reasoning: Confirms bloody stool. Early bleeding is characteristic of ischemic colitis (late finding in mesenteric ischemia).

physicalExam.finding: Regular rhythm, no murmurs. No signs of heart failure.
physicalExam.reasoning: Atrial fibrillation would suggest embolic etiology.

physicalExam.finding: Diminished dorsalis pedis pulses bilaterally. No femoral bruits.
physicalExam.reasoning: Confirms peripheral vascular disease, supporting vasculopathic etiology.

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Mild leukocytosis common. Hgb stable suggests not massive bleeding. Marked leukocytosis >20k raises concern for necrosis.

labs.name: BMP
labs.reasoning: Mild AKI from dehydration. Hyperglycemia expected in diabetic under stress. Baseline for resuscitation.

labs.name: Lactate
labs.reasoning: Normal lactate is REASSURING. Elevated lactate suggests transmural ischemia/necrosis - surgical emergency.

labs.name: Stool culture, O&P, C. diff toxin
labs.reasoning: Rules out infectious colitis which mimics ischemic colitis.

labs.name: Type and Screen
labs.reasoning: Prepare for potential transfusion if bleeding worsens.

labs.name: PT/INR, PTT
labs.reasoning: Baseline coagulation; elevated INR may suggest liver dysfunction or nutritional deficiency.

labs.name: Liver Function Tests
labs.result: Mildly elevated AST/ALT (stress response). Normal bilirubin.
labs.reasoning: Can be elevated from hypoperfusion; normal is reassuring.

labs.name: Lipase
labs.reasoning: Rules out pancreatitis as cause of abdominal pain.

labs.name: Arterial Blood Gas
labs.result: pH 7.38, normal bicarbonate. No metabolic acidosis.
labs.reasoning: Metabolic acidosis would suggest severe ischemia/necrosis.

labs.name: Procalcitonin
labs.reasoning: May help distinguish infectious from ischemic etiology, but not well studied in this context.

labs.name: LDH
labs.reasoning: Non-specific marker of tissue damage. Markedly elevated suggests necrosis.

labs.name: D-dimer
labs.reasoning: Often elevated in ischemia but non-specific. Does not change management.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis with IV contrast
imaging.result: Segmental wall thickening of sigmoid colon and splenic flexure. No pneumatosis. No free air. Atherosclerotic calcification of aorta and mesenteric vessels.
imaging.reasoning: CT is first-line imaging. Only 39% specific for ischemia but rules out perforation, obstruction, and other causes. Look for 'thumbprinting,' wall thickening, mesenteric stranding.

imaging.name: Abdominal X-ray
imaging.result: Nonspecific. No free air. No obstruction. Possible 'thumbprinting' in descending colon.
imaging.reasoning: Plain film only 30% sensitive but can show thumbprinting, exclude free air and obstruction.

imaging.name: CT Angiography (mesenteric vessels)
imaging.result: No occlusion of SMA or IMA. Diffuse atherosclerosis. No evidence of acute thrombus.
imaging.reasoning: Distinguishes occlusive from non-occlusive ischemia. Rules out acute mesenteric ischemia requiring intervention.

imaging.name: Upright Chest X-ray
imaging.result: No free air under diaphragm.
imaging.reasoning: Rules out perforation.

imaging.name: MR Angiography
imaging.result: Not typically indicated acutely.
imaging.reasoning: CTA is preferred in acute setting. MRA can be used if CTA contraindicated.

procedures.name: Colonoscopy (without standard bowel prep)
procedures.result: Segmental colitis involving splenic flexure and descending colon. Mucosal edema, erythema, and friability. Linear ulceration ('single stripe sign'). No transmural necrosis. Rectum spared.
procedures.reasoning: DIAGNOSTIC gold standard. Perform WITHOUT dehydrating bowel prep. Typical findings: edema, friability, linear ulcers, watershed distribution, rectal sparing.

procedures.name: Large-bore IV access
procedures.result: Two 18-gauge IVs placed.
procedures.reasoning: For resuscitation and potential blood products.

procedures.name: Foley catheter
procedures.result: Placed. Monitoring urine output.
procedures.reasoning: Monitor perfusion during resuscitation.

procedures.name: Nasogastric tube
procedures.result: Not routinely needed unless severe nausea/vomiting.
procedures.reasoning: May help with decompression if significant ileus.

procedures.name: Flexible sigmoidoscopy
procedures.result: Alternative to colonoscopy if patient unstable.
procedures.reasoning: Can visualize left colon without sedation; may miss proximal disease.

treatments.name: IV fluid resuscitation
treatments.result: 2L NS bolus given, then 125 mL/hr maintenance.
treatments.reasoning: OPTIMIZE colonic perfusion. Address underlying low-flow state. This is cornerstone of treatment.

treatments.name: NPO / Bowel rest
treatments.result: Made NPO. Consider NG tube if significant ileus.
treatments.reasoning: Reduces metabolic demand on ischemic bowel. Resume diet when pain resolved and bowel function returns.

treatments.name: Broad-spectrum antibiotics (ciprofloxacin + metronidazole)
treatments.result: Started for moderate disease severity.
treatments.reasoning: Antibiotics for MODERATE-SEVERE disease only. Limited evidence but theoretically prevents bacterial translocation through damaged mucosa.

treatments.name: Hold anticoagulation/antiplatelet
treatments.result: Aspirin held given GI bleeding.
treatments.reasoning: Balance bleeding risk vs cardiovascular risk. Usually hold during active bleeding.

treatments.name: Optimize cardiac output
treatments.result: Avoid hypotension. Gentle hydration.
treatments.reasoning: Low-flow state often precipitates ischemic colitis. Optimize perfusion.

treatments.name: Avoid vasoconstrictors if possible
treatments.result: Pressors avoided; fluid resuscitation adequate.
treatments.reasoning: Vasoconstrictors can worsen mesenteric ischemia. If needed, use lowest effective dose.

treatments.name: Pain management
treatments.result: Morphine 2mg IV PRN.
treatments.reasoning: Adequate pain control. Avoid NSAIDs (bleeding risk).

treatments.name: Blood transfusion
treatments.result: Not needed - Hgb stable.
treatments.reasoning: Transfuse if Hgb drops significantly or symptomatic anemia.

treatments.name: Surgical consultation
treatments.result: Surgery consulted - observation recommended given no peritoneal signs.
treatments.reasoning: Surgery indicated for: peritoneal signs, massive bleeding, fulminant colitis, clinical deterioration.

consults.name: GI consult for colonoscopy
consults.result: GI consulted. Colonoscopy performed.
consults.reasoning: Colonoscopy is diagnostic gold standard.

consults.name: Surgery consult
consults.result: General surgery aware - monitoring for deterioration.
consults.reasoning: Early surgical involvement for patients with moderate-severe disease. Surgery if: peritonitis, massive bleeding, clinical deterioration, necrosis.

consults.name: Vascular surgery consult
consults.result: Not immediately needed for non-occlusive disease.
consults.reasoning: Consider if CTA shows mesenteric occlusion requiring intervention.

disposition.name: Admit to medicine floor with telemetry
disposition.result: Appropriate for mild-moderate ischemic colitis.
disposition.reasoning: Most patients can be managed on floor with close monitoring. Serial exams and lactates.

disposition.name: Admit to ICU
disposition.result: Consider if hemodynamically unstable or peritoneal signs.
disposition.reasoning: ICU for severe disease, hemodynamic instability, or concern for necrosis.

disposition.name: Discharge home
disposition.result: Not appropriate with active ischemic colitis.
disposition.reasoning: Requires inpatient monitoring for clinical deterioration, which can occur rapidly.

actualDiagnosis.name: Ischemic Colitis - Non-Occlusive (Low-Flow State)
keyFindings: GRADUAL onset of abdominal pain (vs. sudden in mesenteric ischemia)
EARLY bleeding (blood with stool from day 2)
Colonoscopy showing segmental colitis at splenic flexure
teachingPoints: BOTTOM LINE: 'Abdominal pain with bloody diarrhea in elderly vasculopath = ischemic colitis until proven otherwise'
HALLMARKS: Gradual onset, early bleeding, non-toxic appearance
WATERSHED AREAS: Splenic flexure (Griffith's point) and rectosigmoid junction most vulnerable
ISCHEMIC vs MESENTERIC: Ischemic colitis = gradual, moderate pain, non-toxic. Mesenteric ischemia = sudden, severe 'pain out of proportion,' toxic.
DIAGNOSIS: Colonoscopy WITHOUT bowel prep confirms. CT only 39% specific but helps rule out other causes.
TREATMENT: Supportive care (fluids, bowel rest). Antibiotics only for moderate-severe disease.
SURGERY INDICATIONS: Peritoneal signs, massive bleeding, fulminant colitis +/- toxic megacolon
COMPLICATIONS: Stricture formation from scarring, chronic ischemia

guidelineReferences.name: ACG Clinical Guidelines: Ischemic Colitis 2015
guidelineReferences.citation: Brandt LJ et al. Am J Gastroenterol. 2015;110(1):18-44
keyPoints: Colonoscopy without prep is diagnostic test of choice
CT not specific (39%) but rules out other diagnoses
Most cases resolve with supportive care
Surgery for peritoneal signs, massive bleeding, or toxic megacolon
Antibiotics only for moderate-severe disease

guidelineReferences.name: AGA Technical Review on Intestinal Ischemia
guidelineReferences.citation: American Gastroenterological Association
keyPoints: Watershed areas: splenic flexure and rectosigmoid most vulnerable
Gradual onset, early bleeding, non-toxic = ischemic colitis
Sudden severe pain out of proportion = mesenteric ischemia (emergency)
Risk factors: advanced age, cardiovascular disease, low-flow states
Stricture is late complication requiring surgery

diagnosticPredictionRule.title: 100% PPV for Ischemic Colitis
diagnosticPredictionRule.criteria: LLQ pain + bloody BM + 4 of 6 risk factors
diagnosticPredictionRule.source: Park et al., Dis Colon Rectum 2007

anatomyPearl.title: Watershed Areas
anatomyPearl.content: SMA supplies up to mid-transverse colon. IMA supplies distal transverse to proximal rectum. Marginal artery of Drummond connects them but is inadequate/absent in 5% of people. Splenic flexure (Griffith's point) and rectosigmoid junction are most vulnerable. Rectum rarely ischemic (dual supply from IMA and internal iliac).

evidenceBasedPearls.content: Plain film 30% sensitive. CT 39% specific. Neither rules in or out ischemic colitis.
Linear colonic ulcer on colonoscopy associated with milder disease and better outcomes.
evidenceBasedPearls.source: Zuckerman et al., Am J Gastroenterol 2003

evidenceBasedPearls.content: Limited evidence - based on animal studies from 1945-1962 showing decreased bowel damage. Recommended only for moderate-severe disease.
Acute: peritoneal signs, massive bleeding, fulminant colitis. Subacute: non-response to medical therapy after 2-3 weeks, recurrent sepsis, symptomatic stricture.

diabetic-78-ischemic.clinicalPearl: BOTTOM LINE: In an elderly vasculopath (DM, HTN, PVD), abdominal pain with bloody diarrhea = ischemic colitis until proven otherwise. The hallmarks are GRADUAL onset, EARLY bleeding, and NON-TOXIC appearance. This is different from mesenteric ischemia which is SUDDEN, SEVERE pain, and TOXIC patient. Treatment is supportive. Surgery only for peritoneal signs or massive bleeding.

patientBackground.recentEvents: Patient was in usual state of health until symptoms began. No major recent life changes.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: No significant recent illnesses other than this presentation.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Multiple chronic conditions. Concerned about maintaining independence.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Has family involved in care. May have some assistance with daily activities.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Generally trusts medical advice. Wants to understand the reasoning behind recommendations.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Uses healthcare appropriately. No excessive ER utilization.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the pain was severe and 'out of proportion' to exam?
whatIfScenarios.description: Sudden severe abdominal pain, minimal findings on exam, patient appears toxic

chiefComplaint.original: I have crampy belly pain and bloody diarrhea
chiefComplaint.modified: Sudden severe abdominal pain - worst of my life. Minimal bloody stool. I feel like I'm dying.

keyDiscriminators: Pain out of proportion to exam

feedback.correct: PAIN OUT OF PROPORTION = acute mesenteric ischemia until proven otherwise. This is SURGICAL EMERGENCY. CTA mesenteric vessels, surgery consult, possible embolectomy/revascularization. Much higher mortality than ischemic colitis.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of mesenteric ischemia. How does it differ from ischemic colitis?
feedback.incorrect: Ischemic colitis = gradual, bloody diarrhea early, non-toxic, supportive care. Acute MESENTERIC ischemia = sudden severe pain, 'out of proportion,' toxic, late bloody stool (bowel dying). SURGICAL EMERGENCY - CTA, OR for revascularization.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Ischemic colitis = large bowel, non-occlusive, supportive care, good prognosis. Mesenteric ischemia = small bowel, often occlusive (embolus/thrombus), surgical emergency, high mortality. Key: pain out of proportion.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there were peritoneal signs and elevated lactate?
whatIfScenarios.description: Ischemic colitis progressing to peritonitis with lactate 8.5

chiefComplaint.original: I have crampy belly pain and bloody diarrhea
chiefComplaint.modified: The pain is much worse now, my whole belly hurts, I feel terrible

feedback.correct: Peritoneal signs + elevated lactate = transmural ischemia/necrosis. This is now SURGICAL. Emergent surgery for resection of necrotic bowel. No longer manageable conservatively.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of surgical emergency. What changed from the baseline case?
feedback.incorrect: Ischemic colitis can progress to transmural necrosis. Signs: peritoneal signs, elevated lactate, fever, shock. This requires SURGICAL resection - can no longer observe. Mortality increases significantly once transmural.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Mucosal ischemic colitis = supportive care, most resolve. Transmural necrosis = surgery required. The lactate and peritoneal signs tell you the bowel is dying.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the pain was on the right side?
whatIfScenarios.description: Right-sided colonic ischemia in young patient on estrogen

chiefComplaint.original: I have crampy belly pain and bloody diarrhea
chiefComplaint.modified: 32-year-old woman on birth control: Right-sided abdominal pain with bloody diarrhea

feedback.correct: RIGHT-sided ischemic colitis has WORSE prognosis than left-sided (closer to SMA territory). In young patients, think hypercoagulable states (estrogen, antiphospholipid, malignancy). May need more aggressive management.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of atypical location. Why does right-sided matter?
feedback.incorrect: Typical ischemic colitis = LEFT-sided (watershed areas). RIGHT-sided is atypical and more concerning - closer to SMA territory, higher mortality, more often requires surgery. In young patients, investigate for hypercoagulable states (estrogen, thrombophilia, malignancy).

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Left-sided = typical watershed ischemia, usually resolves with supportive care. Right-sided = atypical, worse prognosis, more likely to need surgery. Location predicts outcome.

nurse-27-needlestick.name: S. Martinez
nurse-27-needlestick.variantDescription: Acute viral hepatitis from occupational needle stick

missedConsequences.Acute Hepatitis B: Needle stick exposure not elicited in history. Patient treated for nonspecific viral illness. Develops fulminant hepatic failure 2 weeks later.
missedConsequences.Hepatitis C: HCV not tested. Patient progresses to chronic HCV (75-80% do). Discovered years later with cirrhosis.

commonPitfalls: Not asking about exposure history (needle stick, sexual contacts, IV drug use)
Only testing for one type of hepatitis when all should be checked
Misinterpreting hepatitis serologies
Not recognizing that most acute viral hepatitis is ASYMPTOMATIC
Using ultrasound to diagnose viral hepatitis (serologies are diagnostic)
Not adjusting hepatically metabolized medications

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Hepatitis B
allDiagnoses.rationale: Needle stick exposure 10 weeks ago. Classic incubation period. Jaundice, RUQ pain, hepatomegaly.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Hepatitis C
allDiagnoses.rationale: Also transmitted via needle stick. Often co-exists with HBV.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Hepatitis A
allDiagnoses.rationale: Fecal-oral transmission. Ask about travel, food exposures. Usually self-limited.

allDiagnoses.name: Autoimmune Hepatitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Young woman with hepatitis - must consider autoimmune.

allDiagnoses.name: Drug-Induced Liver Injury
allDiagnoses.rationale: Ask about medications, supplements, acetaminophen.

allDiagnoses.name: Alcoholic Hepatitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Less likely at age 27 without significant alcohol history.

allDiagnoses.name: Biliary Obstruction
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would expect more colicky pain, elevated alk phos out of proportion to transaminases.

nurse-27-needlestick.oldcartsNarrative: "I've been exhausted for about 3 weeks - can barely get through my shifts at work. I'm a nurse in the ER. Then a few days ago, my husband noticed my eyes were yellow. I've had this dull ache in my right upper belly that's constant. I don't have much appetite and feel a little nauseous. I didn't want to come in, but... I should tell you - I had a needle stick about 10 weeks ago. I know I should have reported it, but I was busy and the patient's HIV was negative, so I didn't think much of it."

historyQuestions.text: When did you start feeling sick?
historyQuestions.answer: The fatigue started about 3 weeks ago - at first I thought I was just tired from work. Then a few days ago I noticed the yellow eyes and the pain in my right side started.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prodrome of fatigue followed by jaundice is classic for acute viral hepatitis

historyQuestions.text: Tell me more about the needle stick
historyQuestions.answer: It was 10 weeks ago. Hollow needle, stuck my finger after drawing blood. The patient was negative for HIV but I never found out about hepatitis.
historyQuestions.reasoning: This is THE KEY history. 10 weeks = classic incubation for HBV. Hollow needle = high risk for transmission.

historyQuestions.text: Are you vaccinated for hepatitis B?
historyQuestions.answer: I got the series as a kid but I don't know if I ever had my titers checked.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Vaccination status crucial. If vaccinated AND immune (anti-HBs positive), protected from HBV.

historyQuestions.text: Any other exposures - IV drugs, tattoos, new sexual partners?
historyQuestions.answer: No, none of that. I've been married for 3 years, no other partners.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Rules out other transmission routes.

historyQuestions.text: Any medications, supplements, or acetaminophen use?
historyQuestions.answer: Just ibuprofen occasionally for headaches. No supplements.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Rules out DILI as cause.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Past medical history affects diagnosis and treatment.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

physicalExam.finding: Young woman appearing fatigued. Obvious scleral icterus. Skin with mild jaundice.
physicalExam.reasoning: Only 30% of acute viral hepatitis presents with jaundice - this patient is in that minority.

physicalExam.finding: Tender hepatomegaly - liver edge palpable 3cm below costal margin. No splenomegaly. No ascites.
physicalExam.reasoning: Tender hepatomegaly classic for acute hepatitis.

physicalExam.finding: No spider angiomata. No palmar erythema. No needle tracks.
physicalExam.reasoning: No stigmata of chronic liver disease - this is ACUTE.

physicalExam.finding: No lymphadenopathy
physicalExam.reasoning: Lymphadenopathy might suggest EBV, CMV, or other causes.

labs.name: Liver Function Tests
labs.result: AST 1,245, ALT 1,890 (both markedly elevated). Alk phos 145, Total bili 8.2, Direct bili 6.1
labs.reasoning: Transaminases in thousands = acute hepatocellular injury. Conjugated hyperbilirubinemia from impaired excretion.

labs.name: Albumin and PT/INR
labs.reasoning: SYNTHETIC FUNCTION preserved - this is acute, not fulminant. Albumin/PT much more important than AST/ALT for prognosis.

labs.name: Hepatitis Panel (HAV, HBV, HCV)
labs.result: HBsAg POSITIVE, HBcAb IgM POSITIVE, HBeAg POSITIVE, Anti-HBs NEGATIVE. Anti-HCV NEGATIVE. HAV IgM NEGATIVE.
labs.reasoning: Confirms ACUTE Hepatitis B. HBsAg + HBcAb IgM = acute infection. HBeAg positive = highly infectious.

labs.name: HBV DNA viral load
labs.reasoning: High viral load confirms active replication. Not essential for diagnosis but helps assess infectivity.

labs.name: HIV antibody/antigen
labs.reasoning: Always test with HBV - can be co-transmitted and affects management significantly.

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: May show mild lymphocytosis in viral hepatitis. Thrombocytopenia suggests chronic liver disease.

labs.name: Basic Metabolic Panel
labs.reasoning: Baseline renal function; hyponatremia can occur in severe hepatitis.

labs.name: Comprehensive Metabolic Panel
labs.reasoning: Full metabolic panel provides comprehensive assessment.

labs.name: GGT (Gamma-Glutamyl Transferase)
labs.reasoning: Elevated in liver disease; helps confirm hepatobiliary origin of elevated alk phos.

labs.name: LDH
labs.reasoning: Non-specific marker of tissue damage; can be elevated in hepatitis.

labs.name: Ammonia
labs.reasoning: Would be elevated in hepatic encephalopathy; normal here confirms no encephalopathy.

labs.name: Lipase
labs.reasoning: Rules out pancreatitis as cause of abdominal pain.

labs.name: EBV/CMV titers
labs.reasoning: EBV and CMV can cause hepatitis-like syndrome. Rule out if hepatitis panel negative.

labs.name: ANA, Anti-smooth muscle antibody
labs.reasoning: Would order if autoimmune hepatitis suspected; clear exposure history here makes this less likely.

labs.name: Ceruloplasmin
labs.reasoning: For Wilson's disease workup; not indicated with positive viral serologies.

labs.name: Iron studies/Ferritin
labs.reasoning: For hemochromatosis workup; not indicated with positive viral serologies.

imaging.name: RUQ Ultrasound
imaging.result: Hepatomegaly. Normal gallbladder, no stones, no biliary dilation. No ascites. No focal liver lesions.
imaging.reasoning: Rules out biliary obstruction and focal lesions. NOT diagnostic for viral hepatitis - that's serologic.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen
imaging.result: Not indicated with clear viral hepatitis diagnosis
imaging.reasoning: Does not add to ultrasound for viral hepatitis. Would consider if abscess or tumor suspected.

imaging.name: MRCP
imaging.result: Not indicated - no biliary dilation on ultrasound
imaging.reasoning: For biliary pathology workup; not needed with normal biliary tree on US.

imaging.name: Doppler of portal/hepatic veins
imaging.result: Patent hepatic and portal veins
imaging.reasoning: Would order if concern for Budd-Chiari syndrome or portal vein thrombosis.

procedures.name: Liver biopsy
procedures.result: Not indicated for acute viral hepatitis
procedures.reasoning: Biopsy rarely needed in acute viral hepatitis. Serologies are diagnostic.

procedures.name: IV access
procedures.result: Peripheral IV placed for hydration.
procedures.reasoning: For supportive care and potential medication administration.

procedures.name: Paracentesis
procedures.result: No ascites present - not indicated
procedures.reasoning: Would perform if ascites present, but not seen in acute uncomplicated hepatitis.

treatments.name: Supportive care (IV fluids, antiemetics)
treatments.result: IV NS for hydration, ondansetron PRN nausea
treatments.reasoning: Most acute HBV in adults (>95%) will clear spontaneously. Supportive care is mainstay.

treatments.name: Hold hepatotoxic medications
treatments.result: Ibuprofen held. Acetaminophen limited to <2g/day.
treatments.reasoning: Must adjust all hepatically metabolized drugs. Review all medications.

treatments.name: Strict alcohol avoidance
treatments.result: Counseled to avoid all alcohol until liver enzymes normalize
treatments.reasoning: Alcohol is hepatotoxic and can significantly worsen liver injury.

treatments.name: Antiviral therapy (tenofovir, entecavir)
treatments.result: NOT indicated for uncomplicated acute HBV
treatments.reasoning: Antivirals reserved for fulminant hepatitis, immunocompromised, or severe acute HBV. Most adults clear spontaneously.

treatments.name: Corticosteroids
treatments.result: NOT indicated - would worsen viral hepatitis
treatments.reasoning: Steroids are CONTRAINDICATED in viral hepatitis - would enhance viral replication.

treatments.name: Nutritional support
treatments.result: Low-fat diet as tolerated. Adequate protein intake.
treatments.reasoning: Maintain nutrition; avoid excessive dietary restrictions.

treatments.name: Contact and body fluid precautions
treatments.result: Standard precautions. Counsel on transmission prevention.
treatments.reasoning: Patient is HBeAg positive = highly infectious. Counsel on preventing transmission to close contacts.

consults.name: GI/Hepatology consult
consults.result: Hepatology consulted for management and follow-up planning.
consults.reasoning: Hepatology follow-up to monitor resolution and confirm clearance of infection.

consults.name: Infectious Disease consult
consults.result: Consulted for post-exposure counseling and contact tracing.
consults.reasoning: Can help with occupational exposure protocols and contact management.

consults.name: Occupational Health notification
consults.result: Occupational exposure reported per protocol.
consults.reasoning: Required reporting for needle stick injury. Ensures proper documentation and follow-up.

disposition.name: Hospital admission
disposition.result: Admit for monitoring given jaundice and significantly elevated LFTs.
disposition.reasoning: Symptomatic acute hepatitis with jaundice warrants admission for monitoring and supportive care.

disposition.name: Discharge with close follow-up
disposition.result: Consider if mild disease, stable, reliable follow-up.
disposition.reasoning: Mild acute hepatitis can be managed outpatient with very close follow-up.

disposition.name: ICU admission
disposition.result: Not needed - synthetic function preserved, no encephalopathy.
disposition.reasoning: ICU only for fulminant hepatic failure (coagulopathy, encephalopathy).

actualDiagnosis.name: Acute Hepatitis B Infection - Occupational Needle Stick
keyFindings: Needle stick exposure 10 weeks prior (classic incubation 6-24 weeks)
Jaundice, fatigue, RUQ pain, low-grade fever
HBsAg positive + HBcAb IgM positive = ACUTE HBV
teachingPoints: EXPOSURE HISTORY IS KEY: Always ask about needle sticks, blood exposures, sexual contacts, IV drug use
MOST ACUTE VIRAL HEPATITIS IS ASYMPTOMATIC: Only 30% present with jaundice
AST/ALT = hepatocellular DAMAGE (not function). ALBUMIN/PT = synthetic FUNCTION
SEROLOGY: HBsAg + HBcAb IgM = acute HBV. HBeAg = high infectivity
HBV TRANSMISSION RISK from needle stick: 5-25%
PROGNOSIS: >95% of immunocompetent adults clear acute HBV. Only 5% become chronic.
TREATMENT: Supportive for acute HBV. No antivirals needed unless fulminant.
POST-EXPOSURE PROPHYLAXIS: If exposed and NOT immune, give HBIG + start vaccine series

chronificationRisk.title: Risk of Acute → Chronic HBV by Age

evidenceBasedPearls.content: Patients with acute HBV are TWICE as likely to have symptoms compared to acute HCV.
Ultrasound not diagnostic for viral hepatitis. May show hepatomegaly, gallbladder wall thickening (50% of acute HBV), but serologies are definitive.
75-80% of acute HCV progresses to chronic HCV. No clear predictors of who will clear vs. become chronic.
HBV needle stick transmission: 5-25%. HCV needle stick transmission: 1.8%. HIV needle stick transmission: 0.3%.

guidelineReferences.name: CDC Updated Guidelines for Post-Exposure Prophylaxis 2013
guidelineReferences.citation: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. MMWR. 2013;62(RR-10):1-24
keyPoints: Test source patient for HBV, HCV, HIV immediately
HBV PEP: HBIG + vaccine series if unvaccinated
Check exposed worker's HBsAb titer - if <10, give booster
Follow-up HBV testing at 6 months post-exposure
Document exposure and provide counseling

guidelineReferences.name: AASLD Guidelines for Treatment of Chronic Hepatitis B 2018
guidelineReferences.citation: Terrault NA et al. Hepatology. 2018;67(4):1560-1599
keyPoints: Acute HBV: >95% of immunocompetent adults clear spontaneously
Treatment only indicated for acute HBV if fulminant/severe
HBsAg + anti-HBc IgM = acute infection
Monitor for chronicity: recheck HBsAg at 6 months
Vaccinate household contacts and sexual partners

nurse-27-needlestick.clinicalPearl: BOTTOM LINE: When evaluating jaundice and elevated transaminases, the EXPOSURE HISTORY is the smoking gun for viral hepatitis. Ask about needle sticks, blood exposures, IV drug use, and sexual contacts. Most acute hepatitis is asymptomatic (only 30% jaundiced). HBsAg + HBcAb IgM = acute HBV. Over 95% of immunocompetent adults will clear acute HBV spontaneously - treatment is supportive.

patientBackground.recentEvents: Patient was in usual state of health until symptoms began. No major recent life changes.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: No significant recent illnesses other than this presentation.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Otherwise healthy and active. This illness is unexpected and concerning.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Young adult with supportive family and friends. Active social life.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Generally trusts medical advice. Wants to understand the reasoning behind recommendations.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Uses healthcare appropriately. No excessive ER utilization.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the INR was 4.2?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same presentation but with coagulopathy and encephalopathy

chiefComplaint.original: My eyes and skin are turning yellow and I feel exhausted
chiefComplaint.modified: My eyes are yellow and I'm confused [patient is somnolent, has asterixis]

feedback.correct: Elevated INR + encephalopathy = ACUTE LIVER FAILURE (not just acute hepatitis). This is life-threatening. Transfer to liver transplant center immediately. N-acetylcysteine may help even in non-acetaminophen ALF.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of liver failure. What's the defining feature?
feedback.incorrect: Acute liver failure = encephalopathy + coagulopathy (INR >1.5) in patient without prior liver disease. This is NOT just 'bad hepatitis.' Mortality up to 80% without transplant. Transfer to transplant center ASAP. List for emergent transplant.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Acute hepatitis = elevated transaminases, may have jaundice, usually self-limited. Acute liver failure = adds coagulopathy + encephalopathy, life-threatening, needs transplant center.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient was a heavy drinker?
whatIfScenarios.description: Similar presentation but with AST:ALT ratio >2 and heavy alcohol history

chiefComplaint.original: My eyes and skin are turning yellow and I feel exhausted
chiefComplaint.modified: I've been drinking heavily for 20 years and my eyes are turning yellow

feedback.correct: AST:ALT >2:1 + heavy drinking = alcoholic hepatitis. Calculate Maddrey discriminant function: ≥32 = severe, consider steroids if no infection/GI bleed. Abstinence is critical for survival.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of alcoholic pattern. How do you assess severity?
feedback.incorrect: Alcoholic hepatitis: AST:ALT ratio >2:1 (often >3:1), AST usually <300-500, heavy alcohol history. Maddrey discriminant function (MDF) ≥32 = severe, 30-day mortality ~30-50%. Consider prednisolone if MDF ≥32 and no contraindications.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Viral hepatitis = AST/ALT can be thousands, AST:ALT ~1:1. Alcoholic hepatitis = AST rarely >500, AST:ALT >2:1. Different etiology, prognosis, and treatment.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there was acetaminophen overdose?
whatIfScenarios.description: Elevated transaminases with history of acetaminophen ingestion

chiefComplaint.original: My eyes and skin are turning yellow and I feel exhausted
chiefComplaint.modified: I took a lot of Tylenol for my headaches over the past few days

feedback.correct: Acetaminophen toxicity = #1 cause of acute liver failure in US. N-acetylcysteine (NAC) is antidote - give if any suspicion. Most effective within 8 hours but still beneficial up to 72+ hours. Check APAP level and calculate Rumack-Matthew nomogram.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of APAP toxicity. What's the treatment?
feedback.incorrect: Acetaminophen = most common cause of acute liver failure. NAC is the antidote - repletes glutathione. Give NAC if ANY suspicion of APAP toxicity. Effective within 8 hours, but still beneficial even >24 hours. 21-hour IV or 72-hour oral protocol.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Viral hepatitis = supportive care only. APAP toxicity = NAC is specific antidote and life-saving. Always ask about APAP/Tylenol use in any patient with elevated transaminases.

guidelineReferences.name: Tokyo Guidelines 2018 for Acute Cholecystitis
guidelineReferences.citation: Yokoe M, et al. J Hepatobiliary Pancreat Sci. 2018;25:41-54
keyPoints: Murphy's sign + fever + elevated WBC suggests diagnosis
Ultrasound is first-line imaging
Early cholecystectomy (within 72 hours) is preferred
Grade severity to guide management approach

female-38-cholecystitis.name: M. Torres
female-38-cholecystitis.chiefComplaint: Sharp pain in my upper right belly since last night
female-38-cholecystitis.variantDescription: Classic acute cholecystitis with 4 F's risk factors

missedConsequences.Acute Cholecystitis: Without surgery, cholecystitis progresses to gangrenous cholecystitis and perforation. Patient develops peritonitis and sepsis, requiring emergent open surgery with prolonged ICU stay.
missedConsequences.Ascending Cholangitis: Missed cholangitis leads to septic shock within hours. Despite antibiotics and emergent ERCP, patient develops multi-organ failure.

commonPitfalls: Confusing biliary COLIC (self-limited, <6 hours) with CHOLECYSTITIS (prolonged, with fever) - duration and fever are key
Not recognizing the 4 F's: Female, Forty, Fertile (estrogen), Fat - but don't forget males get gallstones too
Missing Charcot's triad (fever + jaundice + RUQ pain) which indicates cholangitis - a more serious condition
Forgetting to check for Murphy's sign - inspiratory arrest with RUQ palpation is highly specific for cholecystitis
Not asking about prior similar episodes - recurrent biliary colic that now presents with fever suggests progression

mcqs.question: The patient reports 2-3 prior episodes of similar pain that resolved within a few hours. This episode has lasted 14+ hours with fever. What does this pattern suggest?

options.text: The patient has a low pain tolerance and is exaggerating
options.explanation: The pattern is clinically significant, not exaggeration.

options.text: Previous episodes were biliary colic; this episode has progressed to cholecystitis
options.explanation: Biliary colic = transient stone impaction that resolves (<6 hours). Cholecystitis = persistent impaction with gallbladder inflammation (>6 hours, fever). This is the classic progression.

options.text: All episodes were cholecystitis but she didn't seek care before
options.explanation: Episodes that resolve in a few hours are colic, not cholecystitis. The distinction matters for management.

options.text: She probably has a different diagnosis this time
options.explanation: The similar trigger (fatty meal) and location suggest the same underlying disease, now progressed.

mcqs.question: You perform Murphy's sign and the patient catches her breath mid-inspiration when you palpate the RUQ. What does this indicate?

options.explanation: Murphy's sign is an objective finding, not subjective.

options.text: The inflamed gallbladder descends onto your palpating fingers during inspiration, causing pain
options.explanation: Murphy's sign: During inspiration, the diaphragm pushes the liver/gallbladder down. An inflamed gallbladder contacts the examiner's fingers, causing pain and inspiratory arrest. Highly specific for cholecystitis.
Murphy's sign localizes to gallbladder, not liver size.

options.text: The patient has a perforated ulcer
options.explanation: Perforation would cause diffuse peritonitis, not localized Murphy's sign.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Cholecystitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Classic presentation: RUQ pain after fatty meal, radiating to scapula, fever, positive Murphy's sign. Prior biliary colic episodes. Risk factors: Female, Forty, Fertile, Fat.

allDiagnoses.name: Biliary Colic
allDiagnoses.rationale: Similar presentation but typically resolves within 6 hours. This episode is prolonged (>12 hours) with fever, indicating progression to cholecystitis.

allDiagnoses.name: Hepatitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause RUQ pain. Elevated LFTs present. But pattern of pain, Murphy's sign, and ultrasound findings point to biliary disease.

allDiagnoses.name: Ascending Cholangitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: LIFE-THREATENING infection of biliary tree. Charcot's triad: fever, jaundice, RUQ pain. Must evaluate for this.

allDiagnoses.name: Liver Abscess
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would expect higher fever, more systemic illness. Ultrasound would show abscess.

allDiagnoses.name: Perforated Peptic Ulcer
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause RUQ pain. Would expect more acute onset, peritoneal signs, free air on imaging.

allDiagnoses.name: Pancreatitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Gallstones can cause pancreatitis. But pain location and normal lipase argue against this.

allDiagnoses.name: Hepatic Congestion (CHF)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause RUQ discomfort. No CHF history or findings.

female-38-cholecystitis.oldcartsNarrative: "The pain started last night, about 2 hours after dinner. We had fried chicken and fries - pretty greasy. At first it was crampy but then became sharp and constant. It's in my upper right belly and goes to my right shoulder blade. It's been about 7 out of 10, but earlier it was 9 out of 10. It hurts to take deep breaths and when you press on it. I feel nauseous and threw up once this morning. I felt feverish with chills overnight. This has actually happened before - I've had 2 or 3 episodes like this in the past year after heavy meals, but they went away on their own after a few hours. This one hasn't gone away. My mom had her gallbladder out at 45 - I'm worried it's the same thing."

historyQuestions.text: When did this start?
historyQuestions.answer: Last night, about 2 hours after dinner - so around 8 PM. It's been going on for about 14-16 hours now and hasn't gone away.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Timeline critical - pain >6 hours suggests cholecystitis rather than simple biliary colic

historyQuestions.text: When did the pain start in relation to eating?
historyQuestions.answer: About 2 hours after dinner. We had fried chicken and fries - pretty greasy food.
historyQuestions.reasoning: FATTY MEAL TRIGGER is classic for biliary disease. Fat stimulates CCK release → gallbladder contraction → stone impaction → pain.

historyQuestions.text: Does the pain go anywhere else?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, it goes to my right shoulder blade. Sometimes I feel it in my back between my shoulder blades.
historyQuestions.reasoning: REFERRED PAIN TO RIGHT SCAPULA is classic for biliary disease. Diaphragmatic irritation refers pain via phrenic nerve (C3-5).

historyQuestions.text: How long has the pain lasted?
historyQuestions.answer: It started last night around 8 PM, so it's been about 14-16 hours now and it hasn't gone away.
historyQuestions.reasoning: DURATION IS KEY: Biliary colic typically resolves in <6 hours. Pain >6 hours suggests progression to cholecystitis.

historyQuestions.text: Has this ever happened before?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, 2 or 3 times in the past year after heavy meals. But those went away on their own after a few hours. This one is different - it's lasting longer and I feel sicker.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior self-limited episodes were likely biliary colic. This prolonged episode with fever indicates progression to cholecystitis.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had fever or chills?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I felt hot and had chills overnight. I definitely feel feverish.
historyQuestions.reasoning: FEVER indicates infection/inflammation - distinguishes cholecystitis from simple biliary colic.

historyQuestions.text: Any nausea or vomiting?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I've been nauseous and threw up once this morning.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Nausea and vomiting common with biliary disease.

historyQuestions.text: Have you noticed any yellowing of your skin or eyes?
historyQuestions.answer: I don't think so. My husband didn't mention anything.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No jaundice makes cholangitis less likely. Charcot's triad (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain) suggests cholangitis.

historyQuestions.text: Any changes in urine or stool color?
historyQuestions.answer: My urine might be a little darker than usual. Stools look normal.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Dark urine/pale stools would suggest biliary obstruction with cholangitis.

historyQuestions.text: Any family history of gallbladder problems?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, my mom had her gallbladder removed when she was 45. She had gallstones.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Family history of gallstones is a risk factor.

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Just birth control pills.
historyQuestions.reasoning: OCPs slightly increase gallstone risk.

historyQuestions.text: Any allergies to medications?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies that I know of.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important before antibiotics and surgical intervention

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I've been pretty healthy. Just these episodes of belly pain after eating that I mentioned.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Confirms otherwise healthy patient; prior episodes support gallstone disease

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries. This would be my first if I need my gallbladder out.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No prior abdominal surgery; will affect surgical approach if cholecystectomy needed

historyQuestions.text: Is there any chance you could be pregnant?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I'm on the pill and we're careful. My period was normal last week.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important to rule out pregnancy before surgery and certain imaging/medications

historyQuestions.text: How much alcohol do you drink?
historyQuestions.answer: Just socially - wine with dinner on weekends, maybe one or two glasses. Nothing heavy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Alcohol use helps assess hepatitis risk; moderate use actually protects against gallstones

historyQuestions.text: Any recent weight changes?
historyQuestions.answer: Actually yes, I've lost about 20 pounds over the last 3 months on a new diet. I was pretty proud of that.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Rapid weight loss is major risk factor for gallstone formation

historyQuestions.text: When did you last eat or drink?
historyQuestions.answer: I had a few crackers this morning trying to settle my stomach but threw them up. Haven't had anything since then.
historyQuestions.reasoning: NPO status important for potential surgery

historyQuestions.text: What are you most worried about?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm worried I need surgery. My mom said getting her gallbladder out was the best decision she ever made, but surgery still scares me.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Opens discussion about likely need for cholecystectomy and patient expectations

diet_fat_intake.answer: I eat a lot of fast food. Pizza, burgers, fried stuff. I know it's not great.
diet_fat_intake.relevance: High-fat diet contributes to gallstone formation and triggers biliary colic after fatty meals.

diet_recent_meals.answer: Had pizza and wings last night for a friend's birthday. That's when it started.
diet_recent_meals.relevance: Classic trigger - high-fat meal precipitates gallbladder contraction against stones, causing pain.

diet_weight_changes.answer: I've been trying to lose weight. Dropped 20 pounds in the last few months with intermittent fasting.
diet_weight_changes.relevance: Rapid weight loss is major risk factor for gallstone formation - bile becomes supersaturated with cholesterol.

occupation_current_schedule.answer: I work in marketing. Long hours, eat at my desk. Not much time for real meals.
occupation_current_schedule.relevance: Irregular eating patterns may contribute to gallbladder stasis and stone formation.

social_lifestyle.answer: Mostly sedentary. I know I should exercise more.
social_lifestyle.relevance: Sedentary lifestyle associated with increased gallstone risk.

social_alcohol_use.answer: Social drinker. Wine with dinner, more on weekends. Nothing excessive.
social_alcohol_use.relevance: Moderate alcohol actually associated with reduced gallstone risk; not a major factor here.

reproductive_history.answer: No kids. Been on the pill for about 5 years.
reproductive_history.relevance: Nulliparity and OCP use are recognized gallstone risk factors (the '4 F's: fertile).

diet_fiber_intake.answer: Not great. I don't eat many vegetables.
diet_fiber_intake.relevance: Low fiber intake associated with increased gallstone risk.

physicalExam.finding: BP 110/68, HR 104 (tachycardic), RR 18, Temp 100.8°F (low-grade fever), SpO2 99%.
physicalExam.reasoning: Low-grade fever and tachycardia support inflammatory/infectious process.

physicalExam.finding: Mildly distressed female, prefers lying still. Not jaundiced. Not toxic-appearing.
physicalExam.reasoning: Not toxic-appearing argues against sepsis. No jaundice argues against cholangitis.

physicalExam.finding: Sclera white. No icterus.
physicalExam.reasoning: No icterus makes cholangitis less likely.

physicalExam.finding: Abdomen soft, non-distended. No scars.
physicalExam.reasoning: No distension, no prior surgical scars.

physicalExam.finding: Bowel sounds normoactive.
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal bowel sounds.

physicalExam.finding: Significant tenderness to light and deep palpation in RUQ. Voluntary guarding present. No rigidity.
physicalExam.reasoning: Localized RUQ tenderness with guarding is classic for cholecystitis.

physicalExam.finding: POSITIVE - Patient catches breath/arrests inspiration when palpating RUQ during deep inspiration.
physicalExam.reasoning: MURPHY'S SIGN is highly specific for cholecystitis. Patient stops breathing when inflamed gallbladder descends onto examiner's fingers.

physicalExam.finding: No tenderness in LUQ, LLQ, or RLQ. No rebound tenderness.
physicalExam.reasoning: Localized to RUQ - not generalized peritonitis.

physicalExam.finding: Liver span normal (approximately 10 cm in MCL). Liver edge not palpable below costal margin.
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal liver span argues against hepatomegaly/hepatitis as primary cause.

physicalExam.finding: No pulsatile mass. No widening of aorta.
physicalExam.reasoning: Rules out AAA as cause of abdominal pain.

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Leukocytosis with left shift supports acute infection/inflammation. Higher WBC concerning for complicated cholecystitis.

labs.name: CMP with LFTs
labs.result: AST 58, ALT 64 (mildly elevated), Alk Phos 150 (mildly elevated), Total bilirubin 1.8, Direct bili 0.8, Cr 0.9.
labs.reasoning: Mild LFT elevation common with cholecystitis. Significant bilirubin elevation (>4) would suggest CBD stone/cholangitis.

labs.name: Lipase
labs.reasoning: Rules out gallstone pancreatitis. Elevated lipase would change management significantly.

labs.name: Urine hCG
labs.reasoning: Rules out pregnancy before surgery and imaging.

labs.name: BMP
labs.reasoning: Assess renal function before contrast imaging and surgery.

labs.name: PT/INR, PTT
labs.reasoning: Pre-operative coagulation assessment. Would be elevated in severe liver disease.

labs.name: Type and Screen
labs.reasoning: Pre-operative preparation.

labs.name: Lactate
labs.reasoning: Would be elevated in complicated cholecystitis, gangrenous gallbladder, or sepsis.

labs.name: Amylase
labs.reasoning: Less specific than lipase for pancreatitis. Lipase is preferred.

labs.name: Procalcitonin
labs.reasoning: May help distinguish cholecystitis from cholangitis. Higher in bacterial infection.

labs.name: Blood cultures
labs.result: Drawn but typically negative in uncomplicated cholecystitis.
labs.reasoning: Consider if septic or suspected cholangitis. Often negative in simple cholecystitis.

imaging.name: RUQ Ultrasound
imaging.result: Gallstones present (multiple, largest 1.2cm). Gallbladder wall thickened (5mm, normal <3mm). Pericholecystic fluid present. POSITIVE sonographic Murphy's sign. CBD 5mm (normal <7mm).
imaging.reasoning: ULTRASOUND IS FIRST-LINE for cholecystitis. Diagnostic criteria: stones + wall thickening >3mm + pericholecystic fluid + sonographic Murphy's.

imaging.name: HIDA scan (hepatobiliary iminodiacetic acid)
imaging.result: Non-visualization of gallbladder at 4 hours = cystic duct obstruction.
imaging.reasoning: HIDA scan when ultrasound equivocal. Non-filling of gallbladder is highly sensitive/specific for acute cholecystitis.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast
imaging.result: Gallbladder wall thickening and enhancement. Pericholecystic stranding. No perforation. No abscess.
imaging.reasoning: CT less sensitive than US for stones but shows complications (perforation, abscess, gangrenous changes).

imaging.name: MRCP (MR Cholangiopancreatography)
imaging.result: No CBD stones. Gallbladder with stones and wall thickening.
imaging.reasoning: Use when concerned for choledocholithiasis (CBD stone). Better visualization of biliary tree than US.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: No free air under diaphragm. Lungs clear.
imaging.reasoning: Pre-operative baseline. Can see free air if perforation.

imaging.name: Abdominal X-ray
imaging.result: Non-specific. Only 10-15% of gallstones are radiopaque.
imaging.reasoning: Most gallstones are cholesterol and NOT visible on X-ray. US is far superior.

imaging.name: ERCP
imaging.result: NOT indicated - no evidence of CBD stone.
imaging.reasoning: ERCP for choledocholithiasis (CBD stone), not cholecystitis. Invasive with risk of pancreatitis.

procedures.name: IV access
procedures.result: 18-gauge IV placed.
procedures.reasoning: For fluids, antibiotics, and medications.

treatments.name: NPO
treatments.result: Patient made NPO for bowel rest and surgery prep.
treatments.reasoning: Bowel rest reduces gallbladder stimulation. Required for surgery.

treatments.name: IV fluids
treatments.result: Lactated Ringer's at 125 mL/hr.
treatments.reasoning: Resuscitation and hydration. Replace losses from vomiting and NPO status.

treatments.name: IV Antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam or ceftriaxone + metronidazole)
treatments.result: Piperacillin-tazobactam 3.375g IV q6h started.
treatments.reasoning: Antibiotics for infected gallbladder. Cover gram-negatives (E. coli, Klebsiella) and anaerobes.

treatments.name: IV Pain management (morphine/hydromorphone)
treatments.result: Morphine 4mg IV q4h PRN for pain.
treatments.reasoning: Appropriate pain control. Severe biliary pain requires IV opioids.

treatments.name: Antiemetics (ondansetron)
treatments.result: Ondansetron 4mg IV for nausea.
treatments.reasoning: Symptomatic relief of nausea/vomiting.

treatments.name: NSAIDs (ketorolac)
treatments.result: Ketorolac 15-30mg IV can help with biliary pain.
treatments.reasoning: NSAIDs can reduce biliary pain and inflammation. Use with opioids.

treatments.name: Oral antibiotics only
treatments.result: INSUFFICIENT for acute cholecystitis.
treatments.reasoning: IV antibiotics required for acute cholecystitis. Oral antibiotics alone would not adequately treat.

treatments.name: Proton pump inhibitor
treatments.result: Not specifically indicated for cholecystitis.
treatments.reasoning: May help if concurrent GERD but doesn't treat cholecystitis.

treatments.name: Ursodiol (bile acid dissolution therapy)
treatments.result: NOT indicated for acute cholecystitis.
treatments.reasoning: Ursodiol is for chronic gallstone management, not acute cholecystitis. Takes months to work.

treatments.name: Discharge with pain meds and follow-up
treatments.result: INAPPROPRIATE - requires admission and surgery.
treatments.reasoning: Acute cholecystitis requires admission. Cannot be managed outpatient.

consults.name: General Surgery consult - same day
consults.result: Surgery consulted. Plan for laparoscopic cholecystectomy within 24-72 hours.
consults.reasoning: Early cholecystectomy (within 72 hours) preferred. Reduces complications, length of stay, and total cost.

consults.name: GI/Hepatology consult
consults.result: Not needed for uncomplicated cholecystitis.
consults.reasoning: GI consult for ERCP if choledocholithiasis suspected. Not needed for simple cholecystitis.

consults.name: Interventional Radiology
consults.result: Consider for cholecystostomy tube if patient too ill for surgery.
consults.reasoning: Percutaneous cholecystostomy for critically ill patients who cannot undergo surgery.

disposition.name: Admit for early cholecystectomy
disposition.result: Admitted. Laparoscopic cholecystectomy performed on hospital day 2. Pathology: acute cholecystitis with cholelithiasis.
disposition.reasoning: Cholecystitis requires admission and surgery. Early cholecystectomy (within 72 hours) is standard of care.

disposition.name: Admit for IV antibiotics, delay surgery
disposition.result: May be appropriate for high-risk surgical patients.
disposition.reasoning: Delayed (interval) cholecystectomy for very high-risk patients, but early surgery preferred when possible.

disposition.name: Discharge with oral antibiotics
disposition.result: INAPPROPRIATE - requires admission and surgery.
disposition.reasoning: Acute cholecystitis requires surgical management. Outpatient treatment leads to recurrence, perforation, sepsis.

disposition.name: Discharge and schedule elective surgery
disposition.result: INAPPROPRIATE for acute cholecystitis.
disposition.reasoning: Acute cholecystitis needs admission and early surgery. Cannot safely wait for elective scheduling.

actualDiagnosis.name: Acute Calculous Cholecystitis
keyFindings: Classic presentation: RUQ pain after fatty meal, radiating to right scapula
Prolonged episode (>12 hours) - distinguishes from biliary colic (<6 hours)
POSITIVE MURPHY'S SIGN - highly specific for cholecystitis
Prior episodes of biliary colic that resolved spontaneously
Risk factors: Female, Forty, Fertile, Fat (OCP use, family history)
Ultrasound confirms: gallstones, wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, sonographic Murphy's
teachingPoints: 4 F's RISK FACTORS: Female, Forty, Fertile (estrogen), Fat. Also: Family history, Fair-skinned, Fasting (rapid weight loss).
BILIARY COLIC VS CHOLECYSTITIS: Colic = intermittent, resolves in <6 hours, no fever. Cholecystitis = prolonged (>6 hrs), fever, positive Murphy's.
MURPHY'S SIGN: Patient catches their breath when you palpate RUQ during deep inspiration. The inflamed gallbladder descends onto your fingers. Highly specific.
ULTRASOUND IS FIRST-LINE: Look for: stones, wall thickening (>3mm), pericholecystic fluid, sonographic Murphy's sign.
CHARCOT'S TRIAD MEANS CHOLANGITIS: Fever + Jaundice + RUQ pain = cholangitis (infected bile duct). More serious than cholecystitis. Reynolds' pentad adds AMS + hypotension.
EARLY SURGERY IS BETTER: Cholecystectomy within 72 hours has better outcomes than delayed surgery.
FATTY MEAL TRIGGER: Fat → CCK release → gallbladder contraction → stone impacts cystic duct → pain.

guidelineReferences.name: Tokyo Guidelines 2018: Acute Cholecystitis
guidelineReferences.citation: Yokoe M et al. J Hepatobiliary Pancreat Sci. 2018;25(1):41-54
keyPoints: Diagnosis: local signs (Murphy's, RUQ mass) + systemic inflammation + imaging
Grade I (mild): Early laparoscopic cholecystectomy within 72 hours
Grade II (moderate): Early surgery if good surgical risk
Grade III (severe): Organ dysfunction - may need percutaneous drainage first
Ultrasound is first-line imaging; HIDA scan if uncertain

guidelineReferences.name: SAGES Guidelines on Safe Cholecystectomy 2020
guidelineReferences.citation: Society of American Gastrointestinal and Endoscopic Surgeons
keyPoints: Critical View of Safety is gold standard for identifying cystic structures
Laparoscopic approach preferred in most cases
Time-out before clipping/cutting any ductal structures
Early surgery (<72h) associated with shorter hospital stay
Consider cholangiography if concern for CBD stones

closureScript.diagnosis: Ms. Torres, you have gallstones, and one of them has gotten stuck and caused your gallbladder to become inflamed and infected. This is called cholecystitis. Those episodes you had before that went away on their own were warning signs - we call that biliary colic. This time the stone stayed stuck longer and caused a bigger problem.
closureScript.plan: You're going to need surgery to remove your gallbladder. We'll do it laparoscopically - through small incisions - which means you should be able to go home in a day or two and recover pretty quickly. In the meantime, we're giving you IV fluids, antibiotics to treat the infection, and medicine for pain and nausea. The surgeon will be by to talk to you about the procedure.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Let us know right away if your pain gets much worse, if your fever goes up, or if you notice your skin or eyes turning yellow. These could mean the infection is spreading or a stone has moved into the bile duct.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: You mentioned your mom had this too - it does run in families. Can you live without your gallbladder? Absolutely. The gallbladder just stores bile, but your liver will still make it and it will just flow directly into your intestine. Most people don't notice any difference, though some people find that fatty foods cause loose stools at first.

patientBackground.recentEvents: Patient was in usual state of health until symptoms began. No major recent life changes.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: No significant recent illnesses other than this presentation.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Dealing with typical life stressors. This illness is disrupting normal activities.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Has family and friends available for support. Not socially isolated.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Generally trusts medical advice. Wants to understand the reasoning behind recommendations.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Uses healthcare appropriately. No excessive ER utilization.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient was jaundiced?
whatIfScenarios.description: RUQ pain with fever AND jaundice (Charcot's triad)

chiefComplaint.original: My right side under my ribs is really hurting since dinner last night
chiefComplaint.modified: My right side hurts, I have fever, and my eyes are turning yellow

keyDiscriminators: Charcot's triad: fever, jaundice, RUQ pain

feedback.correct: Charcot's triad (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain) = CHOLANGITIS, not just cholecystitis. The bile duct is infected. Needs ERCP for drainage, not just cholecystectomy. More urgent than cholecystitis.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of Charcot's triad. What's the treatment difference?
feedback.incorrect: Cholangitis = infected bile duct (not just gallbladder). Charcot's triad: RUQ pain + fever + jaundice. Reynolds' pentad adds: AMS + shock. Treatment: antibiotics + ERCP for biliary drainage. More serious than cholecystitis.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Cholecystitis = gallbladder infection, surgery. Cholangitis = bile duct infection, ERCP for drainage is priority. Look for jaundice and elevated bilirubin/alk phos to differentiate.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there were no stones on ultrasound?
whatIfScenarios.description: ICU patient with RUQ pain, fever, positive Murphy's but NO gallstones

chiefComplaint.original: My right side under my ribs is really hurting since dinner last night
chiefComplaint.modified: [ICU patient on pressors and TPN develops RUQ tenderness and fever, no stones on US]

feedback.correct: Acalculous cholecystitis = gallbladder inflammation WITHOUT stones. Occurs in critically ill, septic, trauma, burns, TPN patients. High mortality. May need percutaneous cholecystostomy if too sick for surgery.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of acalculous cholecystitis. Who gets this?
feedback.incorrect: No stones doesn't mean no cholecystitis! ACALCULOUS cholecystitis occurs in critically ill patients (ICU, sepsis, trauma, burns, TPN, vasopressors). Gallbladder becomes ischemic/inflamed without stone. Higher mortality than calculous. May need percutaneous drainage.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Calculous = stones cause obstruction, healthy patients. Acalculous = ischemia/stasis cause, critically ill patients, higher mortality. Treatment may differ (cholecystostomy vs cholecystectomy).

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient was septic?
whatIfScenarios.description: RUQ pain with high fever, tachycardia, hypotension, elevated lactate

chiefComplaint.original: My right side under my ribs is really hurting since dinner last night
chiefComplaint.modified: My right side has hurt for 3 days, now I feel terrible all over [patient is septic, BP 88/52]

feedback.correct: Sepsis with cholecystitis = COMPLICATED (gangrenous/perforated). Emergent surgery needed. Higher morbidity/mortality. May need open surgery instead of laparoscopic.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of complicated cholecystitis. What are the implications?
feedback.incorrect: Delayed presentation + sepsis = gangrenous or perforated cholecystitis. The gallbladder wall is necrotic. EMERGENT surgery needed. Higher conversion rate to open surgery. Much higher morbidity/mortality than uncomplicated.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Uncomplicated cholecystitis = early surgery within 72h, low risk. Complicated (gangrenous/perforated) = emergent surgery, high risk, often requires open approach.

guidelineReferences.name: ACG Nausea and Vomiting Guidelines 2001
guidelineReferences.citation: Quigley EM, et al. Am J Gastroenterol. 2001;96:3024-3031
keyPoints: Identify and treat underlying cause
Antiemetics based on etiology
Correct dehydration and electrolytes

guidelineReferences.name: ACOG Nausea and Vomiting of Pregnancy 2018
guidelineReferences.citation: ACOG Practice Bulletin No. 189. Obstet Gynecol. 2018;131:e15-e30
keyPoints: Consider pregnancy in reproductive-age women
Pyridoxine and doxylamine first-line
IV fluids and thiamine for hyperemesis

female-34-hyperemesis.name: S. Reyes
female-34-hyperemesis.chiefComplaint: I've been throwing up for two days and can't keep anything down
female-34-hyperemesis.variantDescription: Hyperemesis gravidarum - patient unaware she's pregnant
