missedConsequences.Early Pregnancy with Hyperemesis Gravida: Pregnancy not diagnosed. Patient given metoclopramide and sent home. Returns with severe dehydration, hypokalemia, and cardiac arrhythmia. Also receives CT scan exposing fetus to radiation.
missedConsequences.Small Bowel Obstruction: If this were obstruction and missed, patient would develop bowel necrosis requiring emergent surgery.
missedConsequences.Diabetic Ketoacidosis: If DKA missed, patient could progress to coma and death.

commonPitfalls: NOT GETTING A PREGNANCY TEST - the cardinal sin in any reproductive-age woman with nausea/vomiting
Assuming irregular periods means she can't be pregnant
Not recognizing that patients often don't consider pregnancy as a cause of their symptoms
Forgetting that hyperemesis causes metabolic ALKALOSIS (from vomiting H+), not acidosis
Not giving thiamine BEFORE dextrose - can precipitate Wernicke encephalopathy in malnourished patient
Missing the signs of dehydration: tachycardia, hypotension, dry mucous membranes, decreased skin turgor

mcqs.question: A 34-year-old woman presents with 2 days of vomiting. She hasn't considered pregnancy because her periods are 'irregular.' What should you do?

options.text: Respect her assessment that she's not pregnant and focus on GI workup
options.explanation: NEVER rely on patient assessment of pregnancy status. Always test.

options.text: Only test for pregnancy if she requests it
options.explanation: Pregnancy test is a medical necessity, not patient preference.

options.text: Get a pregnancy test regardless - it's mandatory in reproductive-age women with these symptoms
options.explanation: PREGNANCY TEST IS MANDATORY in any reproductive-age woman with nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, or altered mental status. Many women don't recognize early pregnancy symptoms.

options.text: Irregular periods mean pregnancy is unlikely, so focus on other causes
options.explanation: Irregular periods don't prevent pregnancy. In fact, they make it harder to track, increasing chance of unrecognized pregnancy.

mcqs.question: The patient is dehydrated with ketonuria. You're starting IV fluids. What's a critical consideration before giving dextrose-containing fluids?

options.text: Check glucose first - don't give dextrose if glucose is high
options.explanation: While true, this isn't the critical safety issue in a malnourished patient.

options.text: Give thiamine FIRST to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy
options.explanation: In malnourished or chronically vomiting patients, giving dextrose before thiamine can precipitate Wernicke encephalopathy (confusion, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia). Always give thiamine first!
Dextrose is fine in pregnancy. The concern is thiamine deficiency.

options.text: Start with normal saline only - never use dextrose in hyperemesis
options.explanation: Dextrose can be given, but thiamine must come first.

allDiagnoses.name: Early Pregnancy with Hyperemesis Gravidarum
allDiagnoses.rationale: Reproductive-age woman, irregular periods, LMP 'over a month ago,' sexually active without contraception, persistent severe nausea/vomiting. Classic presentation.

allDiagnoses.name: Viral Gastroenteritis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Common cause of acute nausea/vomiting. But no diarrhea, no sick contacts, and she had milder episodes over past month.

allDiagnoses.name: Gastritis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause nausea and epigastric discomfort. But severity and pattern suggest pregnancy.

allDiagnoses.name: Small Bowel Obstruction
allDiagnoses.rationale: Surgical emergency. Would expect distension, obstipation, colicky pain, high-pitched bowel sounds.

allDiagnoses.name: Diabetic Ketoacidosis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause severe nausea/vomiting. No diabetes history, no polyuria/polydipsia, no fruity breath.

allDiagnoses.name: Increased Intracranial Pressure
allDiagnoses.rationale: Brain tumor, bleed can cause vomiting. No headache, no vision changes, no neuro deficits.

allDiagnoses.name: Medication Side Effect
allDiagnoses.rationale: Only taking multivitamin.

allDiagnoses.name: Pancreatitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would expect more severe epigastric pain radiating to back.

allDiagnoses.name: Hepatitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would expect RUQ pain, jaundice.

female-34-hyperemesis.oldcartsNarrative: "I've been throwing up for two days and I can't keep anything down. I'm vomiting 6 or 7 times a day - mostly clear or yellow liquid since there's nothing in my stomach. No blood in it. I can't eat or drink anything without throwing it up. I feel weak and dizzy. My stomach has some mild cramping but no severe pain. I haven't had any diarrhea or fever. Actually, I've been having some mild nausea on and off for the past month but I thought it was stress or not eating well. My last period was a little over a month ago - my cycles are kind of irregular so I didn't think much of it. I'm sexually active with my fiancé and we haven't been using any birth control for the past few months. I haven't taken a pregnancy test - I didn't even think about that."

historyQuestions.text: When did this start?
historyQuestions.answer: The severe vomiting started 2 days ago and I haven't been able to keep anything down since. But actually, I've had some mild nausea on and off for the past month - this is just much worse.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Subacute nausea over weeks followed by severe vomiting is classic for pregnancy with hyperemesis

historyQuestions.text: When was your last menstrual period?
historyQuestions.answer: A little over a month ago, maybe 5-6 weeks? My cycles are kind of irregular so it's hard to keep track exactly.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL: Missed period in reproductive-age woman with nausea/vomiting = pregnancy until proven otherwise.

historyQuestions.text: Are you using any form of birth control?
historyQuestions.answer: No, we stopped using anything about 3 months ago. We're not actively trying to get pregnant but we're not preventing it either.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No contraception for 3 months = high likelihood of pregnancy. Must test.

historyQuestions.text: Have you taken a pregnancy test?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I honestly didn't even think about that. I've just been so focused on feeling sick.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Patient hasn't considered pregnancy despite obvious risk. Provider must always think of it.

historyQuestions.text: What does the vomit look like?
historyQuestions.answer: Mostly clear or yellow liquid. Sometimes foam. No blood or anything that looks like coffee grounds.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Non-bloody, non-bilious vomiting. Bilious (green) vomiting would suggest obstruction distal to ampulla.

historyQuestions.text: Any diarrhea?
historyQuestions.answer: No diarrhea at all.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No diarrhea makes gastroenteritis less likely.

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned having nausea before this episode?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, for the past month or so I've had waves of nausea, especially in the mornings. I thought it was stress or my eating habits.
historyQuestions.reasoning: MORNING NAUSEA for past month - classic 'morning sickness' that patient attributed to other causes.

historyQuestions.text: Any headaches or vision changes?
historyQuestions.answer: No headaches. Vision is fine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No neurological symptoms makes ICP elevation very unlikely.

historyQuestions.text: Describe any abdominal pain.
historyQuestions.answer: Just mild cramping in my upper stomach area. Not severe pain.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Only mild epigastric discomfort. Severe pain would suggest other diagnoses.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have diabetes?
historyQuestions.answer: No, never had any problems with blood sugar.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No diabetes history makes DKA very unlikely.

historyQuestions.text: Anyone around you been sick?
historyQuestions.answer: No, everyone at home and work has been healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No sick contacts makes infectious gastroenteritis less likely.

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Just a multivitamin. I was on birth control pills but stopped them a few months ago.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Confirms stopped contraception; no meds causing nausea

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important before any treatment

historyQuestions.text: Any medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I've been pretty healthy. Never had any surgeries or serious illnesses.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Confirms healthy patient with no prior GI disease or diabetes

historyQuestions.text: When did you last eat or drink and keep it down?
historyQuestions.answer: I had some toast yesterday morning and kept it down for about an hour. I've tried sipping water but that comes up too.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Assessing degree of dehydration and NPO status

historyQuestions.text: How often have you been urinating?
historyQuestions.answer: Not much. Maybe twice today and it was dark yellow.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Decreased dark urine indicates significant dehydration

historyQuestions.text: Any breast tenderness or changes?
historyQuestions.answer: Actually yes, they've been really tender the past couple weeks. I thought it was my period coming.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Breast tenderness is classic early pregnancy symptom

historyQuestions.text: Have you been having bowel movements?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I had one yesterday. It was normal.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Recent normal bowel movement argues against obstruction

diet_recent_food.answer: Nothing unusual. But I can't keep anything down. Even water comes back up.
diet_recent_food.relevance: No obvious food trigger; severity of symptoms (can't keep water down) indicates significant pathology requiring workup.

occupation_current_workplace.answer: Receptionist at a law firm. Been having to leave work because of this.
occupation_current_workplace.relevance: Work impact indicates severity; desk job with no obvious occupational triggers.

occupation_chemical_exposures.answer: No chemicals. Just a regular office.
occupation_chemical_exposures.relevance: No toxic exposures to explain symptoms.

social_stress_factors.answer: Work has been stressful. Also some relationship stuff I'm dealing with.
social_stress_factors.relevance: Stress can contribute to GI symptoms but doesn't explain this severity.

housing_water_source.relevance: No water contamination to explain symptoms.

social_substance_use.answer: I don't smoke. Had some wine last weekend but nothing excessive.
social_substance_use.relevance: Low substance use; note alcohol consumption - relevant if patient is pregnant.

reproductive_contraception.answer: I'm on the pill... though I might have missed a few this month with everything going on.
reproductive_contraception.relevance: Missed OCPs means pregnancy must be ruled out - critical for differential given nausea/vomiting in reproductive-age female.

diet_eating_patterns.answer: I've been trying to eat but nothing stays down. This started maybe 2 weeks ago.
diet_eating_patterns.relevance: 2-week duration aligns with early pregnancy timeline; persistent symptoms require workup.

physicalExam.finding: BP 100/64 (borderline low), HR 108 (tachycardic), RR 18, Temp 99.1°F, SpO2 99%.
physicalExam.reasoning: Tachycardia and borderline low BP suggest dehydration from persistent vomiting.

physicalExam.finding: Fatigued-appearing, mildly dehydrated. No acute distress.
physicalExam.reasoning: Dehydration evident from appearance.

physicalExam.finding: Dry mucous membranes. Decreased skin turgor. Sunken eyes.
physicalExam.reasoning: Clinical signs of dehydration from persistent vomiting.

physicalExam.finding: Abdomen flat, soft, not distended. No visible peristalsis.
physicalExam.reasoning: Non-distended abdomen argues strongly against bowel obstruction.

physicalExam.finding: Bowel sounds present, slightly hypoactive. No high-pitched or tinkling sounds.
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal-to-hypoactive bowel sounds. High-pitched sounds would suggest obstruction.

physicalExam.finding: Mild epigastric tenderness. No rebound, no guarding. No masses. No distension.
physicalExam.reasoning: Benign abdominal exam rules out surgical abdomen.

physicalExam.finding: No papilledema. Normal disc margins.
physicalExam.reasoning: No papilledema rules out elevated ICP.

physicalExam.finding: No fruity or acetone odor on breath.
physicalExam.reasoning: No fruity breath makes DKA very unlikely.

physicalExam.finding: Alert and oriented x3. No focal deficits.
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal neuro exam argues against intracranial pathology.

labs.name: Urine pregnancy test
labs.reasoning: MANDATORY FIRST TEST: Patient is pregnant. This explains the nausea/vomiting. Never skip this in reproductive-age women!

labs.name: Quantitative serum β-hCG
labs.reasoning: Confirms pregnancy and estimates gestational age. High levels associated with severe nausea. Helps assess viability with ultrasound.

labs.name: BMP
labs.result: Na 132 (low), K 3.0 (low), Cl 92, CO2 30 (high), BUN 22, Cr 1.0, Glucose 78.
labs.reasoning: Classic METABOLIC ALKALOSIS from vomiting (losing H+). Hyponatremia, hypokalemia, hypochloremia. Requires correction.

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Hemoconcentration from dehydration. Would expect elevated hematocrit. WBC helps rule out infection.

labs.name: Urinalysis
labs.result: Specific gravity 1.030 (concentrated), ketones 2+, no WBC, no bacteria.
labs.reasoning: Concentrated urine with KETONURIA indicates dehydration and starvation ketosis. Ketones guide need for admission.

labs.name: TSH and Free T4
labs.result: TSH: 0.3 mIU/L (low), Free T4: 1.5 ng/dL (slightly elevated).
labs.reasoning: Transient 'gestational thyrotoxicosis' occurs in early pregnancy (hCG cross-reacts with TSH receptor). Usually resolves without treatment.

labs.name: Liver Function Tests
labs.result: AST 45, ALT 52 (mildly elevated), Alk Phos 85, T.Bili 0.8.
labs.reasoning: Mild transaminase elevation can occur with severe hyperemesis. Marked elevation would suggest other pathology.

labs.name: Lipase
labs.reasoning: Rules out pancreatitis as cause of nausea/vomiting.

labs.name: Blood glucose
labs.reasoning: Assess for hypoglycemia from poor intake. Also rules out DKA if hyperglycemic.

labs.name: Magnesium level
labs.reasoning: Can be depleted with prolonged vomiting. Replete if low.

labs.name: Phosphorus level
labs.reasoning: Can be depleted. Monitor during refeeding.

labs.name: Arterial Blood Gas
labs.reasoning: Confirms metabolic alkalosis from vomiting. BMP usually sufficient.

labs.name: CT Head
labs.reasoning: No headache, no focal deficits, no papilledema. Radiation exposure should be avoided in pregnancy if not indicated.

imaging.name: Pelvic/Transvaginal ultrasound
imaging.result: Intrauterine pregnancy, single fetus, gestational sac with yolk sac. Estimated 6 weeks gestation. Fetal cardiac activity present.
imaging.reasoning: Confirms INTRAUTERINE pregnancy (rules out ectopic). Documents viability. No radiation exposure.

imaging.name: Abdominal X-ray
imaging.result: AVOID in pregnancy unless absolutely necessary.
imaging.reasoning: Radiation exposure to fetus. Ultrasound is preferred. X-ray only if obstruction strongly suspected.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen
imaging.result: AVOID - radiation exposure to fetus.
imaging.reasoning: CT delivers significant radiation to fetus. Should not be done for suspected hyperemesis. Use ultrasound or MRI if imaging needed.

imaging.name: RUQ Ultrasound
imaging.result: Gallbladder normal. No stones.
imaging.reasoning: Could consider if RUQ pain or concern for biliary disease.

procedures.name: IV access
procedures.result: 20-gauge IV placed for fluid resuscitation.
procedures.reasoning: Required for IV fluid and medication administration.

treatments.name: Thiamine 100mg IV FIRST
treatments.result: Thiamine 100mg IV given BEFORE any dextrose-containing fluids.
treatments.reasoning: CRITICAL: Give thiamine BEFORE dextrose to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy in malnourished/starving patients. This is a safety imperative.

treatments.name: IV fluid resuscitation
treatments.result: 2L NS bolused, then D5 1/2NS + 20 mEq KCl/L at 150 mL/hr.
treatments.reasoning: Aggressive rehydration is first-line treatment. Start with NS, add dextrose once thiamine given. Replace potassium.

treatments.name: Potassium replacement
treatments.result: KCl 20 mEq in each liter of fluids. Recheck K+ in 4 hours.
treatments.reasoning: Hypokalemia from vomiting must be corrected. Can cause arrhythmias if severe.

treatments.name: Ondansetron (Zofran) 4mg IV
treatments.result: Ondansetron 4mg IV q8h. Effective for nausea control.
treatments.reasoning: First-line antiemetic for hyperemesis. Generally considered safe in pregnancy. Discuss minimal risks.

treatments.name: Promethazine (Phenergan) 25mg IV/IM
treatments.result: Can add if ondansetron insufficient.
treatments.reasoning: Second-line antiemetic. Causes sedation which can be helpful for severe cases.

treatments.name: Metoclopramide (Reglan) 10mg IV
treatments.result: Alternative antiemetic with prokinetic effects.
treatments.reasoning: Prokinetic agent. Risk of extrapyramidal symptoms.

treatments.name: Pyridoxine (Vitamin B6) 25mg TID
treatments.result: Started for ongoing nausea management.
treatments.reasoning: First-line OUTPATIENT treatment for nausea of pregnancy. Safe and effective. Continue after discharge.

treatments.name: Doxylamine + Pyridoxine (Diclegis/Bonjesta)
treatments.result: Prescription for outpatient use.
treatments.reasoning: FDA-approved for nausea/vomiting of pregnancy. Combination of B6 and antihistamine.

treatments.name: Ginger supplements
treatments.result: Discussed for adjunctive outpatient use.
treatments.reasoning: Some evidence for mild pregnancy nausea. Non-pharmacologic option.

treatments.name: Corticosteroids (methylprednisolone)
treatments.result: Reserved for refractory cases not responding to standard treatment.
treatments.reasoning: Last-line treatment for severe refractory hyperemesis. Avoid in first trimester if possible.

treatments.name: Proton pump inhibitor
treatments.result: Can add if GERD component suspected.
treatments.reasoning: GERD common in pregnancy. PPIs generally safe.

treatments.name: Opioid pain medication
treatments.result: NOT indicated - this is nausea, not pain.
treatments.reasoning: Opioids cause nausea and are not indicated for hyperemesis. Would worsen symptoms.

consults.name: OB/GYN consult
consults.result: OB consulted for new pregnancy diagnosis and hyperemesis management. Prenatal care established.
consults.reasoning: New pregnancy diagnosis requires OB involvement. Severe hyperemesis may need ongoing specialized management.

consults.name: Nutrition/Dietitian consult
consults.result: Dietary counseling for small frequent meals, avoiding triggers.
consults.reasoning: Dietary modifications can help with ongoing nausea.

consults.name: GI consult
consults.result: Not needed - diagnosis is clear.
consults.reasoning: Hyperemesis gravidarum is an OB diagnosis. GI consult not needed unless other pathology suspected.

disposition.name: Admit for IV hydration and monitoring
disposition.result: Admitted overnight. Ketonuria cleared by morning. Tolerating oral intake. Discharged with prenatal vitamins, pyridoxine/doxylamine, and OB follow-up in 1 week.
disposition.reasoning: Severe hyperemesis with ketonuria and electrolyte abnormalities requires admission for IV repletion and monitoring.

disposition.name: Observation unit for IV fluids
disposition.result: May be appropriate for moderate cases without severe electrolyte abnormalities.
disposition.reasoning: Short observation with IV fluids and reassessment can work for less severe cases.

disposition.name: Discharge with oral fluids and antiemetics
disposition.result: Not appropriate given severity of dehydration and ketonuria.
disposition.reasoning: Patient too dehydrated for outpatient management. Ketonuria indicates need for IV repletion.

disposition.name: Discharge without pregnancy test
disposition.result: DANGEROUS - would miss the diagnosis entirely.
disposition.reasoning: NEVER discharge reproductive-age woman with nausea/vomiting without pregnancy test. This is the diagnosis!

actualDiagnosis.name: Hyperemesis Gravidarum (Early Pregnancy)
keyFindings: Missed period (LMP 5-6 weeks ago) with irregular cycles
Sexually active without contraception for 3 months
Dehydration: tachycardia, dry mucous membranes, decreased skin turgor
Labs: hyponatremia, hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis, ketonuria - classic for severe vomiting
Pelvic US confirms viable intrauterine pregnancy at 6 weeks
teachingPoints: PREGNANCY TEST IN ALL REPRODUCTIVE-AGE WOMEN: Cannot emphasize enough. Nausea/vomiting in young woman = pregnancy until proven otherwise.
HYPEREMESIS VS MORNING SICKNESS: Morning sickness is common and mild. Hyperemesis = severe nausea/vomiting with dehydration, ketonuria, electrolyte abnormalities, and weight loss >5%.
ELECTROLYTE PATTERN: Vomiting causes hyponatremia, hypokalemia, hypochloremia, and metabolic alkalosis (loss of H+ and Cl- in gastric contents).
KETONURIA INDICATES SEVERITY: Ketones in urine mean the body is breaking down fat due to starvation. Indicates need for IV therapy.
THIAMINE BEFORE DEXTROSE: In malnourished patients, giving dextrose without thiamine can precipitate Wernicke encephalopathy. Always give thiamine first.
β-hCG AND SYMPTOMS: Higher hCG levels (twins, molar pregnancy) associated with more severe nausea. Very high hCG warrants molar pregnancy consideration.
PATIENT EDUCATION: Many women don't consider pregnancy as cause of their symptoms. Always maintain high index of suspicion.

guidelineReferences.name: ACOG Practice Bulletin: Nausea and Vomiting of Pregnancy
guidelineReferences.citation: ACOG Practice Bulletin No. 189. Obstet Gynecol. 2018;131(1):e15-e30
keyPoints: Hyperemesis gravidarum: persistent vomiting with >5% weight loss, ketonuria, electrolyte abnormalities
First-line treatment: vitamin B6 (pyridoxine) ± doxylamine
Severe cases: IV fluid replacement, thiamine supplementation, antiemetics
Consider molar pregnancy if β-hCG disproportionately elevated
Enteral or parenteral nutrition if oral intake inadequate despite treatment

guidelineReferences.name: Society of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists of Canada (SOGC) Guideline
guidelineReferences.citation: Campbell K et al. J Obstet Gynaecol Can. 2016;38(12):1127-1137
keyPoints: PUQE score (Pregnancy-Unique Quantification of Emesis) for severity assessment
Thiamine 100mg IV before IV dextrose to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy
Ondansetron safe after first trimester; use cautiously in first trimester
Hospitalization if: severe dehydration, ketonuria, electrolyte abnormalities, weight loss >5%
Consider corticosteroids (methylprednisolone) for refractory cases

closureScript.diagnosis: Ms. Reyes, I have some surprising news - you're pregnant! That's actually what's causing all this vomiting. The pregnancy test was positive, and the ultrasound shows you're about 6 weeks along. The severe nausea and vomiting you're experiencing is called hyperemesis gravidarum - it's like extreme morning sickness.
closureScript.plan: We're going to admit you to the hospital for at least overnight, maybe a couple of days. You're very dehydrated from all the vomiting, and your body is running on fumes. We need to get fluids into you through an IV, give you vitamins including one called thiamine that's important for your brain, and medications to help with the nausea. Once we get you hydrated and the vomiting under control, you'll start feeling much better.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: The nausea will probably continue for the first trimester, but we'll give you medications you can take at home to manage it. Come back if you can't keep anything down for 24 hours, if you feel dizzy or faint, or if you see any blood when you vomit.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know this is a lot to process - you didn't even know you were pregnant, and now you're in the hospital. Take a deep breath. Hyperemesis is miserable but it's treatable, and it doesn't hurt the baby. In fact, some studies show that women with more nausea have lower miscarriage rates. Would you like me to call anyone for you while you take this in?

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the β-hCG was extremely high with an abnormal ultrasound?
whatIfScenarios.description: β-hCG 250,000 with 'snowstorm' appearance on ultrasound, no fetus visualized

chiefComplaint.original: I can't stop throwing up
chiefComplaint.modified: Extreme nausea, β-hCG is 250,000, ultrasound shows no fetus - just a 'grape-like' mass

feedback.correct: Molar pregnancy: abnormal placental proliferation, very high β-hCG, 'snowstorm' ultrasound, severe hyperemesis (due to high hCG). Needs D&C/suction evacuation, close hCG monitoring (risk of malignancy), contraception for 6-12 months. GYN oncology referral.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of molar pregnancy. What's the follow-up concern?
feedback.incorrect: Very high β-hCG + 'snowstorm' ultrasound = MOLAR PREGNANCY. Treatment: suction evacuation. Critical: serial β-hCG monitoring afterward - molar pregnancy can become malignant (choriocarcinoma). Avoid pregnancy for 6-12 months during monitoring. GYN oncology involvement.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Normal pregnancy = expected β-hCG, visible fetus. Molar pregnancy = sky-high β-hCG, no fetus, snowstorm US. Molar pregnancy has malignant potential and needs specialized follow-up.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient developed confusion and ataxia?
whatIfScenarios.description: Prolonged hyperemesis now with altered mental status, nystagmus, gait instability

chiefComplaint.original: I can't stop throwing up
chiefComplaint.modified: I've been vomiting for weeks, now I'm confused, my eyes are jerking, and I can't walk straight

feedback.correct: Wernicke encephalopathy = confusion + ataxia + ophthalmoplegia (nystagmus). From thiamine (B1) deficiency in malnourished patients. GIVE THIAMINE BEFORE DEXTROSE - glucose without thiamine worsens it. IV thiamine 500mg TID for 3 days. Reversible if treated early.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of Wernicke's. What's the critical treatment order?
feedback.incorrect: Wernicke encephalopathy: classic triad = confusion, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia. Caused by thiamine (B1) deficiency from malnutrition. CRITICAL: give thiamine BEFORE dextrose - glucose metabolism requires thiamine and will deplete remaining stores. Untreated → Korsakoff syndrome (irreversible amnesia).

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Simple hyperemesis = IV fluids with dextrose. Prolonged/severe = always give THIAMINE FIRST before any dextrose. Prevent Wernicke's rather than treat it.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the pregnancy test was negative?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same severe vomiting in young woman but negative pregnancy test

chiefComplaint.original: I can't stop throwing up
chiefComplaint.modified: I've been vomiting for 2 weeks. Pregnancy test is NEGATIVE.

expectedReasoning.topDiagnosis: GI or CNS cause of vomiting

feedback.correct: Not pregnant = NOT hyperemesis. Consider: GI (obstruction, gastroparesis, pancreatitis), CNS (increased ICP, vestibular), metabolic (DKA, adrenal insufficiency), cyclical vomiting syndrome. Workup: KUB/CT, amylase/lipase, consider brain imaging if headache/neuro signs.
feedback.partial: Good differential without pregnancy. What workup is needed?
feedback.incorrect: Persistent vomiting in non-pregnant patient: GI causes (obstruction, gastroparesis, pancreatitis), CNS (increased ICP, posterior fossa lesion), metabolic (DKA, uremia, adrenal insufficiency), cyclical vomiting syndrome. Workup depends on associated symptoms - abdominal imaging, labs, possibly brain imaging.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Reproductive-age woman with vomiting = pregnancy test FIRST. If negative, pursue other differential. Don't assume nausea/vomiting is always GI - can be CNS, metabolic, or other causes.

It happens almost every day after dinner and gets worse when I lie down. Tums help temporarily. I've been eating dinner late - around 9pm - then going to bed around 10:30. I drink coffee in the morning and wine with dinner."

diet_eating_timing.answer: I eat dinner around 9pm most nights because I work late. Then bed by 10:30.

social_alcohol_use.answer: Glass or two of wine with dinner, maybe 4-5 nights a week.
social_alcohol_use.relevance: Alcohol relaxes LES and stimulates acid production.

occupation_current_stress.answer: Project manager. Deadlines constant. Eating at my desk.

housing_sleep_setup.answer: I sleep flat, no extra pillows.
housing_sleep_setup.relevance: Elevating head of bed can reduce nocturnal reflux.

physicalExam.reasoning: Normal vitals. Mildly overweight.

physicalExam.finding: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Mild epigastric tenderness.
physicalExam.reasoning: Benign exam with mild epigastric tenderness common in GERD.

labs.name: H. pylori testing (stool antigen or breath test)
labs.reasoning: H. pylori can cause or worsen dyspepsia. Test-and-treat strategy recommended if positive. Stool antigen preferred over serology (serology stays positive after eradication).

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Rules out anemia from occult GI bleeding. Iron deficiency anemia would be an alarm symptom requiring EGD.

labs.name: Iron studies
labs.result: Not indicated - no anemia or alarm symptoms
labs.reasoning: Would only check if CBC showed anemia. Iron deficiency suggests chronic blood loss.

imaging.name: No imaging routinely indicated
imaging.result: GERD is a clinical diagnosis based on typical symptoms
imaging.reasoning: Typical GERD without alarm symptoms does not require imaging. Empiric PPI trial is both diagnostic and therapeutic.

imaging.name: Barium swallow
imaging.result: Not indicated for typical GERD
imaging.reasoning: Poor sensitivity for GERD. May show hiatal hernia but doesn't change management. Reserved for dysphagia evaluation.

procedures.name: Upper Endoscopy (EGD)
procedures.result: NOT INDICATED - no alarm symptoms present
procedures.reasoning: EGD indications: Alarm symptoms (dysphagia, odynophagia, weight loss, GI bleeding, anemia), age >60 with new symptoms, failure of PPI trial, screening for Barrett's in high-risk patients.

procedures.name: Ambulatory pH monitoring
procedures.result: Not indicated for typical GERD
procedures.reasoning: Reserved for atypical symptoms, refractory cases, or pre-surgical evaluation. Confirms acid exposure time.

procedures.name: Esophageal manometry
procedures.result: Not indicated
procedures.reasoning: Pre-operative test before fundoplication. Evaluates esophageal motility.

treatments.name: Lifestyle modifications
treatments.result: Counseled extensively on lifestyle changes as FIRST-LINE therapy
treatments.reasoning: EVIDENCE-BASED lifestyle changes: (1) Elevate head of bed 6-8 inches, (2) Avoid eating 2-3 hours before lying down, (3) Weight loss if overweight, (4) Avoid trigger foods (alcohol, caffeine, chocolate, fatty foods, mint), (5) Smoking cessation, (6) Avoid tight-fitting clothing.

treatments.name: Proton Pump Inhibitor (omeprazole 20mg daily)
treatments.result: Omeprazole 20mg once daily before breakfast x 8 weeks prescribed
treatments.reasoning: PPI is first-line pharmacotherapy. Take 30-60 minutes before first meal for maximum efficacy. 8-week trial is both diagnostic and therapeutic - symptom resolution confirms diagnosis.

treatments.name: H2 receptor antagonist (famotidine)
treatments.result: Alternative option discussed - less potent than PPI
treatments.reasoning: H2 blockers are less effective than PPIs but useful for intermittent symptoms, on-demand use, or nocturnal breakthrough on PPI.

treatments.name: Antacids (Tums, Maalox)
treatments.result: For breakthrough symptoms only
treatments.reasoning: Rapid but short-duration relief. Good for occasional breakthrough symptoms but not maintenance therapy.

treatments.name: NSAID avoidance counseling
treatments.result: Counseled to avoid NSAIDs which worsen symptoms
treatments.reasoning: NSAIDs impair mucosal protection and worsen reflux symptoms. Use acetaminophen for pain if needed.

consults.name: GI referral not indicated
consults.result: Primary care management appropriate for uncomplicated GERD
consults.reasoning: Refer to GI for: refractory symptoms despite 8 weeks PPI, alarm symptoms, need for EGD, consideration of anti-reflux surgery.

disposition.name: Outpatient management with follow-up
disposition.result: Follow up in 8 weeks to assess PPI response. If symptoms resolve, attempt to step down to lowest effective dose or on-demand therapy.
disposition.reasoning: Most GERD is managed outpatient. Reassess at 8 weeks - good response confirms diagnosis. Poor response requires further workup.

actualDiagnosis.name: Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD)
keyFindings: No alarm symptoms (no dysphagia, weight loss, bleeding, anemia)
Lifestyle factors: late eating, alcohol, eating before bed
teachingPoints: LIFESTYLE MODIFICATION IS FIRST-LINE: Elevate head of bed 6-8 inches, avoid eating 2-3 hours before lying down, weight loss, avoid triggers (alcohol, caffeine, fatty foods).
PPI TRIAL IS DIAGNOSTIC AND THERAPEUTIC: 8-week trial of once-daily PPI before breakfast. Symptom resolution confirms diagnosis.
ALARM SYMPTOMS REQUIRE EGD: Dysphagia, odynophagia, weight loss, GI bleeding, anemia, new symptoms age >60.
H. PYLORI TESTING: Consider stool antigen test. Treat if positive (triple therapy). Don't use serology (stays positive after treatment).
STEP-DOWN THERAPY: After symptom control, attempt lowest effective PPI dose or on-demand therapy to minimize long-term PPI risks.
LONG-TERM PPI RISKS: Discuss C. diff infection, bone fractures, hypomagnesemia, B12 deficiency with prolonged use - but benefits often outweigh risks.

guidelineReferences.name: ACG Clinical Guideline: Diagnosis and Management of GERD 2022
guidelineReferences.citation: Katz PO et al. Am J Gastroenterol. 2022;117(1):27-56
keyPoints: Empiric PPI trial is first-line for typical symptoms without alarm features
EGD if alarm symptoms, inadequate PPI response, or Barrett's screening
Lifestyle modifications: weight loss, elevate HOB, avoid eating 2-3h before bed
8-week PPI course; then step down to lowest effective dose
H. pylori testing reasonable in dyspepsia but not required in typical GERD

guidelineReferences.name: AGA Clinical Practice Update on PPI Therapy 2022
guidelineReferences.citation: Freedberg DE et al. Gastroenterology. 2022;163(5):1152-1161
keyPoints: Use lowest effective PPI dose
Long-term PPI risks are small but consider in decision-making
Don't stop PPIs solely due to concerns about adverse effects if indicated
On-demand therapy appropriate for mild, intermittent symptoms
Annual reassessment of need for continued PPI therapy

closureScript.diagnosis: This is most likely GERD - acid from your stomach coming back up into your esophagus.
closureScript.plan: I'm starting omeprazole. But lifestyle changes are key: eat dinner earlier, elevate head of bed, cut back on wine.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Return if you develop trouble swallowing, weight loss, or blood in your stool.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: GERD is very common and treatable with lifestyle changes and medication.

Antacid response is expected in GERD.

allDiagnoses.name: Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD)

female-42-classic.oldcartsNarrative: "This burning feeling started maybe 4-5 months ago. It's right here in my chest, behind my breastbone. I'd say it's about a 4 or 5 out of 10 usually. It definitely gets worse after I eat, especially dinner - we tend to eat late. Lying down at night makes it worse too. Tums help for a little while, maybe 30 minutes. I've been taking them almost every day now. I also get this sour taste in my mouth sometimes, especially in the morning. No trouble swallowing, no vomiting, weight's been stable."

historyQuestions.text: When did this start?
historyQuestions.answer: Maybe 4-5 months ago. It started gradually but it's been getting more frequent. Now it's almost every day.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Chronic progressive symptoms over months establishes GERD pattern

historyQuestions.text: Any difficulty swallowing food or pills?
historyQuestions.answer: No, swallowing is fine. Food goes down normally.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Dysphagia is an alarm symptom requiring endoscopy. Absence is reassuring.

historyQuestions.text: Any unintentional weight loss?
historyQuestions.answer: No, actually I've gained a few pounds. That's part of the problem probably.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Unintentional weight loss is alarm symptom. Weight gain actually supports lifestyle-related GERD.

historyQuestions.text: Do you ever get food or liquid coming back up?
historyQuestions.answer: Sometimes I get that sour taste in my mouth, especially if I bend over after eating. Once or twice actual liquid came up.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Regurgitation is classic GERD symptom, helps distinguish from cardiac causes.

historyQuestions.text: Does this ever wake you up at night?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, sometimes I wake up with that burning feeling. I've started sleeping on extra pillows.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Nocturnal symptoms indicate more severe GERD and may warrant more aggressive treatment.

historyQuestions.text: Do you take any anti-inflammatory medications like ibuprofen?
historyQuestions.answer: I take Advil for headaches, maybe 2-3 times a week.
historyQuestions.reasoning: NSAID use can cause both gastritis and ulcers; important to identify and address.

historyQuestions.text: What time do you usually eat dinner?
historyQuestions.answer: Late - usually 8 or 9 PM. We both work late. Then I'm in bed by 10:30.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Eating close to bedtime worsens reflux. Key modifiable factor.

historyQuestions.text: Are there specific foods that make it worse?
historyQuestions.answer: Coffee definitely. Wine too. And tomato sauce - we eat a lot of Italian food.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Patient identifies triggers - important for lifestyle counseling.

historyQuestions.text: Do you smoke or drink alcohol?
historyQuestions.answer: I don't smoke. I have wine with dinner most nights, maybe 1-2 glasses.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Alcohol relaxes LES and is modifiable risk factor.

historyQuestions.text: Have you tried anything other than Tums?
historyQuestions.answer: I bought some Prilosec over the counter last month. It helped a lot but I stopped after 2 weeks.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Response to PPI supports acid-related diagnosis.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

diet_eating_patterns.answer: We eat dinner late because of work schedules. Big meals, then I'm on the couch watching TV.
diet_eating_patterns.relevance: Late large meals with recumbency shortly after is classic GERD-promoting behavior pattern.

diet_trigger_foods.answer: Coffee every morning, wine with dinner. Love spicy food and tomato-based dishes.
diet_trigger_foods.relevance: Multiple dietary triggers identified - coffee, alcohol, acidic foods all worsen GERD.

occupation_current_job.answer: Office manager. Desk job, stressful. I eat lunch at my desk most days.
occupation_current_job.relevance: Sedentary work and stressed eating contribute to weight gain and poor eating habits.

occupation_work_stress.answer: Very stressful. Always putting out fires. I eat fast when I'm stressed.
occupation_work_stress.relevance: Stress eating and rapid consumption can worsen reflux symptoms.

exercise_activity_level.answer: Not much exercise. I'm tired after work. Maybe a walk on weekends.
exercise_activity_level.relevance: Sedentary lifestyle contributes to weight gain, which worsens GERD.

social_sleep_position.answer: I sleep flat usually. Started propping up on pillows but it's uncomfortable.
social_sleep_position.relevance: Sleep positioning matters - head of bed elevation is evidence-based intervention.

clothing_habits.answer: I wear tight pants for work. They've been getting tighter lately.
clothing_habits.relevance: Tight clothing increases intra-abdominal pressure and can worsen reflux.

social_weight_history.answer: I've gained about 15 pounds over the past year. The symptoms started after the weight gain.
social_weight_history.relevance: Weight gain temporally correlated with symptom onset - weight loss is most evidence-based intervention.

labs.result: WBC 7.2, Hgb 13.8, Hct 41%, MCV 88, Plt 245 - all normal. No anemia.

procedures.name: Upper Endoscopy (EGD)
procedures.result: Not indicated at this time - no alarm symptoms. Would perform if refractory to 8 weeks PPI or alarm symptoms develop.
procedures.reasoning: EGD indicated for: (1) Alarm symptoms - dysphagia, odynophagia, weight loss, anemia, GI bleeding, persistent vomiting; (2) Refractory GERD after 8-week PPI trial; (3) Barrett's screening in high-risk patients (chronic GERD >5 years, male, >50, obesity, Caucasian, hiatal hernia, family history).

procedures.name: Ambulatory pH Monitoring (if diagnosis unclear)
procedures.result: Not needed - classic symptoms responding to PPI confirms GERD.
procedures.reasoning: Gold standard for GERD diagnosis when uncertain. Wireless Bravo or catheter-based. Consider before anti-reflux surgery or if PPI-refractory.

procedures.name: Esophageal Manometry
procedures.result: Not indicated - would use if surgery planned or motility disorder suspected.
procedures.reasoning: Required before fundoplication to ensure adequate esophageal motility. Also evaluates for achalasia or other motility disorders.

treatments.name: Proton Pump Inhibitor - Omeprazole 20mg daily
treatments.result: Started omeprazole 20mg daily before breakfast for 8 weeks. Take 30-60 minutes before first meal.
treatments.reasoning: PPIs are first-line pharmacotherapy for GERD. Once-daily dosing before breakfast is standard. 8-week trial, then reassess. If inadequate, can increase to BID.

treatments.name: Weight Loss Counseling
treatments.result: Discussed weight loss as strongest evidence-based lifestyle intervention. Goal 5-10% body weight loss. Referral to dietitian offered.
treatments.reasoning: STRONGEST evidence for lifestyle intervention. Weight loss reduces abdominal pressure and GERD symptoms. Even modest weight loss (5-10 lbs) can improve symptoms.

treatments.name: Meal Timing Modification
treatments.result: Counseled to eat dinner at least 3 hours before lying down. Avoid late-night eating.
treatments.reasoning: Eating close to bedtime worsens nocturnal reflux. Recommend dinner by 7 PM if bedtime is 10 PM.

treatments.name: Head of Bed Elevation
treatments.result: Instructed to elevate head of bed 6-8 inches with blocks or wedge pillow. Extra pillows are not as effective.
treatments.reasoning: Elevating head of bed (not just pillows) reduces nocturnal reflux. Use bed risers or foam wedge under mattress.

treatments.name: Trigger Avoidance
treatments.result: Counseled to reduce coffee, alcohol, tomato products. Avoid eating 3 hours before bed. Loose-fitting clothes.
treatments.reasoning: Common triggers include coffee, alcohol, chocolate, mint, citrus, tomato, fatty/spicy foods. Evidence for trigger avoidance is weaker than weight loss but reasonable.

treatments.name: H2 Blocker PRN (optional)
treatments.result: May use famotidine 20mg for breakthrough symptoms or before trigger meals.
treatments.reasoning: H2 blockers work faster than PPIs for acute symptoms. Can be used PRN or at bedtime for nocturnal breakthrough.

treatments.name: Antacids PRN
treatments.result: Tums or Maalox PRN for breakthrough symptoms.
treatments.reasoning: Antacids provide immediate relief for breakthrough symptoms but don't treat underlying disease.

treatments.name: Discontinue/Minimize NSAIDs
treatments.result: Counseled to stop regular ibuprofen. Use acetaminophen for headaches instead.
treatments.reasoning: NSAIDs cause gastritis and ulcers. If must use, take with PPI. Acetaminophen is safer for GI.

consults.name: Gastroenterology Referral
consults.result: Not needed for initial management. Refer if refractory to 8-week PPI, alarm symptoms develop, or for Barrett's screening.
consults.reasoning: Primary care can manage typical GERD. GI referral for: refractory GERD, alarm symptoms, Barrett's screening, or if surgical intervention considered.

consults.name: Registered Dietitian
consults.result: Referral offered for weight loss counseling and dietary modification.
consults.reasoning: Dietitian can help with weight loss plan and identify dietary triggers.

disposition.name: Outpatient Management with Follow-up
disposition.result: Outpatient management. Follow-up in 8 weeks to assess PPI response. Earlier if symptoms worsen or alarm symptoms develop.
disposition.reasoning: GERD is managed outpatient. Reassess in 8 weeks. If good response, attempt to step down to lowest effective PPI dose or H2 blocker.

disposition.name: Alarm Symptom Education
disposition.result: Counseled to return immediately for: difficulty swallowing, food getting stuck, unintentional weight loss, vomiting blood, black stools.
disposition.reasoning: Patient must know alarm symptoms that require urgent evaluation and EGD.

actualDiagnosis.name: Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD)
keyFindings: Relief with antacids and prior PPI trial
No alarm symptoms (dysphagia, weight loss, bleeding)
Multiple modifiable risk factors: late meals, alcohol, weight
teachingPoints: GERD diagnosis is clinical - typical symptoms responding to acid suppression confirms diagnosis
Alarm symptoms (dysphagia, weight loss, anemia, vomiting) require endoscopy
Lifestyle modifications are first-line: weight loss (strongest evidence), avoiding late meals, head of bed elevation
PPI step-down approach: treat for 8 weeks, then attempt to reduce to lowest effective dose
Environmental factors matter: eating timing, meal size, trigger identification, sleep positioning

closureScript.diagnosis: Based on your symptoms - the burning after meals, worse at night, relieved by antacids - you have gastroesophageal reflux disease, commonly called GERD or acid reflux.
closureScript.plan: The good news is this is very treatable. I'm going to recommend a PPI medication like omeprazole for 8 weeks. But equally important are lifestyle changes: eating dinner earlier (at least 3 hours before bed), elevating the head of your bed, and working on weight loss - even 5-10 pounds can make a big difference.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Come back if you develop difficulty swallowing, unintentional weight loss, vomiting, or if you see blood in your stool. Also return if symptoms don't improve in 2-4 weeks.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: This is very common and manageable. The lifestyle changes are as important as the medication, and many people can eventually reduce or stop the medication once they make those changes.

male-32-sushi.name: Kevin Young
male-32-sushi.chiefComplaint: Terrible stomach pain that started hours after eating sushi
male-32-sushi.variantDescription: Acute gastric anisakiasis from raw fish consumption

missedConsequences.Anisakiasis: Untreated gastric anisakiasis causes ongoing severe pain. Can progress to bowel obstruction or perforation.
missedConsequences.Acute Pancreatitis: Missed pancreatitis leads to necrosis and multiorgan failure.

commonPitfalls: Not asking about raw fish consumption
Missing the characteristic delayed onset (hours after eating)
Not recognizing throat tingling during meal as larva migration
Treating as gastritis without considering parasitic cause
Not performing endoscopy for diagnosis and treatment

allDiagnoses.name: Anisakiasis (Fish Parasite)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Raw sushi + delayed severe epigastric pain + throat tingling during meal = gastric anisakiasis.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Pancreatitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Should check lipase; epigastric pain radiating to back.

allDiagnoses.name: Gastritis/Peptic Ulcer
allDiagnoses.rationale: Too acute and severe with clear food trigger.

allDiagnoses.name: Food Poisoning (Bacterial)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Bacterial food poisoning causes diarrhea, not isolated epigastric pain.

male-32-sushi.oldcartsNarrative: "I had sushi for lunch at a new omakase place - lots of raw fish including mackerel and squid. About 4 hours later, I started having terrible cramping pain in my upper stomach. It comes in waves. I've thrown up twice. The pain is the worst I've ever had. While I was eating, I felt something tickling my throat - I thought it was just the texture of the fish."

historyQuestions.text: Describe the pain
historyQuestions.answer: Intense cramping in my upper abdomen. Comes in waves. Worst pain I've ever had.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Severe crampy epigastric pain is characteristic

historyQuestions.text: Exactly when did it start?
historyQuestions.answer: Started about 4 hours after lunch. I had sushi at noon, pain started around 4 PM.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Delayed onset (1-12 hours) is characteristic

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the sushi
historyQuestions.answer: I tried a new omakase place. Had lots of raw fish - salmon, mackerel, squid. The mackerel tasted a little different.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Mackerel and squid are high-risk fish

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned throat tingling?
historyQuestions.answer: While I was eating, I felt something tickling my throat. I thought it was just the texture.
historyQuestions.reasoning: PATHOGNOMONIC - larva crawling in throat/esophagus

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Past medical history affects diagnosis and treatment.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

water_food_raw_foods.answer: I eat sushi at least twice a week. Love raw fish.
water_food_raw_foods.relevance: Frequent raw fish consumption increases cumulative risk of anisakiasis exposure.

water_food_food_sources.answer: New restaurant - omakase style. Chef's choice. Had several types of raw fish including mackerel and squid.
water_food_food_sources.relevance: Mackerel and squid are high-risk fish for Anisakis larvae.

physicalExam.finding: Uncomfortable, clutching abdomen, writhing in pain
physicalExam.reasoning: Severe discomfort

physicalExam.finding: BP 128/82, HR 98, RR 18, Temp 99.4°F, SpO2 99% on RA
physicalExam.reasoning: Mild tachycardia from pain

physicalExam.finding: Epigastric tenderness to palpation. No rebound or guarding. Hyperactive bowel sounds.
physicalExam.reasoning: Localized epigastric tenderness suggests gastric pathology

physicalExam.finding: Mild pharyngeal erythema. No visible parasites (sometimes can be seen).
physicalExam.reasoning: Occasionally larva visible in throat

labs.name: Lipase
labs.reasoning: Rules out pancreatitis - important differential for severe epigastric pain.

labs.name: CBC with differential
labs.result: WBC 9.8 with 8% eosinophils (mildly elevated), otherwise normal
labs.reasoning: Eosinophilia supports parasitic infection. May be normal early - eosinophilia develops over days.

labs.name: Basic Metabolic Panel
labs.reasoning: Baseline metabolic panel. Assess hydration status if vomiting.

labs.name: Anisakis-specific IgE/IgG
labs.result: Can confirm diagnosis but results not immediately available. Don't delay endoscopy.
labs.reasoning: Serologic testing available but rarely needed - diagnosis usually made endoscopically. May be useful for epidemiologic purposes.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast
imaging.result: Focal thickening of gastric wall in the antrum. Mild surrounding inflammation. No perforation. Normal pancreas. No free air.
imaging.reasoning: Shows focal gastric wall thickening and inflammation. Rules out perforation and other pathology. Findings suggestive but not diagnostic.

imaging.name: Upright Chest X-ray
imaging.result: No free air under diaphragm.
imaging.reasoning: Rules out perforation. Quick screening test if CT not immediately available.

procedures.name: Upper endoscopy (EGD) - DIAGNOSTIC AND THERAPEUTIC
procedures.result: 2cm white threadlike worm embedded in gastric mucosa, surrounded by erythema and edema. Removed intact with forceps. Specimen sent for confirmation: Anisakis simplex larva.
procedures.reasoning: GOLD STANDARD: Endoscopy is both diagnostic and therapeutic. Visual identification of embedded larva is pathognomonic. Removal provides immediate symptom relief.

procedures.name: IV Access
procedures.result: 18-gauge IV placed for fluids and medications.
procedures.reasoning: IV access for hydration, antiemetics, and sedation for endoscopy.

treatments.name: Endoscopic larva removal (DEFINITIVE)
treatments.result: Larva grasped with forceps and removed intact. Symptoms resolve almost immediately after removal.
treatments.reasoning: DEFINITIVE TREATMENT: Removal of the embedded larva provides rapid, complete symptom resolution. This is why urgent endoscopy is indicated.

treatments.name: Ondansetron 4mg IV
treatments.result: Ondansetron 4mg IV for nausea and vomiting.
treatments.reasoning: Control nausea while awaiting endoscopy. Improves patient comfort.

treatments.name: Pain management
treatments.result: Ketorolac 30mg IV for pain. Morphine/hydromorphone if NSAID insufficient.
treatments.reasoning: Pain can be severe. Provide adequate analgesia while arranging endoscopy. Pain resolves immediately once worm removed.

treatments.name: IV Fluid Resuscitation
treatments.result: NS 1L bolus for dehydration from vomiting.
treatments.reasoning: Replace fluid losses from vomiting. Prepare for procedural sedation.

treatments.name: Albendazole (if endoscopy not available)
treatments.result: NOT FIRST-LINE. Consider albendazole 400mg BID x 3-5 days only if endoscopy unavailable.
treatments.reasoning: Anthelmintics are second-line. Less effective than endoscopic removal and slower symptom relief. Reserve for cases where endoscopy is not feasible.

treatments.name: Corticosteroids (for allergic symptoms)
treatments.result: Not indicated - no allergic reaction.
treatments.reasoning: Steroids used only if significant allergic reaction (urticaria, angioedema) accompanies gastric symptoms. Anisakis can cause IgE-mediated allergy.

consults.name: GI consult for urgent EGD
consults.result: GI consulted. Urgent EGD performed within hours. Larva identified and removed. Patient's symptoms resolved immediately.
consults.reasoning: GI consult essential for therapeutic endoscopy. Should be performed urgently - delays prolong severe symptoms and risk of complications.

consults.name: Surgery (if complications)
consults.result: Not indicated - no perforation or obstruction.
consults.reasoning: Surgery rarely needed. Would be indicated for: intestinal perforation, bowel obstruction, or failed endoscopic removal.

disposition.name: Discharge after successful endoscopy
disposition.result: Discharged home after EGD with successful removal. Symptoms resolved. No further treatment needed.
disposition.reasoning: Once larva removed, no further treatment needed. Symptoms resolve immediately. Patient can be discharged same day.

disposition.name: Raw fish counseling
disposition.result: Counseled about anisakiasis risk with raw fish. Reputable restaurants should freeze fish to kill parasites (FDA requirement for sushi-grade fish). Risk is lower at established sushi restaurants.
disposition.reasoning: Patient education important. Not all raw fish is equally risky. FDA requires freezing for fish intended to be eaten raw. Higher risk: homemade ceviche, unregulated sources.

disposition.name: Return precautions
disposition.result: Return if: recurrent abdominal pain, fever, bloody stool, or symptoms don't fully resolve.
disposition.reasoning: Incomplete removal or intestinal migration (if larvae pass to small bowel) can cause ongoing symptoms. Perforation would cause peritonitis.

disposition.name: Follow-up plan
disposition.result: No routine follow-up needed if symptoms fully resolve. Return PRN.
disposition.reasoning: Successful endoscopic removal is curative. No follow-up needed unless symptoms recur.

actualDiagnosis.name: Gastric Anisakiasis
keyFindings: Raw sushi ingestion 4 hours before symptoms
teachingPoints: Anisakis is a parasitic worm in raw/undercooked fish
High-risk fish: mackerel, salmon, squid, herring, cod
Larvae penetrate GI mucosa causing intense pain
Gastric symptoms occur 1-12 hours after ingestion
Intestinal form occurs 5-7 days later if larvae pass to intestine
Throat tingling during meal = larva crawling (pathognomonic if noticed)
Endoscopic removal is treatment of choice
Freezing fish kills larvae (FDA requires for sushi-grade fish)

closureScript.diagnosis: You have a parasitic infection called anisakiasis from a worm that was in the raw fish you ate. The worm burrowed into the wall of your stomach, which is causing the severe pain.
closureScript.plan: The good news is we can remove the worm with an endoscopy - a camera we put down your throat. Once it's out, your pain should resolve quickly.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: After we remove it, you should feel better almost immediately. If the pain comes back or you develop fever, come back right away.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: You can still enjoy sushi - just be aware that raw fish carries some risk. Reputable restaurants should be freezing their fish properly to kill parasites. The tingling you felt in your throat was actually the worm - that's a sign to be aware of in the future.

guidelineReferences.name: CDC Anisakiasis Information
guidelineReferences.citation: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
keyPoints: Caused by Anisakis simplex larvae in raw fish
Symptoms: severe epigastric pain, nausea, vomiting
Diagnosis: endoscopy visualization of larvae
Treatment: endoscopic removal is curative
Prevention: freeze fish at -20°C for 7 days

guidelineReferences.name: FDA Fish and Fishery Products Hazards Guide
guidelineReferences.citation: Food and Drug Administration
keyPoints: Freezing requirements: -4°F for 7 days or -31°F for 15 hours
Applies to fish intended to be served raw
High-risk fish: salmon, mackerel, squid, herring
Cooking to 145°F kills parasites
Sushi-grade fish must meet freezing requirements

female-45-amatoxin.name: Sarah Kennedy
female-45-amatoxin.chiefComplaint: Terrible vomiting and diarrhea that started yesterday, and now my stomach really hurts
female-45-amatoxin.variantDescription: Amatoxin poisoning from misidentified Amanita phalloides (death cap)

missedConsequences.Amatoxin Mushroom Poisoning: Amatoxin causes delayed hepatotoxicity. By day 3-4, fulminant liver failure develops. Mortality 10-30% even with treatment. Liver transplant may be only option.

commonPitfalls: Dismissing as viral gastroenteritis without asking about mushroom ingestion
Being falsely reassured by the 'honeymoon period' improvement in symptoms
Not recognizing 6-12 hour delay from ingestion to symptoms as RED FLAG for amatoxin
Waiting for LFT elevation before treating (LFTs rise LATE)
Not contacting poison control and hepatology IMMEDIATELY

allDiagnoses.name: Amatoxin Poisoning (Amanita)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Foraged mushrooms + 6-12 hour delayed GI symptoms + delayed onset = classic amatoxin poisoning until proven otherwise.

allDiagnoses.name: Other Mushroom Poisoning
allDiagnoses.rationale: Other toxic mushrooms have different timelines and effects - species identification helps.

allDiagnoses.name: Bacterial Gastroenteritis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Timeline and severity, plus mushroom history, point to toxin.

allDiagnoses.name: Viral Gastroenteritis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Doesn't explain severity or mushroom exposure.

female-45-amatoxin.oldcartsNarrative: "I went foraging for mushrooms yesterday morning with my husband. I'm getting into wild foods. We found some beautiful mushrooms that looked like the ones in my guidebook. Made a nice sauté for dinner around 7 PM. About 6 hours later - like 1 AM - I woke up with horrible nausea and then started vomiting and having watery diarrhea non-stop. My husband ate them too and he's also sick, but not as bad as me. This morning I felt a little better but now the pain is back and worse."

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the mushrooms you ate
historyQuestions.answer: We found them growing under some oak trees in the park. White cap, white gills, a ring on the stem. Looked like the edible ones in my field guide. We ate maybe a cup each, sautéed.
historyQuestions.reasoning: DEATH CAP description: white cap/gills, ring (annulus), under oaks. Classic misidentification.

historyQuestions.text: Walk me through the exact timing of symptoms
historyQuestions.answer: Ate dinner at 7 PM. Felt fine until about 1 AM when I woke up nauseated. Then violent vomiting and diarrhea until about 8 AM. Actually felt a bit better mid-morning. But now at 2 PM the pain is back.
historyQuestions.reasoning: 6-HOUR DELAY is the KEY. Early-onset (<6h) = likely GI irritants. Delayed (6-24h) = AMATOXIN until proven otherwise

historyQuestions.text: You said you felt better this morning?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, the vomiting stopped and I thought maybe it was over. But now I feel worse again.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CLASSIC HONEYMOON PERIOD - patients feel better while liver is being destroyed. Deadly trap.

historyQuestions.text: How is your husband doing?
historyQuestions.answer: He's also sick but not as severe. He ate less than me. He stayed home today but I was worried enough to come in.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Both sick = confirms exposure. He needs to be evaluated too. Dose-dependent toxicity.

historyQuestions.text: Did you save any mushrooms or take photos?
historyQuestions.answer: I have photos on my phone! And there might be some left in the fridge.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL - mycologist identification confirms species. Save specimens!

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Past medical history affects diagnosis and treatment.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

hobbies_foraging.answer: I've been getting into foraging lately - mushrooms, berries, greens. Taught myself from books and YouTube.
hobbies_foraging.relevance: FORAGING without expert training is extremely dangerous. Amanita phalloides is responsible for 90% of mushroom fatalities worldwide.

food_consumption.answer: Ate the foraged mushrooms for dinner last night around 7 PM. Sautéed in butter with garlic.
food_consumption.relevance: Cooking does NOT destroy amatoxins. Amount consumed affects severity.

geographic_foraging_location.answer: Found them in a local park, growing under oak trees.
geographic_foraging_location.relevance: Amanita phalloides grows under oaks. Common in California, Pacific Northwest - expanding range with imported cork oaks.

physicalExam.reasoning: Hypotension and tachycardia from volume depletion. Will worsen with liver failure.

physicalExam.finding: Diffuse tenderness, particularly RUQ. No peritoneal signs. Hyperactive bowel sounds.
physicalExam.reasoning: RUQ tenderness may indicate early hepatotoxicity

physicalExam.finding: Dry mucous membranes. Decreased skin turgor. No jaundice yet.
physicalExam.reasoning: Dehydration from GI losses. Jaundice appears LATER (days 3-4)

physicalExam.finding: Alert but anxious. Oriented x3.
physicalExam.reasoning: Mental status changes indicate hepatic encephalopathy - late and ominous sign

labs.name: Liver function tests
labs.result: AST 85, ALT 92, Alk Phos 95, T. Bili 1.1 - mildly elevated (will rise dramatically over next 24-48h)
labs.reasoning: LFTs may be near-normal early. Serial LFTs ESSENTIAL - expect dramatic rise.

labs.name: Coagulation studies
labs.reasoning: INR is best early marker of liver synthetic function. Will rise.

labs.name: Basic metabolic panel
labs.reasoning: Hypoglycemia is sign of liver failure. AKI common. Replace K.

labs.name: Lactate
labs.reasoning: Elevated lactate indicates tissue hypoperfusion

labs.name: Ammonia level
labs.reasoning: Tracks hepatic encephalopathy risk

imaging.name: RUQ ultrasound
imaging.result: Normal liver echotexture. Patent hepatic vessels. No biliary dilation.
imaging.reasoning: Baseline liver imaging. Amatoxin doesn't cause structural changes initially.

procedures.name: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation
procedures.result: NS 2L bolus, then maintenance
procedures.reasoning: Volume repletion critical. May need large volumes.

consults.name: Poison Control Center
consults.result: Call 1-800-222-1222. Confirms suspected amatoxin. Recommends N-acetylcysteine protocol, silibinin if available.
consults.reasoning: CALL IMMEDIATELY. They coordinate treatment and can source silibinin.

consults.name: Hepatology/Transplant Surgery
consults.result: Evaluating for potential liver transplant listing. King's College criteria being monitored.
consults.reasoning: EARLY transplant eval is critical - may need liver within days

consults.name: Mycologist identification
consults.result: Photos and specimens sent. Preliminary ID: Amanita phalloides (death cap).
consults.reasoning: Species confirmation guides prognosis and treatment

disposition.name: ICU admission
disposition.result: Admitted to ICU for monitoring and treatment protocol
disposition.reasoning: Amatoxin poisoning requires ICU level care

actualDiagnosis.name: Amatoxin Poisoning (Amanita phalloides)
keyFindings: Foraged mushrooms under oak trees (death cap habitat)
6-hour delay between ingestion and symptom onset
Classic 'honeymoon period' improvement followed by worsening
teachingPoints: 6-HOUR DELAY is the critical clue - early onset GI (<6h) is usually benign, delayed (>6h) is AMATOXIN
Amanita phalloides (death cap) causes 90% of mushroom fatalities worldwide
The 'honeymoon period' (12-24h after GI phase) is DECEPTIVE - liver damage is ongoing
LFTs rise AFTER the clinical honeymoon - don't wait for lab confirmation to treat
Treatment: supportive care, N-acetylcysteine, silibinin (milk thistle extract), and possibly liver transplant
Cooking does NOT destroy amatoxins
Death caps are often mistaken for edible mushrooms - white, with ring and cup (volva)
ALWAYS call Poison Control and get mycologist identification

closureScript.diagnosis: I'm very concerned you've eaten a poisonous mushroom called Amanita phalloides - the 'death cap.' The 6-hour delay before your symptoms started and then feeling better this morning before getting worse - that's the pattern we see with this specific toxin. It attacks the liver.
closureScript.plan: We're admitting you to the ICU. We're starting treatments to protect your liver - a medication called N-acetylcysteine and we're trying to get another called silibinin. I've called poison control and the liver transplant team, because in severe cases people need a new liver. We also need to contact your husband - he needs to come in too.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: I have to be very direct with you. This is serious. The toxin from these mushrooms causes liver failure, and that can be fatal. Over the next 2-3 days, your liver tests are going to get worse even if you feel okay. We're going to monitor you very closely.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know this is terrifying. We're doing everything we can. Do you have the leftover mushrooms or photos? That would help us confirm what species you ate. And please - never eat foraged mushrooms unless identified by an expert mycologist. Books and YouTube aren't enough for safe identification.

guidelineReferences.name: AAPCC Guidelines for Mushroom Poisoning
guidelineReferences.citation: American Association of Poison Control Centers
keyPoints: Call Poison Control: 1-800-222-1222
GI onset >6 hours suggests amatoxin (high mortality)
GI onset <6 hours usually benign
N-acetylcysteine protocol for hepatoprotection
Early transplant evaluation critical

guidelineReferences.name: NAMA North American Mycological Association Toxicology Resources
guidelineReferences.citation: North American Mycological Association
keyPoints: Amanita phalloides causes 90% of mushroom deaths
Cooking does NOT destroy amatoxins
Silibinin (milk thistle) may be hepatoprotective
Preserve specimens for mycologist identification
Clinical honeymoon period is deceptive

ibd_flare.name: IBD Flare

doorNote.chiefComplaint: Bloody diarrhea getting worse over 2 weeks
doorNote.nurseNotes: Patient has history of ulcerative colitis diagnosed 2 years ago. Reports 6-8 bloody stools daily. Recently stopped taking his mesalamine because he felt fine.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your ulcerative colitis - when were you diagnosed and how has it been controlled?
historyQuestions.answer: Diagnosed 2 years ago. I was doing fine on mesalamine, having maybe 1-2 normal stools a day. But I felt so good I stopped taking it about a month ago.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medication non-adherence is a common trigger for IBD flares

historyQuestions.text: Describe the blood - is it mixed in the stool or just on the surface?
historyQuestions.answer: It's mixed in, and there's a lot of mucus too. The whole stool is bloody and mucousy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Blood mixed with mucus is classic for colonic inflammation

historyQuestions.text: How many bowel movements are you having?
historyQuestions.answer: 6-8 times a day, sometimes more at night. I'm getting up 2-3 times to go.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Nocturnal diarrhea suggests organic rather than functional disease; frequency indicates disease activity

historyQuestions.text: Do you have urgency or a feeling of incomplete evacuation?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes! I have to run to the bathroom and even after I go I feel like I still need to go.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Urgency and tenesmus indicate rectal inflammation

historyQuestions.text: Any abdominal pain?
historyQuestions.answer: Crampy pain in my left lower side, gets better after I have a bowel movement.
historyQuestions.reasoning: LLQ pain reflects left-sided colonic disease typical of UC

historyQuestions.text: Any joint pain, eye problems, or skin rashes?
historyQuestions.answer: Actually yes, my right knee has been achy and I noticed a tender red bump on my shin.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Extraintestinal manifestations (arthritis, erythema nodosum) indicate active IBD

historyQuestions.text: Have you lost any weight?
historyQuestions.answer: About 8 pounds in the last 2 weeks.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Weight loss indicates severity of flare

historyQuestions.text: Any recent travel or antibiotic use?
historyQuestions.answer: No travel. I did take amoxicillin for a sinus infection about 3 weeks ago.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Antibiotics can trigger C. diff superimposed on IBD - must rule out

historyQuestions.text: Have you taken any NSAIDs like ibuprofen?
historyQuestions.answer: I've been taking ibuprofen for my knee pain, maybe 4-6 tablets a day.
historyQuestions.reasoning: NSAIDs can trigger or worsen IBD flares

historyQuestions.text: Do you smoke?
historyQuestions.answer: I quit smoking about 6 months ago.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Smoking cessation can paradoxically trigger UC flares (protective in UC)

physicalExam.reasoning: Stable vitals but low-grade fever indicates active inflammation

physicalExam.reasoning: Pallor suggests anemia from chronic blood loss

physicalExam.reasoning: Erythema nodosum is extraintestinal manifestation confirming active IBD

physicalExam.finding: Right knee with small effusion, mild warmth, full ROM with discomfort.
physicalExam.reasoning: IBD-associated arthritis typically large joint, non-destructive

physicalExam.finding: No conjunctival injection, pupils normal, no photophobia.
physicalExam.reasoning: Checking for uveitis/episcleritis as extraintestinal manifestation

ibd_flare.teachingPoint: IBD flare workup: 1) Assess severity (Mayo score: stool frequency, bleeding, physician assessment, endoscopy), 2) Rule out infection (C. diff especially with recent antibiotics!), 3) Identify triggers (medication non-adherence, NSAIDs, smoking cessation in UC, infections), 4) Look for extraintestinal manifestations. Treatment escalation: 5-ASA → steroids → biologics.
ibd_flare.clinicalPearl: The triad of medication non-adherence + NSAID use + recent smoking cessation makes this flare very predictable. Always check for C. diff - it's common in IBD and changes management.

cdiff.name: C. difficile Colitis

doorNote.nurseNotes: Patient was discharged from hospital 10 days ago after hip replacement surgery. Received IV cefazolin perioperatively. Having 10+ watery stools daily with crampy abdominal pain.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your recent hospitalization.
historyQuestions.answer: I had hip replacement surgery 2 weeks ago. Was in the hospital for 4 days. Got IV antibiotics during and after surgery.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Recent hospitalization + antibiotics are major risk factors for C. diff

historyQuestions.text: Which antibiotics did you receive and for how long?
historyQuestions.answer: They gave me something through the IV - I think cef-something? For about 2 days.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Cephalosporins are high-risk antibiotics for C. diff

historyQuestions.text: Describe the diarrhea - watery, bloody, mucusy?
historyQuestions.answer: Very watery, almost like water. Has a terrible smell. No blood that I can see.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Foul-smelling watery diarrhea is classic for C. diff

historyQuestions.text: How many bowel movements per day?
historyQuestions.answer: At least 10-12. I can't control it sometimes. Had an accident yesterday.
historyQuestions.reasoning: High frequency and incontinence indicate severity

historyQuestions.text: Any abdominal pain?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, crampy pain all over my belly, especially the lower part.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Diffuse/lower abdominal cramping common in C. diff

historyQuestions.text: Have you had fevers?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I've felt feverish and had chills. My daughter took my temperature and it was 101.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Fever indicates systemic inflammatory response

historyQuestions.text: Have you been able to eat and drink?
historyQuestions.answer: I've been trying to drink but I have no appetite. Everything goes right through me.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Poor intake with high output = dehydration risk

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever had C. diff before?
historyQuestions.answer: Actually yes, about a year ago after I took antibiotics for a UTI.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior C. diff significantly increases recurrence risk

historyQuestions.text: Do you take any acid-reducing medications?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I take omeprazole every day for my reflux.
historyQuestions.reasoning: PPIs are a risk factor for C. diff

historyQuestions.text: Have you felt confused or not yourself?
historyQuestions.answer: My daughter says I've been a little confused, but I think I'm just tired from not sleeping.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Altered mental status suggests severe infection/sepsis

physicalExam.finding: BP 100/60, HR 102, Temp 101.4°F. Orthostatics: sitting to standing BP drops to 88/54 with HR 118.
physicalExam.reasoning: Significant orthostatic hypotension indicates severe dehydration

physicalExam.finding: Elderly female appearing ill, fatigued, dry mucous membranes. Oriented but slow to respond.
physicalExam.reasoning: Ill appearance with mild confusion concerning for severe infection

physicalExam.finding: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs. JVP flat.
physicalExam.reasoning: Flat JVP confirms volume depletion

physicalExam.finding: Distended, tympanitic, diffusely tender but no rebound or guarding. Decreased bowel sounds.
physicalExam.reasoning: Distension with decreased bowel sounds concerning - need to evaluate for toxic megacolon

physicalExam.finding: Watery, foul-smelling brown stool. No blood. Decreased rectal tone.
physicalExam.reasoning: Classic foul-smelling watery stool of C. diff

differentials.name: Ischemic Colitis
keyFeatures: Would expect more pain and bloody stools

differentials.name: Toxic Megacolon
keyFeatures: Need imaging to rule out given distension

cdiff.teachingPoint: C. diff severity criteria: WBC >15K, Cr >1.5, age >65, fever, hypotension, ileus, or altered mental status = SEVERE. This patient has multiple severity markers. Treatment: Severe C. diff → oral vancomycin (NOT metronidazole). Consider fidaxomicin for recurrent cases. Always stop inciting antibiotics if possible. CT if concerned for toxic megacolon.
cdiff.clinicalPearl: The '4 C's' of C. diff risk: Cephalosporins, Clindamycin, Carbapenems, and (fluoro)Quinolones. This patient has SEVERE C. diff (age, hypotension, confusion, distension) - needs vancomycin and close monitoring.

keyFindings: Recent antibiotic use (C. diff ruled out)
teachingPoints: Always ask about recent antibiotics - C. diff kills people
Assess hydration status: mucous membranes, skin turgor, orthostatics
Most acute diarrhea is viral and self-limited - supportive care only

Oral rehydration is preferred when patient can tolerate - WHO solution or sports drinks

female-28-acute.teachingPoint: This patient needs IV fluid resuscitation given positive orthostatic changes and moderate dehydration signs. Once rehydrated, can transition to oral. C. diff testing was appropriate given recent antibiotics. Most cases of acute diarrhea are viral and self-limited. Antibiotics are NOT routinely indicated and can prolong illness in some infections (EHEC - risk of HUS).
female-28-acute.clinicalPearl: The '3 D's' of diarrhea history: Duration (acute vs chronic), Dysentery (bloody or not), and Drugs (antibiotics = C. diff risk). For hydration assessment remember: thirst = mild, dry mucous membranes/decreased urine = moderate, orthostatic changes/lethargy = severe.
