historyQuestions.answer: About 3 months now. It started gradually and has been getting worse.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Chronic diarrhea (>4 weeks) requires workup for organic disease.

historyQuestions.text: How many bowel movements per day?
historyQuestions.answer: Usually 4-5 times, sometimes more. They're loose, not formed.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Frequency and consistency help characterize severity.

historyQuestions.text: Does it ever wake you up at night?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, at least a couple times a week I wake up and have to go.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL: Nocturnal symptoms strongly suggest organic disease over functional (IBS).

historyQuestions.text: Any blood in your stool?
historyQuestions.answer: Occasionally I see a little blood, maybe once a week. Mostly just mucus.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Blood indicates mucosal inflammation or malignancy.

historyQuestions.text: Have you lost weight?
historyQuestions.answer: Yeah, about 15 pounds in the last 3 months. I haven't been trying to.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Unintentional weight loss is an alarm symptom requiring workup.

historyQuestions.text: Where exactly is your abdominal pain?
historyQuestions.answer: Mostly in my right lower belly. Crampy, comes and goes, sometimes worse after eating.
historyQuestions.reasoning: RLQ pain suggests terminal ileum involvement - classic for Crohn's.

historyQuestions.text: Any family history of bowel disease?
historyQuestions.answer: My mom had some kind of colitis. She was on medications for it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: IBD has genetic component - 10-20% have affected family member.

historyQuestions.text: Any joint pain, skin problems, or eye issues?
historyQuestions.answer: Now that you mention it, my right knee has been achy lately.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Extraintestinal manifestations (arthritis, uveitis, skin lesions) occur in 25-40% of IBD.

historyQuestions.text: Do you smoke?
historyQuestions.answer: I quit about 5 years ago. Smoked for 15 years before that.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Interesting: smoking is protective for UC but INCREASES Crohn's risk. Ex-smokers at higher risk.

historyQuestions.text: Any medications including NSAIDs?
historyQuestions.answer: Just Imodium for the diarrhea and occasional ibuprofen for headaches.
historyQuestions.reasoning: NSAIDs can trigger or worsen IBD flares.

physicalExam.finding: BP 118/72, HR 78, Temp 99.2°F. BMI 22 (was 25 per patient).
physicalExam.reasoning: Low-grade fever suggests active inflammation.

physicalExam.finding: Thin, tired-appearing male. Appears older than stated age. Pale conjunctivae.
physicalExam.reasoning: Chronically ill appearance with signs of anemia.

physicalExam.finding: Soft, tender in RLQ with possible fullness/mass. No rebound. Normal bowel sounds.
physicalExam.reasoning: RLQ fullness suggests inflamed/thickened terminal ileum.

physicalExam.finding: Multiple skin tags. Small posterior fissure. No active fistula drainage.
physicalExam.reasoning: CLASSIC: Perianal disease highly suggestive of Crohn's disease.

physicalExam.finding: No erythema nodosum. No pyoderma gangrenosum.
physicalExam.reasoning: Checking for extraintestinal manifestations.

physicalExam.finding: Right knee with small effusion, mild warmth. No other joints involved.
physicalExam.reasoning: Peripheral arthritis is most common extraintestinal manifestation of IBD.

imaging.name: CT Enterography
imaging.result: Terminal ileum wall thickening (8mm) with mucosal enhancement. 'Comb sign' (prominent vasa recta). No abscess or fistula.
imaging.reasoning: CT enterography is test of choice for small bowel Crohn's evaluation.

imaging.name: MR Enterography
imaging.result: Would show similar findings without radiation
imaging.reasoning: Preferred for young patients needing serial imaging.

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Anemia of chronic disease + iron deficiency. Thrombocytosis suggests inflammation.

labs.name: CRP and ESR
labs.reasoning: Elevated inflammatory markers support active inflammatory disease.

labs.name: Fecal Calprotectin
labs.reasoning: Best non-invasive marker for intestinal inflammation. Helps distinguish IBD from IBS.

labs.name: Iron Studies
labs.reasoning: Iron deficiency from chronic GI blood loss and malabsorption.

labs.name: TTG-IgA with Total IgA
labs.reasoning: Rules out celiac disease.

labs.name: Albumin
labs.reasoning: Low albumin reflects protein-losing enteropathy and malnutrition.

procedures.name: Colonoscopy with biopsies
procedures.result: Skip lesions with cobblestoning in terminal ileum and cecum. Deep linear ulcers. Rectal sparing. Biopsies show non-caseating granulomas.
procedures.reasoning: DIAGNOSTIC: Colonoscopy with ileoscopy and biopsies confirms Crohn's disease.

actualDiagnosis.name: Crohn's Disease - Ileocolonic, Moderate Severity
teachingPoints: Chronic diarrhea (>4 weeks) with alarm symptoms requires colonoscopy
Perianal disease is nearly pathognomonic for Crohn's - always examine!
Crohn's can affect any part of GI tract; UC only affects colon
Fecal calprotectin excellent for distinguishing IBD from IBS
CT/MR enterography for small bowel evaluation (not reached by colonoscopy)
Treatment: steroids for induction, immunomodulators/biologics for maintenance

male-45-chronic.teachingPoint: This case illustrates classic Crohn's disease presentation. Key clerkship pearls: 1) Chronic diarrhea + weight loss = scope them, 2) Always examine the perianal area - Crohn's loves it, 3) Know the difference between Crohn's and UC (Crohn's: skip lesions, transmural, any GI location, granulomas, perianal disease; UC: continuous, mucosal only, rectum to colon, no granulomas), 4) Fecal calprotectin is your friend for organic vs functional.
male-45-chronic.clinicalPearl: On GI rotation, you'll hear about 'alarm symptoms' constantly: Unintentional weight loss >5%, GI bleeding, Nocturnal symptoms, Fever, Anemia, Family history of GI cancer/IBD. These mandate workup. IBS is a diagnosis of EXCLUSION.

patientBackground.recentIllnesses: No significant recent illnesses other than this presentation.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Dealing with typical life stressors. This illness is disrupting normal activities.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Has family and friends available for support. Not socially isolated.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Generally trusts medical advice. Wants to understand the reasoning behind recommendations.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Uses healthcare appropriately. No excessive ER utilization.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient recently took antibiotics?
whatIfScenarios.description: Diarrhea started 1 week after completing course of clindamycin

chiefComplaint.original: I've had diarrhea and stomach pain for 6 weeks
chiefComplaint.modified: I finished antibiotics for a dental infection 10 days ago. Now I have watery diarrhea 8 times a day with cramping.

feedback.correct: C. diff: recent antibiotics (especially clindamycin, fluoroquinolones, cephalosporins) + watery diarrhea. Test: PCR or toxin assay. Treatment: oral vancomycin or fidaxomicin (NOT metronidazole for most cases per 2021 guidelines). Stop offending antibiotic if possible.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of C. diff. What's the current first-line treatment?
feedback.incorrect: C. difficile: antibiotic exposure within 8 weeks, watery diarrhea, leukocytosis (sometimes very high >15k). Diagnosis: stool PCR or toxin. Treatment: oral vancomycin 125mg QID x 10 days or fidaxomicin. Metronidazole is now second-line only. Watch for severe (WBC >15k, Cr rise) or fulminant (hypotension, megacolon).

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: IBD = chronic, months of symptoms, systemic features. C. diff = acute after antibiotics. Recent antibiotic history is the key distinguishing feature.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient just returned from Mexico?
whatIfScenarios.description: Acute watery diarrhea starting 2 days into trip to Mexico

chiefComplaint.original: I've had diarrhea and stomach pain for 6 weeks
chiefComplaint.modified: I'm on vacation in Mexico. Started having watery diarrhea 2 days ago - going 6-8 times a day. Crampy pain.

keyDiscriminators: No blood or fever in typical case

feedback.correct: Traveler's diarrhea: usually ETEC (enterotoxigenic E. coli). Self-limited in 3-5 days. Treatment: hydration + loperamide for symptoms + azithromycin or fluoroquinolone shortens course. Avoid loperamide if febrile or bloody (invasive pathogen).
feedback.partial: Good recognition of traveler's diarrhea. When do you give antibiotics?
feedback.incorrect: Traveler's diarrhea: ETEC most common. Treatment: hydration primary. Antibiotics (azithromycin 1g x1 or cipro 500mg BID x1-3 days) shorten course. Loperamide helps symptoms but avoid if fever/bloody stool. If bloody/febrile = consider Shigella, Campylobacter, Salmonella.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Chronic diarrhea + weight loss = IBD workup. Acute diarrhea + travel = likely infectious, often self-limited. Duration and travel history guide approach.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there was fever and bloody diarrhea?
whatIfScenarios.description: Acute bloody diarrhea with fever, severe cramping

chiefComplaint.original: I've had diarrhea and stomach pain for 6 weeks
chiefComplaint.modified: Fever 102°F, severe abdominal cramping, and bloody diarrhea for 2 days. I ate at a picnic 3 days ago.

expectedReasoning.topDiagnosis: Invasive bacterial enteritis (Shigella, Campylobacter, Salmonella, EHEC)

feedback.correct: Bloody diarrhea + fever = invasive pathogen. Send stool culture and PCR. If undercooked beef exposure, consider EHEC (O157:H7) - DO NOT give antibiotics (increases HUS risk). Shigella/Campylobacter: azithromycin. Salmonella: antibiotics only if severe or immunocompromised.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of invasive pathogen. When do you avoid antibiotics?
feedback.incorrect: Bloody diarrhea + fever = bacterial dysentery. Pathogens: Shigella, Campylobacter, Salmonella, EHEC. CRITICAL: if EHEC (hamburger exposure, no fever), DO NOT give antibiotics - increases HUS risk. Otherwise: azithromycin for Shigella/Campylobacter. Watch for HUS (anemia, thrombocytopenia, AKI).

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Watery, non-bloody = usually viral or toxigenic. Bloody + febrile = invasive bacterial. Blood and fever change the differential and management.

guidelineReferences.name: Surviving Sepsis Campaign Guidelines 2021
guidelineReferences.citation: Evans L, et al. Crit Care Med. 2021;49(11):e1063-e1143
keyPoints: Septic shock: antimicrobials within 1 hour (Strong)
30 mL/kg crystalloid, but use caution in cirrhosis - increased bleeding risk
Consider albumin for patients receiving large volumes of crystalloids (Weak)
Target MAP ≥65 mmHg with norepinephrine
Avoid nephrotoxic agents; use either CRRT or intermittent RRT for AKI
VTE prophylaxis with LMWH over unfractionated heparin (Strong)

guidelineReferences.name: AASLD Practice Guidance: Ascites and SBP 2021
guidelineReferences.citation: Biggins SW, et al. Hepatology. 2021;74(3):1014-1048
keyPoints: Diagnostic paracentesis in all hospitalized cirrhotics with ascites
SBP diagnosis: ascitic fluid PMN ≥250 cells/mm³
Empiric cefotaxime 2g IV q8h or ceftriaxone 2g IV daily
IV albumin 1.5 g/kg day 1, then 1 g/kg day 3 reduces mortality

male-53-hcv-cirrhosis.name: Dennis Murphy

missedConsequences.Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis: Untreated SBP progresses to septic shock and hepatorenal syndrome. 1-year mortality post-SBP is 60-70% without treatment.
missedConsequences.Secondary Bacterial Peritonitis: If perforation is missed, patient develops overwhelming sepsis and dies without surgical intervention.
missedConsequences.Hepatorenal Syndrome: Delayed treatment leads to progressive renal failure that may become irreversible.

commonPitfalls: Not performing diagnostic paracentesis in any cirrhotic patient with fever
Waiting for culture results before starting antibiotics
Missing the elevated PMN count as the key diagnostic criterion
Forgetting IV albumin which reduces mortality by preventing hepatorenal syndrome
Not differentiating SBP from secondary peritonitis (which needs surgery)
Not starting SBP prophylaxis after recovery in eligible patients

mcqs.question: What is the most important initial diagnostic test in this patient?

options.text: CT abdomen to look for perforation
options.explanation: CT can wait. Paracentesis should be done first and immediately.

options.text: Diagnostic paracentesis with cell count and culture
options.explanation: Paracentesis is the gold standard for SBP diagnosis. Ascitic PMN ≥250/µL is diagnostic even before cultures return.
Blood cultures should be done but are only positive in ~50% of SBP. Paracentesis is the key test.
Ultrasound can confirm ascites but doesn't diagnose infection.

mcqs.question: What ascitic fluid PMN count is diagnostic for SBP?

options.explanation: This is too low a threshold.
Ascitic PMN ≥250 cells/µL is DIAGNOSTIC for SBP, even if cultures are negative. Treatment should start immediately.
While 500 would definitely be diagnostic, the threshold is 250.
Gram stain is often negative in SBP. PMN count is the key criterion.

mcqs.question: Why is IV albumin given in SBP treatment?

options.explanation: Albumin's role here isn't nutritional.

options.text: To prevent hepatorenal syndrome and reduce mortality
options.explanation: The Sort et al. NEJM study showed IV albumin (1.5 g/kg day 1, 1.0 g/kg day 3) reduced renal impairment from 33% to 10% and mortality from 29% to 10%.
Albumin doesn't affect antibiotic pharmacology.
Albumin is given for renal protection, not ascites management.

allDiagnoses.name: Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Cirrhotic patient with ascites, fever, abdominal pain, worsening encephalopathy - classic presentation. PMN ≥250 confirms diagnosis.

allDiagnoses.name: Secondary Bacterial Peritonitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Must differentiate from perforation. Clues for secondary: multiple organisms, glucose <50, LDH elevated, protein >1g/dL.

allDiagnoses.name: Hepatorenal Syndrome
allDiagnoses.rationale: Rising creatinine in setting of SBP suggests developing HRS. IV albumin is key to prevention.

allDiagnoses.name: Sepsis from Other Source
allDiagnoses.rationale: Consider UTI, pneumonia, cellulitis. But in cirrhotic with ascites, SBP is most likely.

allDiagnoses.name: Worsening Hepatic Encephalopathy
allDiagnoses.rationale: Mental status change could be from infection precipitating encephalopathy (which it is).

allDiagnoses.name: Alcoholic Hepatitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Patient has HCV cirrhosis and has been sober. Not acute alcoholic hepatitis.

allDiagnoses.name: Hepatocellular Carcinoma
allDiagnoses.rationale: Could present with decompensation, but acute fever/peritonitis picture points to SBP.

male-53-hcv-cirrhosis.oldcartsNarrative: [Called by nurse]: "Mr. Martinez in 508 is looking worse. He's been here for hepatic encephalopathy and was getting better, but now he's spiked a fever to 101.4 and says his belly hurts all over. He's more confused than he was yesterday. He's gained like 5 kilos this week and his belly is really tense."

historyQuestions.text: Where exactly does your belly hurt?
historyQuestions.answer: All over... everywhere hurts when you push on it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Diffuse tenderness is classic for peritonitis. Localized pain might suggest secondary cause.

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever had an infection in your belly fluid before?
historyQuestions.answer: No, never had that.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior SBP increases risk of recurrence. These patients should be on prophylaxis.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any belly taps recently?
historyQuestions.answer: Yeah, they drained some fluid a few months ago.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Recent large-volume paracentesis is a risk factor for SBP.

historyQuestions.text: Any vomiting blood or black stools?
historyQuestions.answer: No blood, no black stools.
historyQuestions.reasoning: GI bleed can precipitate SBP and suggests possible variceal source.

historyQuestions.text: Any burning with urination or change in urine?
historyQuestions.answer: No, peeing seems normal.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Rules out UTI as alternate infection source.

historyQuestions.text: How was his mental status yesterday?
historyQuestions.answer: [Nurse]: He was oriented to person, place, and time yesterday. Much better than admission.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Worsening encephalopathy suggests new precipitant - infection is common trigger.

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Past medical history affects diagnosis and treatment.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

physicalExam.finding: T 101.4°F, BP 98/62, HR 108, RR 22, SpO2 94% on 2L NC. Hypotensive and tachycardic = early sepsis.
physicalExam.reasoning: Fever + hypotension + tachycardia = sepsis. Needs aggressive management.

physicalExam.finding: Ill-appearing, uncomfortable, icteric, mildly confused. Asterixis present.
physicalExam.reasoning: Encephalopathy is worsening, likely from infection as precipitant.

physicalExam.finding: DISTENDED, tense, positive fluid wave. Diffuse tenderness especially RLQ and LLQ. Voluntary guarding present but NO rebound tenderness. Hypoactive bowel sounds.
physicalExam.reasoning: Diffuse tenderness with ascites = peritonitis until proven otherwise. Lack of rebound is reassuring against perforation but doesn't rule it out.

physicalExam.text: Look for stigmata of liver disease
physicalExam.finding: Spider angiomata on chest, palmar erythema, caput medusae, bilateral gynecomastia. Consistent with decompensated cirrhosis.
physicalExam.reasoning: Confirms chronic liver disease - SBP only occurs with portal hypertension.

physicalExam.finding: 2+ bilateral pitting edema to knees.
physicalExam.reasoning: Volume overload from cirrhosis and hypoalbuminemia.

physicalExam.finding: Decreased breath sounds at bases bilaterally. No crackles. Dullness to percussion at bases.
physicalExam.reasoning: Likely hepatic hydrothorax. No evidence of pneumonia.

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Leukocytosis supports infection. Thrombocytopenia from cirrhosis/hypersplenism.

labs.name: BMP
labs.reasoning: CREATININE RISING - early AKI, possibly developing hepatorenal syndrome.

labs.name: LFTs
labs.result: AST 89, ALT 52, Alk Phos 142, T.Bili 4.8, Albumin 2.4
labs.reasoning: Elevated bilirubin is poor prognostic sign in SBP.

labs.name: PT/INR
labs.reasoning: Coagulopathy from liver disease - not a contraindication to paracentesis.

labs.name: Lactate
labs.reasoning: Elevated lactate indicates tissue hypoperfusion - sepsis.

labs.name: Ammonia
labs.reasoning: Elevated ammonia contributing to encephalopathy.

labs.name: Blood Cultures x2
labs.reasoning: Positive in ~50% of SBP cases. Draw before antibiotics.

imaging.name: Abdominal X-ray
imaging.result: Large volume ascites. No free air under diaphragm. No bowel obstruction.
imaging.reasoning: Rules out perforation (no free air) - important for differentiating SBP from secondary peritonitis.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis
imaging.result: Large ascites. No free air. No abscess. Cirrhotic liver with portal hypertension. No bowel wall thickening.
imaging.reasoning: Consider if secondary peritonitis suspected. Not urgently needed if clinical picture is clear SBP.

procedures.name: Diagnostic Paracentesis
procedures.result: 4.5L cloudy fluid removed. Cell count: WBC 850, PMN 620 (>250 = DIAGNOSTIC FOR SBP). Albumin 0.6 g/dL. SAAG 1.8. Glucose 95 (normal). Gram stain: GNR seen. Culture pending.
procedures.reasoning: THE KEY TEST. PMN ≥250 is diagnostic. Normal glucose and single organism favor SBP over secondary peritonitis.

sbpDiagnosticCriteria.interpretation: PMN count 620 is DIAGNOSTIC for SBP. Normal glucose and single organism make secondary peritonitis unlikely.

actualDiagnosis.name: Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis with Early Sepsis and AKI
teachingPoints: PARACENTESIS IS MANDATORY: Any cirrhotic patient with fever, abdominal pain, encephalopathy, or decompensation needs diagnostic tap
PMN ≥250 = SBP: Don't wait for culture. Start antibiotics immediately based on cell count!
E. COLI & KLEBSIELLA: Most common organisms (~60%). Gram-positives in 25% (often nosocomial)
CEFTRIAXONE 2G IV DAILY: First-line treatment x 5 days. Alternative: Cefotaxime 2g IV q8h
IV ALBUMIN IS LIFE-SAVING: 1.5 g/kg day 1, 1.0 g/kg day 3. Reduces mortality from 29% to 10% (Sort et al. NEJM 1999)
WATCH FOR HRS: Rising creatinine in SBP = hepatorenal syndrome developing. Albumin helps prevent it
SBP PROPHYLAXIS: After recovery, start Norfloxacin 400mg daily if protein <1.5 g/dL or prior SBP
SECONDARY PERITONITIS CLUES: Multiple organisms, low glucose, high LDH, high protein = perforation, needs surgery
PROGNOSIS: 1-year survival post-SBP is only 30-40%. Consider liver transplant evaluation.

meld.name: MELD Score (Model for End-Stage Liver Disease)
meld.description: Predicts 3-month mortality in cirrhosis and prioritizes liver transplant allocation

thisPatient.clinicalAction: MELD ≥15: Consider liver transplant evaluation. MELD 21-30: ~20% 3-month mortality without transplant.

low.action: Monitor, may not need transplant urgently

childPugh.name: Child-Pugh Score
childPugh.description: Classifies severity of cirrhosis and predicts perioperative mortality

thisPatient.clinicalAction: Child C: 1-year survival ~45%. High surgical mortality (>80%). Transplant evaluation indicated.

childPugh.source: Pugh RN et al. Br J Surg. 1973;60(8):646-649

male-53-hcv-cirrhosis.teachingPoint: In ANY cirrhotic patient with ascites and fever (or encephalopathy, abdominal pain, or unexplained decompensation), perform diagnostic paracentesis IMMEDIATELY. Ascitic PMN ≥250/µL is diagnostic - don't wait for culture results to start treatment! Standard treatment: Ceftriaxone 2g IV daily + IV albumin (1.5 g/kg day 1, 1.0 g/kg day 3). The albumin is crucial - it reduces hepatorenal syndrome and mortality by ~20%. After recovery, start SBP prophylaxis with daily fluoroquinolone.

patientBackground.recentIllnesses: No significant recent illnesses other than this presentation.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Dealing with typical life stressors. This illness is disrupting normal activities.

patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Generally trusts medical advice. Wants to understand the reasoning behind recommendations.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Uses healthcare appropriately. No excessive ER utilization.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the ascitic fluid showed multiple organisms and low glucose?
whatIfScenarios.description: Ascites analysis concerning for secondary peritonitis (perforation)

chiefComplaint.original: My belly hurts and I feel terrible
chiefComplaint.modified: Same presentation - but ascites shows: PMN 5,000, glucose 20, LDH 400, multiple organisms on gram stain

feedback.correct: Secondary peritonitis = surgical emergency! Runyon's criteria: 2 of 3 (glucose <50, protein >1, LDH > serum) suggests perforation. Multiple organisms = polymicrobial = gut contents leaking. CT to find source, surgical consult STAT, broad-spectrum antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam), resuscitation.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of secondary peritonitis. What's the management difference?
feedback.incorrect: Secondary peritonitis: bowel perforation, abscess, or appendicitis. Unlike SBP which is medical, this needs SURGERY. Clues: multiple organisms, low glucose, high LDH/protein, very high PMN. CT abdomen to find source. Broad antibiotics (pip-tazo or carbapenem). Mortality very high without surgery.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: SBP = single organism, normal glucose, medical treatment. Secondary = multiple organisms, low glucose, SURGICAL emergency. Ascites analysis distinguishes them.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if creatinine rose to 4.0 despite albumin?
whatIfScenarios.description: Patient developing hepatorenal syndrome despite treatment

chiefComplaint.original: My belly hurts and I feel terrible
chiefComplaint.modified: SBP being treated with ceftriaxone and albumin. Day 3: creatinine rose from 1.8 to 4.0, urine output 200mL/day, urine Na <10.

feedback.correct: HRS-AKI: renal failure from splanchnic vasodilation in cirrhosis. Urine Na <10, no response to albumin challenge. Treatment: terlipressin + albumin (or norepinephrine + albumin in US). Stop diuretics, lactulose. ICU. Bridge to liver transplant - only cure is new liver.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of HRS. What's the treatment?
feedback.incorrect: Hepatorenal syndrome: cirrhotic kidney failure from severe splanchnic vasodilation. Diagnose: rule out other causes, no improvement with albumin challenge (1g/kg x 2 days). Treat: terlipressin + albumin (or norepinephrine + albumin). Midodrine + octreotide is less effective alternative. Liver transplant is definitive treatment.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: SBP + preserved kidney function = treat SBP, monitor creatinine. SBP + HRS = need vasoconstrictors, poor prognosis without transplant. Kidney function determines prognosis.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if SBP occurred during hospitalization?
whatIfScenarios.description: Patient was already in hospital when SBP developed

chiefComplaint.original: My belly hurts and I feel terrible
chiefComplaint.modified: Patient hospitalized for variceal bleed. Day 5: develops fever, abdominal pain. Paracentesis shows PMN 400, gram-positive cocci.

feedback.correct: Nosocomial SBP: different organisms! More gram-positives (enterococcus, staph), more resistant GNRs. Ceftriaxone may not be enough. Consider piperacillin-tazobactam or carbapenem + vancomycin. Check local resistance patterns. Higher mortality than community-acquired SBP.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of nosocomial pattern. How does coverage change?
feedback.incorrect: Nosocomial SBP (>48h in hospital): gram-positives more common (25-30%), resistant organisms possible. Broader coverage: pip-tazo or meropenem + vancomycin. Hospital antibiogram guides choices. Also consider recent antibiotics, procedures as risk factors. Higher mortality than community-acquired.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Community SBP = E. coli/Klebsiella, ceftriaxone sufficient. Nosocomial SBP = more gram-positives and resistant organisms, need broader coverage. Setting determines empiric antibiotics.

guidelineReferences.name: ACG Clinical Guideline: Upper GI and Ulcer Bleeding 2021
guidelineReferences.citation: Laine L, et al. Am J Gastroenterol. 2021;116:899-917
keyPoints: Glasgow-Blatchford Score 0-1 identifies very low-risk patients for outpatient management
Transfuse RBCs at hemoglobin threshold of 7 g/dL (8 g/dL for cardiovascular disease)
Erythromycin infusion before endoscopy improves visualization
Endoscopy within 24 hours for hospitalized patients
High-dose PPI after endoscopic hemostasis reduces rebleeding

male-66-nsaid-warfarin.name: Robert Jenkins
male-66-nsaid-warfarin.variantDescription: UGIB from peptic ulcer disease in anticoagulated patient

missedConsequences.Bleeding Peptic Ulcer: Without urgent endoscopy, continued bleeding leads to hemorrhagic shock, cardiac ischemia from anemia, and potentially death.
missedConsequences.Esophageal Varices: If this were varices and missed, patient could exsanguinate rapidly. Variceal bleeding has 20% mortality per episode.
missedConsequences.Perforated Ulcer: If perforation is missed, patient develops peritonitis and sepsis requiring emergent surgery.

commonPitfalls: Not checking orthostatic vitals to assess volume status
Underestimating severity based on initial hemoglobin (takes time to equilibrate)
Not reversing anticoagulation in active major bleeding
Delaying endoscopy while waiting for INR to normalize
Forgetting to test for H. pylori (reduces rebleeding by treating it)
Missing the NSAID use as the underlying cause

mcqs.question: The patient has epigastric pain that's worse 2 hours AFTER eating. What does this timing suggest?

options.explanation: Gastric ulcer pain typically occurs WITH or immediately after eating.
Duodenal ulcer pain classically occurs 2-3 hours after meals when gastric acid enters the duodenum without food buffer. Pain is often relieved by eating.
GERD pain is typically positional (worse lying down) and burning.
Biliary pain is colicky and associated with fatty foods, not timing after meals.

mcqs.question: The BUN/Cr ratio is 32. What does an elevated BUN/Cr ratio suggest in this context?

options.explanation: AKI typically shows proportional rises in both BUN and Cr.
While dehydration elevates the ratio, in this context there's a more specific explanation.

options.text: Upper GI bleeding (digested blood increases BUN)
options.explanation: BUN/Cr ratio >30 in a patient with suspected GI bleed strongly suggests UPPER source. Digested blood protein is absorbed and converted to urea.
Diet effect would be minor compared to the dramatic elevation from blood digestion.

mcqs.question: This patient's Glasgow-Blatchford Score is 15. What does this indicate?

options.text: Low risk - safe for outpatient management
options.explanation: Only a score of 0 may be safe for outpatient management.

options.text: Moderate risk - needs admission but not urgent EGD
options.explanation: Score of 15 is very high risk.

options.text: High risk - likely needs intervention (transfusion, endoscopy)
options.explanation: Glasgow-Blatchford score ≥6 indicates >50% risk of needing intervention. Score of 15 is very high risk - needs urgent resuscitation and endoscopy.

options.text: The score doesn't apply to patients on anticoagulation
options.explanation: The score still applies and this patient scores high.

allDiagnoses.name: Peptic Ulcer Disease (Duodenal)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Pain hours after eating, daily NSAID use, melena and hematemesis - classic for bleeding duodenal ulcer.

allDiagnoses.name: Peptic Ulcer Disease (Gastric)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Same risk factors, but gastric ulcer pain is typically WITH meals.

allDiagnoses.name: Esophageal Varices
allDiagnoses.rationale: Must consider in any UGIB. He has no known liver disease, but alcoholism can be occult.

allDiagnoses.name: Perforated Ulcer
allDiagnoses.rationale: With chronic NSAID use, perforation is possible. Would show peritoneal signs and free air.

allDiagnoses.name: Mallory-Weiss Tear
allDiagnoses.rationale: Usually after forceful vomiting/retching. Can cause hematemesis.

allDiagnoses.name: Gastritis/Erosions
allDiagnoses.rationale: NSAIDs cause erosive disease, but usually less dramatic bleeding.

allDiagnoses.name: Esophageal Cancer
allDiagnoses.rationale: Could present with bleeding, but would expect dysphagia, weight loss.

allDiagnoses.name: Dieulafoy Lesion

male-66-nsaid-warfarin.oldcartsNarrative: "I've been having this burning pain in my upper stomach for about 3 days. It's worse a couple hours after I eat. This morning I threw up and there was blood in it - bright red with some clots. My wife almost fainted when she saw it. I've also noticed my stools have been really dark, almost black and sticky, for the past two days. I've been feeling weak and lightheaded, especially when I stand up. I take ibuprofen every day for my back - have for years. My doctor has me on warfarin for a blood clot I had in my leg. I know I'm not supposed to take ibuprofen with it but it's the only thing that helps my pain."

historyQuestions.text: Describe the blood you vomited - was it bright red, dark, or coffee-ground?
historyQuestions.answer: It was bright red with some dark clots. Looked like a lot, maybe a cup full each time.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Bright red blood suggests active/recent bleeding. Coffee-ground suggests older blood exposed to gastric acid.

historyQuestions.text: Describe the dark stools - are they tarry and foul-smelling?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, black like tar and sticky. Smells awful.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Confirms melena - digested blood from upper GI source. Distinct from iron/bismuth-related darkening.

historyQuestions.text: How much ibuprofen do you take and for how long?
historyQuestions.answer: 600mg three times a day, almost every day for maybe 5-6 years. It's the only thing that helps my back.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CHRONIC DAILY NSAID USE is a major cause of PUD. Combined with warfarin = very high GI bleed risk.

historyQuestions.text: Are you taking your warfarin regularly? Any recent dose changes?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, 5mg every day. No changes. I had my INR checked last week and they said it was fine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Warfarin increases bleeding severity. INR needs to be checked now - may be supratherapeutic.

historyQuestions.text: How much alcohol do you drink?
historyQuestions.answer: Just social, maybe 2-3 beers a week. Nothing heavy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Rules out heavy alcohol use that would predispose to varices/liver disease.

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever had ulcers or GI bleeding before?
historyQuestions.answer: No, never had any stomach problems before this.
historyQuestions.reasoning: First presentation, but years of NSAIDs have caused cumulative damage.

historyQuestions.text: Did you pass out or almost pass out?
historyQuestions.answer: I got really lightheaded standing up this morning, had to grab the wall. Didn't fully pass out.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Near-syncope indicates significant volume loss - orthostatic hypotension.

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

physicalExam.finding: BP 102/68, HR 112, RR 20, Temp 98.4°F, SpO2 97% RA. Tachycardia and borderline hypotension = hypovolemia.
physicalExam.reasoning: Resting tachycardia and low-normal BP indicate significant volume loss.

physicalExam.finding: Supine: BP 102/68, HR 112. Standing: BP 84/58, HR 132. Patient feels dizzy. POSITIVE ORTHOSTATICS.
physicalExam.reasoning: Positive orthostatics (SBP drop ≥20, HR increase ≥20) indicate >15-20% volume loss. This is significant hemorrhage.

physicalExam.finding: Pale, diaphoretic, appears anxious but alert. In moderate distress.
physicalExam.reasoning: Pallor and diaphoresis indicate significant anemia and sympathetic response to hypovolemia.

physicalExam.finding: Conjunctival pallor bilaterally. Dry mucous membranes.
physicalExam.reasoning: Conjunctival pallor is a clinical sign of anemia. Dry membranes indicate dehydration.

physicalExam.finding: Soft, mild epigastric tenderness. No rebound, no guarding, no rigidity. Bowel sounds present. No hepatomegaly or splenomegaly.
physicalExam.reasoning: Epigastric tenderness supports PUD. NO peritoneal signs is reassuring against perforation.

physicalExam.finding: Black, tarry stool on gloved finger. Guaiac strongly positive.
physicalExam.reasoning: Confirms melena - key finding for upper GI bleeding.

physicalExam.text: Look for stigmata of liver disease
physicalExam.finding: No spider angiomata, no palmar erythema, no gynecomastia, no caput medusae. No jaundice.
physicalExam.reasoning: Absence of liver disease stigmata makes varices much less likely.

physicalExam.finding: Cool extremities. Capillary refill 3 seconds (delayed). No edema.
physicalExam.reasoning: Cool extremities and delayed cap refill indicate poor peripheral perfusion from hypovolemia.

labs.name: CBC
labs.result: Hgb 7.8 g/dL (↓↓), Hct 23%, MCV 82, WBC 9.2, Plt 185,000
labs.reasoning: HEMOGLOBIN 7.8 - severe anemia. Note: initial Hgb may not reflect true loss (takes 24-72h to equilibrate).

labs.name: BMP
labs.reasoning: BUN/Cr ratio = 32 (>30 suggests UGIB - digested blood protein absorbed as urea).

labs.name: PT/INR, PTT
labs.reasoning: INR 3.2 - SUPRATHERAPEUTIC! Warfarin needs reversal for active bleeding.

labs.name: Type and Crossmatch
labs.reasoning: Blood products will be needed. Type and screen minimum 2 units.

labs.name: Lactate
labs.reasoning: Elevated lactate indicates tissue hypoperfusion from hypovolemia.

labs.name: LFTs
labs.result: AST 28, ALT 32, Alk Phos 78, T.Bili 0.9, Albumin 3.8
labs.reasoning: Normal LFTs - no evidence of liver disease. Rules against variceal source.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: No free air under diaphragm. Lungs clear. Heart size normal.
imaging.reasoning: Rules out perforation (no free air). Important before endoscopy.

procedures.name: Nasogastric tube placement
procedures.result: Aspirate: 150cc of coffee-ground material with some bright red blood. Confirms upper GI source.
procedures.reasoning: Confirms UGIB. Not always done now if EGD is readily available, but can help localize.

procedures.name: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD)
procedures.result: FINDINGS: Duodenal bulb ulcer (1.5cm) with visible vessel (Forrest IIa). Epinephrine injection and bipolar cautery applied with successful hemostasis. No varices.
procedures.reasoning: DIAGNOSTIC AND THERAPEUTIC. Forrest IIa (visible vessel) = 43% rebleed risk without treatment. Dual therapy (injection + cautery) is standard.

glasgowBlatchford.interpretation: Score 15 = Very High Risk. >50% chance of needing intervention. Admission and urgent EGD mandatory.

actualDiagnosis.name: Bleeding Duodenal Ulcer (Forrest IIa) - NSAID-induced, anticoagulated
keyFindings: Duodenal ulcer with visible vessel on EGD
teachingPoints: UGIB CAUSES: PUD 60% (gastric 32%, duodenal 28%), varices 9%, Mallory-Weiss 6%
TIMING OF PAIN: Duodenal ulcer = pain 2-3h AFTER eating. Gastric ulcer = pain WITH eating.
BUN/CR RATIO >30: Suggests UPPER GI source (digested blood protein → urea)
GLASGOW-BLATCHFORD SCORE: Predicts need for intervention. Score 0 MAY be safe for outpatient. Score ≥6 = high risk.
FORREST CLASSIFICATION: Ia (spurting)=100% rebleed, Ib (oozing)=55%, IIa (visible vessel)=43%, IIb (clot)=22%, IIc (spot)=10%, III (clean)=5%
ENDOSCOPIC THERAPY: Dual therapy (epinephrine injection + thermal/clips) superior to monotherapy
IV PPI: 80mg bolus then 8mg/hr x 72h AFTER endoscopic therapy for high-risk lesions
NSAID + ANTICOAGULATION: Extremely high GI bleed risk. Need PPI prophylaxis if must continue.
H. PYLORI: Test ALL patients with bleeding ulcers. Eradication reduces rebleeding from 20% to 3%!
REVERSAL: Active major bleeding on warfarin → give Vitamin K + consider PCC/FFP. Don't delay EGD for INR normalization.

male-66-nsaid-warfarin.teachingPoint: This patient has a bleeding duodenal ulcer from chronic NSAID use, made worse by warfarin anticoagulation. Key management: (1) Resuscitate - 2 large bore IVs, fluids, blood if Hgb <7 or unstable; (2) Reverse warfarin - Vitamin K + consider PCC; (3) IV PPI - 80mg bolus; (4) Urgent EGD within 24h; (5) Endoscopic therapy if high-risk stigmata; (6) Test for H. pylori; (7) Stop NSAIDs permanently, discuss alternatives with pain management. Glasgow-Blatchford score helps risk stratify - score of 0 may be safe for outpatient management.
